column2: Where do you live currently? How long have you lived there? How long have you lived in Wyoming? Where were you born?
column3: What age will you be on Election Day, Nov. 3, 2026?
column4: Please tell the voters about yourself including your background and qualifications.
column5: What are the biggest challenges and opportunities facing Wyoming today? As a legislator, how would you address them?
column6: We asked WyoFile readers to rank issues that are important to them, and healthcare costs and access topped the list. What can the Legislature do to make healthcare more affordable and accessible to Wyomingites?
column7: Do you support efforts to sell or transfer public land in Wyoming? How do you plan to ensure that state and federal land is managed in the best interest of the people of Wyoming?
column8: No two Wyoming counties are the same when it comes to how they generate revenue. As a legislator, how would you approach the public's desire for property tax relief with local revenue needs?
column9: What role, if any, should the Wyoming Legislature play in economic development?
column10: Explain your values when it comes to campaign donations. Does Wyoming's system for monitoring campaign donations and expenditures need reform?

column2: I live at 2240 W. 43rd street, Casper WY  82604  I have lived in this home for 19 years.  I have lived in Wyoming for 59 years and was born here in Casper.
column4: I own my business Forefront Real Estate and have been a business owner for 10 years.  I have worked in various industries - oilfield, trucking, newspaper, and real estate.  I served on Casper City Council for 4 years and was Vice Mayor for a 1 1/2 years.  I am currently the Vice President of Wyoming Realtors, I served on Natrona County Historic Preservation Board, and I serve on Central Wyoming advocacy board for Central Wyoming Realtors.  I love our great state and want to preserve our values, support economic growth, and ensure future generations can enjoy the freedoms we cherish today.
column5: Wyoming's biggest challenge is protecting our way of life while preparing for the future.  We are facing rising property taxes, housing shortages, rural healthcare gaps, education concerns, workforce shortages, and an economy that still depends heavily on energy and mineral revenue.  I believe our biggest opportunity is growth done the Wyoming way.  I support responsible energy development, including nuclear, Ai data centers, traditional energy, renewables, and new industries that bring good jobs while respecting water, property rights, and local communities.  I would make Wyoming more business-friendly, reduce unnecessary red tape, and keep taxes predictable so companies want to invest here.
column6: Healthcare affordability and access are among the most important issues facing Wyoming families, especially in our rural communities.  No Wyoming resident should have to drive hours to see a specialist, wait months for care, or choose between paying for medication and paying their bills.  As a legislator. I would focus on practical solutions such as:  Strengthening rural healthcare, expand telehealth services, reduce healthcare costs through competition and transparency, support preventative care and wellness programs, and protect rural hospitals and clinics.  I believe healthcare decisions should remain as close to patients and their doctors as possible.  We need to remove barriers, encourage innovation, support local healthcare providers and ensure Wyoming families have access to affordable healthcare regardless of where they live.
column7: I believe public lands are on of Wyoming's greatest assets and should be managed in a way that benefits the people of Wyoming.  I do not support large scale sales or transfers of public lands if they would reduce public access, harm Wyoming's outdoor heritage, or place additional financial burdens on taxpayers.  I believe we should have a strong voice in how our federal lands within our borders are managed.  We live, work, ranch, farm, hunt, recreate, and we raise families here, and our voice should be considered in Washington D.C.
column8: Property tax relief is important because many Wyoming families, seniors, and small business owners are feeling the pressure of rising property values and rising tax bills.  People should not be taxed out of their homes simply because the market value of their property has increased.  The long-term solution is to continue to grow Wyoming's economy by attracting new businesses, supporting energy development, encourage innovation to create more jobs so we can broaden our tax base and reduce pressure on property owners over time.
column9: The Wyoming Legislature has an important role in economic development but is not government's job to pick winners and losers in the marketplace.  Our role should be to create an environment where businesses, entrepreneurs, and workers can succeed.  We should create the opportunities so our children and grandchildren can build their futures in Wyoming instead of feeling they have to leave the state to find them.
column10: My approach to campaign donations is simple:  transparency, integrity, and accountability.  Voters deserve to know who is supporting candidates and where campaign money is coming from.  Public trust in government depends on an open and transparent process.

column2: Cody, Wyoming - 5 years, Wyoming 11 years.
column4: I am an attorney, former Drug Court Judge, and community leader who has dedicated my career to public service, the rule of law, and strengthening Wyoming communities. I earned an MBA and law degree and have spent years helping individuals, families, businesses, and local governments navigate complex legal issues. I currently serve as President of the Cody Country Chamber of Commerce and have been recognized for professional excellence by various groups including being one of the Wyoming Chamber of Commerce's 40 Under 40. My background in law, business, and community service gives me the experience and practical perspective needed to represent House District 50 and fight for limited government, local control, and Wyoming values.
column5:
Wyoming faces three major challenges: rising costs for families and businesses, economic uncertainty tied to the energy industry, and a growing disconnect between government and local communities. At the same time, Wyoming has tremendous opportunities to strengthen its economy, develop new energy technologies, support small businesses, and attract families who value freedom and our way of life.

As a legislator, I would focus on making Wyoming more affordable, protecting Wyoming's energy industries while encouraging innovation, and returning more decision-making authority to local communities. I support responsible government spending, reducing unnecessary regulations, and preserving the constitutional rights and freedoms that make Wyoming unique. My goal is to keep Wyoming affordable, prosperous, and independent for future generations.
column6:
Healthcare in Wyoming is expensive for many of the same reasons it is expensive across the country: a lack of competition, excessive bureaucracy, and policies that drive up costs without improving care. The Legislature should focus on increasing access to providers, reducing unnecessary regulations, expanding telehealth options, and improving price transparency so patients can make informed decisions.

Wyoming should encourage market-based solutions that increase competition and lower costs while ensuring rural communities maintain access to essential healthcare services. I support efforts that give patients more control over their healthcare decisions and dollars, while avoiding one-size-fits-all approaches that increase costs or create greater dependence on government programs.
column7:
I do not support the sale or large-scale transfer of Wyoming’s public lands. Public lands are an important part of our state’s heritage, economy, and way of life. They support recreation, hunting, grazing, tourism, and multiple-use access that benefits Wyoming residents and visitors alike.

I do support stronger local input and better management of state and federal lands. Decisions affecting Wyoming should involve the people who live, work, and recreate here, not just distant bureaucracies. I will advocate for policies that promote responsible multiple-use management, protect public access, support responsible resource development, and ensure these lands continue to benefit future generations of Wyomingites.
column8:
Wyoming taxpayers deserve meaningful property tax relief, but we must also recognize that no two counties are the same. Counties that experienced dramatic increases in property valuations should not be treated the same as counties that saw little change. A one-size-fits-all reduction risks creating winners and losers across the state.

I support returning residential property valuations to 2019 levels and establishing a reasonable cap on future increases from that point forward. This approach would provide substantial relief in areas where valuations surged while allowing counties that were less affected to maintain a stable tax base and continue funding essential local services. It offers a balanced solution that restores predictability and affordability for homeowners while respecting local needs and preserving local control.
column9:
The Legislature has an important role in creating an environment where businesses, workers, and communities can succeed, but it should not try to pick winners and losers in the marketplace. The state's primary responsibility is to provide a stable regulatory environment, maintain essential infrastructure, support workforce development, and keep taxes competitive so businesses have the confidence to invest in Wyoming.

As President of the Cody Country Chamber of Commerce, I have seen firsthand how local businesses drive economic growth and strengthen communities. Economic development is most successful when it is driven from the ground up by entrepreneurs, local leaders, and private investment—not directed from Cheyenne. I support policies that reduce barriers to growth, encourage innovation, and allow Wyoming businesses and workers to thrive while preserving the values and quality of life that make our state unique.
column10:
Campaign contributions should be transparent so voters know who is supporting candidates and political causes. Wyoming's elections should be decided by Wyoming voters, not by outside organizations or special interests seeking to influence our state's future.

I am particularly concerned about the growing role of out-of-state money in Wyoming elections. Regardless of political ideology, Wyoming residents should be able to see who is funding campaigns and independent expenditures and make informed decisions accordingly. I support strong transparency and disclosure requirements so voters have access to timely and accurate information. While Wyoming's current system generally provides meaningful disclosure, I am open to reforms that increase transparency and help ensure Wyoming elections remain accountable to the people of Wyoming.

column2: I live in Casper, Wyoming. I've lived here my entire life. I've lived in Wyoming for 43 years. I was born and raised right here in Natrona County.
column4: I'm a lifelong Natrona County resident, born and raised in Casper, and a proud University of Wyoming graduate. I've spent my career in education here at home, and my commitment to this community extends well beyond the workday. I've given my time to organizations that look out for our most vulnerable neighbors — serving on the Juvenile Justice Board, the Natrona County Child Protection Team, and the Self Help Center — and I've spent nearly a decade as secretary of the Wyoming Soccer Association. That service is rooted in a simple belief: the people closest to a problem are best equipped to solve it. As your representative, my priorities are straightforward: strong local control so decisions about our schools and our community stay in Natrona County hands, and public safety for the families who call this place home. I'm running because I believe Wyoming's choices should be made by Wyoming voices, not outside influence.
column5: Wyoming's biggest challenge is keeping decisions in the hands of the people who live with the consequences. Too often, authority drifts away from local communities — like the recent school funding changes that cut roughly 8% from student activities and locked dollars into rigid categories, stripping local boards of the flexibility to spend where their kids actually need it. Families also feel real pressure from rising property taxes and an economy too dependent on a single revenue stream. But our opportunities are just as real: hardworking people, abundant resources, and communities where neighbors look out for one another. As a legislator, I'd restore genuine local control, keep families and first responders safe by backing the people who protect us, and make sure Wyoming's future is built by Wyoming voices — not outside money. The answer to nearly every challenge we face starts with trusting the people closest to the problem.
column6: Healthcare costs and access top voters' lists for good reason — too many Wyomingites drive hours for care, and rural communities are watching providers grow scarce. The Legislature can't fix everything, but it has real tools. First, we should tackle our provider shortage head-on: expand loan-repayment and training programs that bring doctors, nurses, and midwives to underserved areas and keep Wyoming-trained talent here at home. Second, we should strengthen the critical-access hospitals and clinics that small towns depend on, and remove the red tape that keeps qualified providers from practicing to the full extent of their training. Third, we should expand Telehealth and require real price transparency so families can see costs before they're stuck with a bill. We need to keep care close to home and decisions close to the people, rather than waiting for solutions from far away.
column7: No, I don't support selling off Wyoming's public lands. These lands belong to all of us: the hunters, anglers, ranchers, and families who've used them for generations. Once they're sold, they're gone — too often to the highest bidder from out of state, and the public loses access for good. That's not the Wyoming way. I do believe Wyoming deserves a far stronger voice in how the land within our borders is managed. Federal one-size-fits-all decisions rarely fit our state, and the people closest to the land — those who graze it, work it, hunt it, and recreate on it — understand it best. So, my approach is straightforward: keep public lands public and accessible, protect Wyoming's multiple-use traditions, and push for genuine local and state input in management decisions rather than handing our heritage over to distant interests. Wyoming's land should be managed by Wyoming voices, for Wyoming people.
column8: Wyoming homeowners deserve real relief — valuations climbed fast, and folks on fixed incomes got squeezed hard. I take that seriously. But here's what often gets lost: property tax is the most local source of revenue we have. It funds our schools, fire, EMS, and roads — and as your question notes, no two counties raise revenue the same way. When the Legislature hands down blanket cuts, it can quietly strip communities of the power to fund their own priorities — which is its own kind of lost local control. So, I favor targeted relief for the homeowners who need it most, especially seniors, over blunt, across-the-board cuts that leave counties scrambling or force us to backfill with volatile state revenue, such as an increase in the sales tax. And I'd give counties more flexibility to meet their own needs rather than dictating one formula for all 23. Relief and strong local services aren't opposites — but getting the balance right means trusting communities, not overriding them.
column9: The Legislature's role isn't to pick winners and losers — it's to build the foundation that lets Wyoming businesses and communities thrive: a skilled workforce, reliable infrastructure, and strong institutions. I support the Wyoming Business Council as a real partner to our communities, helping local leaders grow the industries that fit their region — because, as your earlier question noted, what works in one county won't work in another. And as a proud University of Wyoming graduate, I see UW as one of our greatest economic engines: training our workforce, driving research, and keeping young talent here at home. Above all, Wyoming has to diversify beyond the boom-and-bust of a single revenue stream. The Legislature should empower local communities to build on their own strengths and back the Wyoming institutions already doing this work — not chase one-size-fits-all schemes from outside. Steady investment in our own people is how we secure Wyoming's future.
column10: My values here are simple: a campaign should be funded by the community it seeks to serve. I'm proud that in my last run, the vast majority of my individual donations came from right here in Natrona County, and my organizational support comes from Wyoming-based groups. When a candidate's money comes from home, they answer to the people back home — not to distant interests writing checks from out of state. That's exactly why I believe Wyoming's disclosure system needs reform. Voters deserve to easily see who's funding the campaigns asking for their vote — including PAC money and dollars flowing in from outside Wyoming. Our reporting is too hard to search and too easy to obscure. I'd support clearer, more accessible, more timely disclosure so sunlight can do its job. Transparency isn't partisan — it's how voters hold all of us accountable. The people of Wyoming deserve to know whose interests a candidate truly represents.

column2: Live in Gillette, WY, current residence since 2010.  I moved to Gillette and lived there since June of 1993.  I was born in Bismarck, ND
column4:
Ken is the founder and president of Gillette Physical Therapy, P.C., serving the Gillette community since 1993. A Doctor of PT and advocate for healthcare reform, he has focused on improving patient care, advancing standards, and expanding access.
Since 2022, Ken has represented HD 32 in the WY Legislature, serving on the Education Committee and the Labor, Health, and Social Services Committee. He completed the Milbank Program for Emerging Leaders in Healthcare in 2025 and has participated in national policy efforts.
He has led key healthcare legislation, including reforms to credentialing, prior authorization, reimbursement, and accessibility. His work also includes criminal justice, energy, and mental health policy.
Ken previously served on the Wyoming State Board of Education, Campbell County School Board (2022 WY School Board Member of the Year), and Campbell County Healthcare Board. He brings clinical, leadership, and policy experience to public service.
column5: Wyoming’s biggest challenges are diversifying our economy, sustaining communities facing rising costs, and maintaining reliable public services in a rural state. At the same time, we have tremendous opportunities in energy innovation, healthcare delivery, and workforce development. As a legislator, I focus on practical solutions, such as supporting responsible energy production while encouraging emerging sectors, strengthening education and job training pipelines, and improving healthcare access. We must make smart investments that keep communities viable, attract and retain workers, and ensure Wyoming remains a great place to live, work, and raise a family.
column6: Healthcare affordability and access require reducing administrative burdens, increasing provider availability, and improving transparency. I’ve worked on reforms to prior authorization, credentialing, and reimbursement to lower costs and keep providers practicing in WY. We should continue expanding telehealth, support rural clinics, and streamline regulations that drive up costs without improving care. Encouraging competition and innovative care models will help patients get timely, affordable services. My focus is practical reform that keeps care local, reduces delays, and ensures Wyoming citizens receive high-quality care without unnecessary barriers. I am looking at bringing a program from Montana that saved @ 12% on healthcare premiums.
column7: Public lands are central to Wyoming’s economy, culture, and way of life. I do not support broad efforts to sell or transfer public lands out of public hands. Instead, we should advocate for balanced, responsible management that protects access for recreation, supports grazing and energy development, and preserves natural resources. The state should work collaboratively with federal partners, local stakeholders, and industry to ensure decisions reflect Wyoming values. Maintaining public access, economic use, and long-term stewardship must remain the priority for the benefit of current and future generations.
column8: Property tax relief is important, but it must be balanced carefully with the need to fund essential local services like schools, emergency response, and infrastructure. A one-size-fits-all solution doesn’t work in Wyoming. I support targeted relief that prioritizes homeowners most impacted, while maintaining flexibility for local communities to meet their needs. We should also examine alternative revenue options and efficiencies to reduce reliance on property taxes over time. Thoughtful, balanced policy ensures tax relief without undermining the services Wyoming communities depend on. I have worked on and co-sponsored long-term homeowners' relief, the 4% cap, and have offered other solutions that were community/county specific.
column9: The Legislature plays an important role in creating the conditions for economic growth, not picking winners and losers. Our job is to ensure a stable regulatory environment, invest in workforce development, and support infrastructure that enables businesses to succeed. By fostering innovation, supporting small businesses, and leveraging Wyoming’s strengths, such as energy, natural resources, and rural entrepreneurship, we can build a more resilient economy. Strategic, responsible policy helps attract investment while preserving the values and quality of life that make Wyoming unique. We need to make Wyoming the most business-friendly state, I have worked each year to lower our Workman's Compensation insurance rates and decrease regulations.
column10:
Transparency and integrity must guide campaign finance. I support full disclosure so voters understand who supports candidates and why. Wyoming’s system works well in many respects, but there is room to improve clarity, accessibility of reporting, and public understanding. Any reforms should strengthen transparency while encouraging, not discouraging, civic participation.
I am committed to ethical fundraising and to making decisions based on sound policy and the needs of my constituents—not contributions. I was disappointed by the recent “Checkgate” controversy, which was inappropriate and diverted valuable legislative time from addressing the real issues facing Wyoming.

column2: I was born in Riverton, WY and grew up on the ranch in Northwestern Natrona County.  I still reside there today.
column4: I am a third generation rancher in Natrona County and I started an oilfield construction business in 2001.  I have served on numerous boards and commissions over the years.  I served on the Natrona Co Fire Protection District for 14 years as well as a Natrona Co Commissioner for 16 years.  Over the years I have gained valuable knowledge about business and the private sector as well as the knowledge of how government works and the Legislative process.  Having lobbied for the WY Stock Growers Assn, the WY County Commissioners Assn and the WY Business Alliance, this has given be the background and knowledge of how to get things done.
column5: The biggest challenges include jobs, the economy, affordable housing, healthcare and funding for local governments. For the opportunities, there are a number of businesses that are interested in moving to our state. We need to continue to improve on our legacy industries so they continue to thrive.  We need to welcome new industries and business technologies into the state.  To attract them, we need to reduce some of the regulations so they will be able to produce the jobs that will keep our kids here and let our economy grow.  As far as housing, the Legislature and counties need to make it easier for housing development across the state.
column6: Healthcare is a very complex issue.  We need to try and attract more insurance companies to come to the state.  Some of the problems in healthcare are the lack of nurses and doctors practicing in the state.  By funding those nursing programs in our local schools it will help keep kids in the state and increase the number of health care providers.
column7: We shouldn’t sell the public lands however implementing trades in certain areas makes sense.  Over the year, Wyoming has been a good steward of the state lands.  Wyoming should have more of a say in management of federal lands.  We can manage them better locally.
column8: Property tax relief comes at a cost to education, local governments and special districts.  At a state level, we need to figure out a way to back fill the cities and counties so they may continue to provide services to the public.  Reducing taxes is great in theory but it comes at a cost to all the services we are accustomed to and enjoy;  Law enforcement, fire protection, roads, parks.
column9: The Legislature should continue to fund economic development programs like those of the WY Business Council and work together with the local county development programs throughout the state. Ie Cheyenne LEADS, Advance Casper and others.
column10: I have no problem in receiving money from people, groups and Pacs that I am aligned with.  I believe, as candidates, we should list our donations and expenditures.  I don’t believe there is any need for reform.

column2: Big horn county, Lovell. Lived here since 1999. Born in Montana
column4: Homeschool dropout who has a heart to do right. Worked in construction since I was 12, and helped my dad provide for his family. What makes anyone qualified? An interest in what is good, true and virtuous is what I can call upon for my qualifications.
column5: The dissolution of truth, or the deviation from the standard of reality is a great challenge facing our country at large. We have the opportunity to raise the standard of truth to the nation, and innovate independence from the unstable markets that control our economics. Allowing free market enterprise within the state is important.
column6: Stop subsidizing it. Artificial or Government interference has a tendency to unbalance the system
column7: I would support the sale of public land that is of no benefit to the public, to individuals who can use it productively (homesites, businesses, schools, churches etc.) and not corporations or large entities intent on making a profit. I would support the state taking over management of federal lands in Wyoming, and to ensure our public lands are managed we need statute to do so. It seems important to preserve places from certain development for the enjoyment of all, and for posterity.
column8: Spend less. Reevaluate what we actually need. I support homestead exemption from all property taxes. We should not be renting our homes from the government. Counties have means to raise funds without appropriation from the state.
column9: Very little. No subsidies, just uphold justice and make sure everyone keeps their promises. Make commerce uniform and consistent.
column10: I have little to no knowledge regarding this topic, therefore I shall forbear speaking upon it, except in this wise. Don’t cheat, don’t steal, don’t take a bribe. Be moral and virtuous.

column2: I live in Bar Nunn, Wyoming. I have lived here or just outside of town for the majority of my life, minus a few years I lived in Casper. I was born in California but raised in Wyoming, as I have lived in Wyoming since I was 2 years old. That marks 20 years living in Wyoming
column3: I will be 22 years old on Election Day.
column4: I am a student and lifelong Wyomingite. I have a BA degree in Political Science from UW and 3 AA degrees in Secondary Education, Political Science, and History from Casper College. My teaching and academic experience includes being a writing tutor, teaching via community presentations, 260 hours of teacher practicum/residency teaching experience, attending conferences on pedagogy, co-running an academic journal, engaging in extensive research, and more. My political experience includes bylaws writing, committee work, recruitment efforts, party strategic planning, running two of my own campaigns, serving as an officer of a county political party, running official social media, managing websites, collecting data, interning for a U.S. Senator, co-planning a national political conference, serving on Casper College Student Senate, presenting at the Midwest Political Science Association Conference, being an ACFR Young Leader, coordinating extensive political activities, and more.
column5: There are many issues impacting all Wyomingites: a lack of educational and job opportunities, young people leaving the state, issues of affordability, a sense of hopelessness, a lack of progress, and increasing division. These challenges, combined with cuts to essential services, uncertainties around emerging technologies, issues of equality and access, and more, require pro-active approaches. I would address these challenges by putting Wyoming First and fighting to move Wyoming Forward. We need a Wyoming vision of progress, principles, and passion in politics. I believe in listening to the community, fighting for people-centered solutions, accountability, and looking out for those among us who are struggling. I recognize that issues are rarely simply black and white, and understanding nuance is required to make real change last. I’m committed to working on real, lasting solutions that move our state forward, not throwing out buzzwords and empty promises to see what sticks.
column6: The Legislature can halt and reverse its efforts to implement unnecessary funding cuts that impact essential services. The Legislature can explore new ways of raising funds to use for funding essential services. For example one idea is, instead of cutting property taxes for those who are vastly wealthy and able to afford it, the Legislature could tax the purchase of new homes worth many millions, tax the building of multi-million dollar data center and nuclear projects, and use the money from that to fund healthcare programs, rural hospitals, ambulance services, and keep hospitals afloat. Additionally, right now, many people fall through the cracks of coverage. Without dependents or the ability to secure a good job in an historically difficult job market, many age out of coverage and don’t make enough to qualify for good ACA plans. The Legislature could finally join the rest of the nation and expand Medicaid so that more low-income childless adults can be eligible for coverage.
column7: Public lands belong in public hands. End of story. The only trustworthy steward of public lands is the public. Selling or exchanging public lands to private entities will limit public accessibility, damage the environment, and erode the land's quality. That is a net-negative for all Wyomingites. The public should be able to use public land for recreation, hunting, fishing, and other outdoor activities. For-profit industries will not treat public land the same. I cannot identify a justifiable reason for selling mass swaths of public lands. The Legislature should reject any effort to sell or transfer swaths of public lands, especially when there is no good or defensible reason for doing so. I plan to support efforts to protect public lands and demand accountability for how those lands are managed. Science, facts, and the people should lead the discussion around public lands, not corporations and profit.
column8: It's true that many people are being crushed by the weight of property taxes. The answer, though, is not to slash taxes without considering the after effects. Further, it most certainly is not to give a tax break to those who have more than enough money to pay a fair share. As such, the Legislature should: prioritize only cutting property taxes for those of lower income levels; explore ways to raise tax revenue through corporations or other earners who are in very high income levels; and reject any effort to broadly reduce property taxes unless a secure, alternative funding model for important programs can be implemented in its place. The state has to cover all bases - backfilling cost if necessary. In Natrona County, the education budget is especially impacted by property tax relief. A balance between relief and funding is possible. Blanket cuts without thinking ahead do not create balance, they create destruction.
column9: The Legislature has an important role to play. One of these is to support state agencies designed to promote economic development, such as the Wyoming Business Council. Instead of threatening to defund the Council, the Legislature should support its mission and find other ways to promote economic growth and diversity. For example, the Legislature could support an all-of-the-above energy approach to diversify Wyoming’s energy economy and ensure more stable funding for essential services. The Legislature has the opportunity right now to take advantage of tech and energy companies’ desires to do business in the state. The Legislature should support regulations to protect communities, let communities choose if they want facilities in their area, and where facilities are built create tax and regulation models that responsibly bolster Wyoming’s funding and economic landscape. The state should promote competition, ensure new business actually benefits the state, and never write blank checks.
column10: My values regarding campaign donations are simple: The people, not donors, come first – always. A legislator’s job is not to serve donors or be beholden to money, and they should never accept donations in the active performance of their jobs. I think our entire campaign finance system as a country needs reform. Money plays far too large a role in politics. Wyoming has historically been insulated from the money-problem in politics, but that is changing. That is why I support efforts like The Montana Plan (montanaplan.org), which uses Montana’s authority to define what powers corporations get and stops giving them the power to spend in state politics. I would like to explore how Wyoming can do something similar to limit the influence of corporate donations on politicians, making whatever changes necessary to stop corporate and dark money. Transparency is key, but limiting the influence of money is foundational.

column2:
641 State Highway 24  Devils Tower, WY
Lived in this area since 1974
Born-Belle Fourche, SD
column4: My qualifications come from a lifetime of experience in agriculture, business, conservation, and public service. Most importantly, I understand the people and communities of northeast Wyoming because I have lived and worked alongside them my entire life. I am committed to continuing to be a steady conservative voice who puts Wyoming values first and delivers results for the citizens of District 1.
column5:
Wyoming's biggest challenge is preserving our quality of life while creating opportunities for future generations. We must grow and diversify our economy without abandoning the industries that built our state—agriculture, energy, mining, and tourism. Rising property taxes, workforce shortages, healthcare access, and federal overreach remain significant concerns.

Our greatest opportunity is Wyoming's people. With abundant natural resources, strong communities, and a business-friendly environment, we can attract investment and create good-paying jobs. We should support responsible energy development, expand value-added agriculture, reduce unnecessary regulations, and invest in workforce training.

As a legislator, I focus on results over rhetoric. I support protecting private property rights, strengthening Wyoming's savings, promoting economic growth, and preserving our agricultural heritage. My goal is to ensure Wyoming remains a place where families and businesses can thrive.
column6:
Affordable, accessible healthcare is essential to Wyoming families, especially in rural communities. While the Legislature cannot solve every healthcare challenge, we can remove barriers, increase competition, and support local providers.

I support strengthening rural hospitals and clinics, expanding telehealth, and addressing workforce shortages by recruiting and retaining healthcare professionals. We should also reduce unnecessary regulations that increase costs and limit access to care.

Wyoming should pursue market-based solutions that give patients more choices, greater price transparency, and improved access to care. Mental health services and preventive care are also important priorities.

As a legislator, I have supported efforts to improve healthcare access while maintaining fiscal responsibility. My goal is to ensure Wyoming residents can obtain quality healthcare close to home at a cost they can afford while preserving vital local providers.
column7:
Wyoming's public lands are essential to our economy, outdoor heritage, and way of life. I do not support wholesale sales or transfers of federal public lands that would reduce public access, threaten multiple-use management, or burden Wyoming taxpayers.

I support giving Wyoming a stronger voice in federal land management decisions. Too often, policies are developed in Washington without sufficient input from the people most affected. Local governments, landowners, permittees, sportsmen, tribes, and state agencies should have meaningful involvement in decisions affecting our communities.

I support active management of public lands, including responsible grazing, energy development, recreation, wildlife habitat improvement, and wildfire mitigation. Healthy forests and rangelands require stewardship, not neglect.

My goal is to keep Wyoming's public lands accessible, productive, and sustainable while protecting jobs, supporting local communities, and preserving opport
column8:
I support targeted property tax relief, particularly for long-term homeowners, seniors, veterans, and Wyoming residents on fixed incomes who are being squeezed by rising property valuations. At the same time, we must ensure local governments can maintain roads, public safety, emergency services, and other essential functions.

The Legislature should provide local flexibility and avoid shifting costs to future generations. We should also continue growing Wyoming's economy and broadening the tax base through responsible business development, energy production, tourism, and value-added agriculture.

My approach is to find practical solutions that provide meaningful tax relief while preserving essential local services. As Vice Chairman of the Senate Appropriations Committee, I have worked to strengthen Wyoming's savings and maintain fiscal stability, and I believe property tax policy should reflect that same balance.
column9:
The Legislature has an important role in economic development, but government does not create prosperity—people, businesses, and entrepreneurs do. Our job is to create an environment where private investment can succeed through low taxes, reasonable regulations, reliable infrastructure, and a skilled workforce.

Wyoming's economy is built on agriculture, energy, mining, and tourism. We should support those industries while encouraging opportunities in manufacturing and value-added agriculture. Economic development should focus on creating jobs, expanding opportunities, and broadening the tax base.

I have consistently supported policies that make Wyoming more competitive, including regulatory reform, workforce development, infrastructure investment, and responsible resource development. The Legislature's role is to remove barriers to success and provide a stable business climate so Wyoming businesses, communities, and families can prosper.
column10:
I believe campaign contributions are a form of free speech and an important way for citizens to participate in the political process. Voters deserve to know who is supporting candidates and how campaign funds are spent.

I have always supported transparency and timely disclosure of campaign contributions and expenditures. Wyoming's system generally works well because campaign reports are publicly available.

I am open to reforms that improve transparency and make campaign finance information easier to access and understand. One principle is non-negotiable: there should never be political contributions solicited, accepted, or exchanged in leadership offices, legislative chambers, or the halls surrounding those chambers.

My record demonstrates that I make decisions based on what is best for Wyoming and the people of Senate District 1, not campaign contributions. Public service requires independence, integrity, accountability, and public trust.

column2: I was born and raised in Casper. I have lived here my entire life, and now I have the great privilege of raising my wonderful children here.
column3: 37. My birthday is a month before the election.
column4:
I am a Wyoming native. I graduated from Natrona County High School, and I hold a bachelor's degree from the University of Wyoming in English literature. I also have a master's in education from St. Catherine's University in St. Paul, MN. My husband is a USMC veteran and is one of the few people who served in both Iraq and Afghanistan. Now, we are raising our children here in Casper.  
I work for the WyoUnited Education Project, and I volunteer with the Casper Unity and Solidarity Project, doing mutual aid work, and I volunteer with Meals on Wheels. I am also a former Woods Learning Center PTC president. My experiences volunteering have played a large part in my decision to run for office. I also serve as board secretary for the WyoUnited (c)(4) and on the Natrona County Historic Preservation Commission.
column5:
I think the biggest challenge facing Wyoming today is the brain drain. With young people leaving in droves, Wyoming will struggle as more people retire. We have to find and build opportunities for young people to stay. 

I was especially offended when the legislature recommended cutting significant funding from UW. This is a great asset to Wyoming as a whole, and I think it is important that we harness that. 

The biggest opportunity, in my opinion, is that because we are such a small state, we have the opportunity to pioneer our way forward in policy that could strengthen our communities and make equality a reality. We have the opportunity to build something better and set the example for other states.
column6:
We need to strengthen Wyoming's healthcare workforce by investing in programs that recruit and retain doctors, nurses, mental health professionals, and other providers, especially in rural communities. No one should have to wait months for an appointment or drive hundreds of miles to see a specialist. 

We should also explore and support policies that lower prescription drug costs, expand access to preventive care, and increase access to mental health services. Investing in prevention and early treatment saves money and improves outcomes.

Finally, we need practical solutions, not political talking points. Legislators need to listen to patients, providers, hospitals, and local communities to identify what is working and what is not. The experience of experts should guide healthcare decisions and what helps Wyoming families stay healthy and financially secure.
column7:
No, I do not support efforts to sell or transfer public land in Wyoming. People come from all over the world to visit Wyoming. Our wide-open spaces and public lands are a big part of that. Tourism is the second-largest industry in Wyoming. However, our public lands are worth more than that. As Wyomingites, we know that the value that our public lands add to our lives cannot be tallied in dollars. 

We have a responsibility to leave Wyoming's land, water, and wildlife better than we found them. I support policies that protect watersheds, reduce wildfire risk, restore wildlife habitat, strengthen reclamation requirements for development projects, and keep public lands in public hands.

I plan to listen to experts and to the people in the communities. Listening and working together is how we do what is in the best interest of Wyoming.
column8: Wyoming families deserve tax policies that are fair and predictable, but we also deserve well-funded schools, safe roads, effective emergency services, and strong local communities. My approach would be to balance tax relief with fiscal responsibility and local control, ensuring that we don't solve one problem by creating another.
column9:
Wyoming needs to invest in the foundations of a strong economy: quality public schools, workforce training, reliable infrastructure, affordable healthcare, and vibrant communities where people want to live and raise families. We also need to support entrepreneurs and small businesses, which are the backbone of many Wyoming communities.

At the same time, we must continue working to diversify our economy. Wyoming has long benefited from the energy industry, and that industry will remain important, but we cannot rely on any single sector to carry our state forward. 

The Wyoming legislature should work to create good-paying jobs, keep young people in Wyoming, and ensure our communities can thrive for generations to come.
column10:
I believe voters deserve to know who is funding political campaigns and how that money is being spent. Transparency builds trust, and trust is essential to a healthy democracy.

Discussion of reform should focus on increasing transparency and public confidence while ensuring ordinary Wyomingites can participate in the political process. The goal should be a system that is open, accountable, and trusted by the public.

At the end of the day, I believe elected officials should earn votes through their ideas, their integrity, and their service to their communities—not through the size of their campaign accounts.

column2:
Where do you live currently? - Casper Wyoming
How long have you lived there? - since 1978
How long have you lived in Wyoming? since 1963
Where were you born? - Morgan Town, West Virginia
column4:
I am Catherine D. McQueen, a lifelong Democrat, longtime Casper resident, and former precinct committeewoman. After the loss of my husband, I raised three children in Wyoming while working hard to provide for my family and build a better future. Today, my children have successful careers, and I am proud that they learned the values of hard work, responsibility, and service to others.

I am running because I believe government should work for everyday people. Wyoming needs leaders who listen, who understand the challenges families face, and who are willing to work across differences to find practical solutions. My life experience, community involvement, and commitment to public service have prepared me to be a strong advocate for the people of House District 38.
column5:
Wyoming faces several significant challenges, including affordable housing, access to healthcare, workforce development, and ensuring our economy remains strong for future generations. Many families are feeling the impact of rising costs while communities work to attract and retain workers and young families.

As a legislator, I would focus on practical policies that help create jobs, expand housing opportunities, support small businesses, and invest in education and workforce training. I believe we must prepare Wyoming’s workforce for the jobs of tomorrow while supporting the industries that have long been the foundation of our economy. I will listen to constituents, work collaboratively with others, and support solutions that strengthen Wyoming families and communities for the long term.
column6:
Healthcare is a concern for families across Wyoming, particularly in rural communities where access to providers can be limited. The Legislature should work to strengthen rural healthcare systems, support efforts to recruit and retain doctors, nurses, and other healthcare professionals, and expand access to telehealth services where appropriate.

We should also look for ways to reduce unnecessary costs, increase transparency, and encourage competition that helps patients receive quality care at a reasonable price. Preventive care and early intervention can improve health outcomes while reducing long-term expenses.

Every Wyoming resident deserves access to affordable, quality healthcare regardless of where they live. I will support policies that help families, seniors, veterans, and rural communities access the care they need without placing additional financial burdens on them.
column7:
Wyoming’s public lands are an important part of our heritage, economy, and way of life. They support recreation, agriculture, energy development, wildlife, and tourism. Any decisions regarding public lands should be made carefully, transparently, and with significant public input.

I believe land management should balance conservation, responsible development, public access, and the needs of local communities. Wyoming residents should have a strong voice in decisions that affect the lands they use and depend upon.

As a legislator, I would support collaborative approaches that bring together local governments, land users, conservation interests, and industry stakeholders to ensure our public lands continue to benefit current and future generations of Wyomingites.
column8:
Property taxes are a significant concern for many homeowners, especially seniors and families living on fixed or limited incomes. At the same time, counties rely on property tax revenue to fund essential services such as public safety, roads, and local government operations.

I believe we should pursue responsible property tax relief while ensuring communities have the resources necessary to provide important services. Any reforms should recognize the unique circumstances of different counties and avoid creating unintended financial hardships for local governments.

The goal should be a balanced approach that protects taxpayers while maintaining the services that residents depend on every day. I would work with local officials and constituents to identify solutions that are fair, sustainable, and responsive to local needs.
column9:
The Legislature plays an important role in creating conditions that allow businesses and workers to succeed. Government cannot create prosperity on its own, but it can help provide the foundation for economic growth through infrastructure, education, workforce development, and a stable business environment.

Wyoming should continue supporting entrepreneurship, small businesses, agriculture, energy production, and emerging industries that can help diversify our economy. We should also focus on retaining young people by expanding opportunities for good-paying careers within the state.

I support policies that encourage investment, innovation, and job creation while ensuring economic growth benefits communities throughout Wyoming. A strong economy provides greater opportunity for families and strengthens the future of our state.
column10:
Campaign contributions should be transparent, lawful, and disclosed in a timely manner so voters can make informed decisions. The public deserves confidence that elected officials are accountable to the people they represent.

My approach is simple: I will be transparent about campaign contributions and follow all reporting requirements. I believe voters should be able to easily access information about who is supporting candidates and how campaign funds are being spent.

Wyoming should continue evaluating its campaign finance reporting systems to ensure they remain transparent, understandable, and accessible to the public. Public trust is strengthened when citizens can clearly see how campaigns are funded and when elected officials are committed to openness and accountability.

column2: I was born in Denver, moved to Casper in 1972 and then moved out of State upon graduation from UW with a BS in mechanical engineering.  After a 33 year career in oil & gas, I'm currently retired and I moved back to Casper's House District 56 several years ago with my family helping support my Mom.
column4: I've gained an extensive understanding of the oil & gas industry having 30+ years of front-line experience.  Managing large budgets, complex projects, working within teams holding dizzying arrays of diverse complexities, I'm well prepared to represent my district's multifaceted perspectives.  District 56 is one of the most politically mixed in the State.  As such, effective representation should position in the center.  I'm willing to reach in both directions so that the votes made in Cheyenne best represent the constituents rather than myopic views of the representative.  That said, I'm running as a Republican because I believe and support the Republican Platform.  I'll have no problem earning the endorsement of the Party when that day comes.  I'm transparent who I am and what I stand for so that the voters have a clear and informed understanding of expectations.  Please visit PeteFoxHD56.com to better understand my vision, plan and why I'm running.
column5: My vision and plan highlight our middle class and youth as facing the most significant challenges and opportunities.  Supporting these two groups helps them and everyone else reliant on them, including the elderly.  What's good for our kids is good for Wyoming and improvements include housing, employment, training, education, healthcare etc.  However, balancing economic prosperity with long term protections for our beautiful State is an absolute must.
column6: One perspective I've recently brushed with is the current pay scale of healthcare providers in our State to ensure we're providing up to date and competitive rates to our providers as a means of stabilizing this community and keep employees 'in-house' with reduced turnover.  I'll be looking further into this aspect to better vet these issues with those who truly understand the details.
column7: Open dialog is a must when land management changes are proposed.  I'll emphasize transparency of information prior to decisions being made.  Broadly, public land is a treasure to Wyoming which must be protected so that citizens can enjoy and cherish these lands without jeopardizing its long term health and access.  Making closed door, willy-nilly decisions involving public lands simply cannot be allowed to occur.
column8: The reductions to our assessed residential  property values via exemptions has allowed significant tax relief for us.  The big bump we had in our tax bills was largely driven by inflation and the exemptions have eliminated this bump.  Good!  However, increased prices are still there.  We feel it as does the government.  2026 dollars have a 44% reduction in purchasing power compared to 2013 dollars.  That's big.  Fortunately per the State Auditor, most cities, counties and special districts are spending less than the revenue coming in.  We're not going broke due to residential property tax reductions.  That said, I'm inclined to hold tight with further reductions in residential property taxes.  The 25% exemption we all received lowered our State's revenue by $100M.  To increase this exemption by another 50% that's on this fall's election ballot seems too much too soon.  Our Legislature will continue to wrestle with this issue and I'm confident balanced thoughtful decisions will prevail.
column9: The Legislature can stifle development with over regulation, or allow major harm by taking too loose of a laissez faire stance.  Our beautiful State demands protection.  So do workers' rights.  Within my 'vision/plan' at PeteFoxHD56.com, I outline my desire to target our middle class and youth for SMART growth.  That comes by balance, having all key stakeholders involved in decision making.  And those conversations require team builders to create a balanced framework that fits all the demands and requirements associated with smart growth.  We have to help our youth and young families.  If we don't, our State will suffer severely in the years to come!
column10: My campaign is small, lean and self-funded.  I have no 'strings attached' so to speak.  Personal donations are certainly welcomed, but there's no outside money coming into my campaign.  Seeing the issues with major electronic firms who organize campaign funding (e.g. 'smurfing' and 'dark money' funds), I've not used these serviced either to protect the donors who do choose to contribute.  If my chances of winning my election suffer from this minimalist approach, so be it.

column2: Casper, WY, HD59, I have lived five years in Wyoming, all in Casper. Business owner in the district over 10 years. I was born in Milwaukee, WI.
column4: I am a trucker and small business owner. I bring a commitment to fiscal responsibility, transparency, and practical problem-solving that is grounded in real-world experience and a strong understanding of how government policy affects everyday people. I’ve worked for a living and built a small business on the side, so I understand both what it means to work for a paycheck and what it takes to take risk, create value, and deal with the costs government decisions place on working people.I have also served the public as a municipal garbage man, doing the kind of honest, essential work that keeps our communities functioning and gave me a deep respect for working people, taxpayer funded services and the value of public service.
column5: Affordability, Inflation, and Trust in government. Addressing the affordability challenge by working to eliminate the corporate welfare that is plaguing this state and return the taxes to the taxpayers. Money is best spent by the people who earned it, not the government. Political insiders and special interest are getting our tax dollars for increased profits while the taxpayers take the risk and losses. The taxes can be used to reduce property taxes, upgrade infrastructure, fund Department of Transportation roadside checks to bust illegal truck drivers and hire more State Troopers. Inflation can be reduced on the state level by lowering regulations. State spending is also a major issue, and it must be managed with greater discipline, transparency, and accountability to taxpayers. More trust in government can be obtained by maintaining a 100% open, transparent, and accountable government. This is vital in a free society. Also, encouraging greater public participation in government.
column6: The Legislature can make healthcare more affordable and accessible by focusing on competition, price transparency, and reducing unnecessary regulatory barriers that drive up costs. When government overregulates healthcare, it limits providers and raises prices for patients. Focus on workforce shortages, consumer choice through Health Savings Account expansion, and a more competitive insurance market. Also, meet with healthcare professionals and discuss opportunities Wyoming has to make a better healthcare system in the state of Wyoming.
column7: I do not support broad sales or transfer of Wyoming’s public lands out of public hands. However, I do support shifting decision-making power closer to the people who actually live with the consequences of those decisions. The goal is not to lock land away or sell it off, but to ensure Wyoming voices carry more weight in how Wyoming land is used and managed.
column8: Eliminate the corporate welfare that is abundant in this state. One department alone gave away $255 million of our tax dollars to big business insiders and special interests for increased profits while the taxpayers take the risk and losses. The taxes would be better spent for local revenue needs instead of corporate welfare.
column9: Set a level playing field, reduce regulations and get out of the way. The current corporate welfare system distorts the free market. Also, promotion of Wyoming to recruit individuals, families and business to move here to grow the tax base without increasing taxes should be a role of the Wyoming Legislature.
column10: If someone likes my platform and wants to donate to that campaign that is okay. I think more reporting instead of reporting once before the election would be a great reform.

column2: Bar Nunn. 10 years. 10 years. Garland, Texas
column4: I'm originally from Alabama. I'm a combat veteran I served two tours in Iraq as a combat Infantryman. My little family has been here since 2016. I transport crude oil. We moved here because of the low taxes and less regulation. I'm currently serving as the the Mayor of Bar Nunn. I served on Bar Nunn Town Council previous to being for about 4 years. I have been married to Andrea Boyer for 18 years and we have 3 children together.
column5:
In my opinion three of the biggest challenges facing Wyoming are:
1. Young people leaving the state
2. Stagnant Economy
3. Poor Health care coverage
All three of these are interrelated
The opportunities we have here are primarily that we have a good base to work from. Oil, coal, gas and cattle have sustained the state for a long time and they provide a good foundation from which to build a more robust economy from.
I think one of the first things we need to do is really emphasizing and supporting the CTE programs for kids to move from high school to college to high paying jobs. We also need to change our mindset about incoming businesses and welcome them to the state. Looking at where regulations can be rolled back and taxes reduced would incentivize economic activity. Health care is tied to both of these issues. A robust economy will attract more health care professionals to the state as they will have high paying jobs to come too.
column6: I've already talked about it but reinvigorating the economy will help immensely with health care as high paying jobs for Wyomingites will draw in doctors to treat the people here.
column7:
No except in the case of public lands being backed up against a municipalities growth boundary in which case those lands need to be carefully considered for annexation and development.
We need to have a state constitutional amendment for one day if the Supreme Court hands down a decision to return federal lands back to the states so that those lands are protected.
column8:
There are two ways in which we can address the property tax issue. 
First: we need to excite the economy so that people can afford their homes and property taxes.
Second: we need to get our legacy industries moving again through deregulation and lowering severance tax.
Third: An incremental approach of slowly raising sales tax while slowly reducing property taxes is just one idea to help people feeling the pinch.
column9: Mainly to get out of the way of economic growth. Make sure there aren't burdensome regulations that will keep business away and keep the infrastructure in good repair. It signals to a business that the government is well run when infrastructure is well maintained and it allows for businesses to feel confident in establishing roots in the state.
column10: No it does need reform.

column2: Meeteetse, Wyoming, born in Cody, WY
column4:
Allen Hogg, 66, is a third-generation rancher who has worked the Upper Greybull River country his entire life. He operates the Hogg Ranch near Meeteetse the land his family has worked since 1918. Allen grew up in the Basin, earned his degree from Northwest College.
He and his wife Kristine have been married 46 years and have raised three sons. 
Allen brings more than two decades of public service to the race. As Vice-Chairman of the Park County Fair Board,  Treasurer of the Park County Predator Board, he helped secure state funding for programs protecting both livestock and Wyoming's wildlife habitat. He is a former President of the Wyoming State Association of County Fairs, and has worked alongside researchers from across the country to ensure that wildlife management decisions are grounded in both scientific research and the on-the-ground knowledge of the people who live and work on the land
column5: One of the biggest challenges facing Wyoming is the declining revenue from the mineral industry, protecting our public lands and protecting Wyoming water. I will work with other legislators to find alternate funding where possible and trim the fat in the budget. I will always support keeping public lands in public hands and protecting water rights.
column6: I support the open market for insurance because there is not enough competition to help lower the premiums.
column7: No!
column8: We need common sense tax relief when there is room in the budget. We must find balance, to keep the vital services available to Wyoming citizens.
column9: Wyoming Legislature should play a limited role in supporting local governments, in economic development.
column10: No

column2: I live in Powell.  I have lived in Powell for 6 years since retiring from the military.  I have been a Wyoming resident my entire life, but was physically in different areas while in the military after college.  I was born in Cody.
column4: I am a third-generation Wyomingite.  I was born in Cody and grew up in Powell.  I attended Northwest College, then the University of Wyoming and earned a bachelor's in electrical engineering.  I joined the Navy as an Officer after college and worked at Naval Reactors and Naval Sea Systems Command designing ships, submarines, and their systems.  I have received many awards for my leadership and hard work for our Nation's security including the Department of Defense Value Engineering Award and the Department of the Navy Cyberspace/IT Person of the Year.  I am a Wyoming Republican Party Platform candidate.  I will protect personal rights to life, liberty, property, and the pursuit of happiness as absolutes.  I will protect parent rights and their right to choose what is best for their children.  I will protect gun rights.  I am a fiscal conservative and will work to reduce taxes while driving for more economic investment to balance budgets.  I will protect public land.
column5:
I will start by saying we live in the most amazing and beautiful State in the Union.  We have a wealth of public lands which we can all enjoy.  There are forces attempting to take and sell these lands.  I will fight to keep our lands public.  Second, I am deeply concerned with the volume and proliferation of the theft of personal data by big tech firms and even auto manufacturers.  I will introduce legislation to protect personal data from theft.  Third, I am concerned by the push to erode parental rights.  I will protect parents’ rights to choose what is best for their children. 
I am a fiscal conservative and will work to reduce taxes which have disproportionately impacted our community seniors.  I will drive for more economic investment to balance budgets.  I am also concerned that we are not making care at home as accessible for our seniors as it should be.  I will fight to make the common sense and more economically viable solution available for all our seniors.
column6: I remember how affordable health care was before Obamacare, so the obvious answer is rolling that back.  Unfortunately, that is a federal issue to tackle.  What we can do is cut unnecessary regulation that forces overhead in the health care systems in Wyoming.  We have tremendous people doing their best at our local hospitals and care facilities.  The 307 Health model should be examined.  In Powell, it has proven to be successful and affordable.  I would point out that part of that success is lack of overhead and bureaucracy. I believe that care at home for our seniors is another critical aspect.  The Powell Senior Center is another example of what right looks like.  Making hygiene care, transportation, food, and social gatherings simple and affordable.  The Wyoming legislature should support these examples and decrease overhead and bureaucracy to the largest degree possible.
column7: Public land in Wyoming needs to remain public land.  I would support transfer of the land from the Federal government to the State government provided there was a plan to continue to support the land financially.
column8: I fully support property tax relief.  Wyoming has one of the most pro-business environments in the nation.  We should be able to attract business to Wyoming to offset the property tax relief.  I will make this a priority.  We need to offer more local jobs to keep our youth here.  We should consider consumption or point-in-time tax options.  Unfortunately, our property taxes disadvantage our seniors who have already paid their dues and are now living off of a fixed and very modest income.  This affects widows even more.  If we can end unnecessary bureaucracy with a scalpel in the state's budget that will help as well.  It will require a lot of work, but I am up for the challenge.
column9: Wyoming has an incredible business environment.  We need to evaluate why businesses aren't flocking here like they are to Texas and Florida.  I am sure part of it is infrastructure and part of it is perception that hiring will be challenging in a low population area.  We need to address these issues with industry.  I strongly believe we can address both of those concerns, but we need to advertise all of the advantages to industry and help provide any missing infrastructure and alleviate concerns about hiring.  Wyomingites have grit and determination and make the absolute best employees.  We need to capitalize on this fact with industry.
column10: My values are absolute transparency at all times.  I am new this year, but from what I can tell Wyoming's system for monitoring campaign donations is reasonable.

column2: I live in Buffalo, Wyoming. I have lived in Buffalo for over 30 years. I was born in Worland, Wyoming.
column4: I am a lifelong Wyomingite, raised in Johnson and Sheridan counties, where I learned the values of hard work, personal responsibility, and service to others. I raised my children in Buffalo. I understand how important it is to keep Wyoming a place where future generations can live, work, and raise their families. For three decades, I have served Wyoming through public service and community leadership, including a Johnson County Commissioner, a CNA, AEMT, Public Health Response Coordinator, Emergency Management Coordinator, and Public Information Officer for local fire districts. These experiences have given me firsthand insight into the challenges facing Wyoming families, farmers and ranchers, healthcare providers, emergency responders, and local governments. I am running to bring practical experience, fiscal responsibility, and conservative Wyoming point of view to the Legislature while protecting our freedoms, strengthening our communities, and securing Wyoming’s future.
column5:
Wyoming's greatest challenge is ensuring long-term economic stability.   As markets change and federal policies continue to impact industries, we must protect our core economic drivers while encouraging responsible diversification and innovation.

Another challenge is retaining and attracting young families, skilled workers, healthcare professionals, and first responders.  We must create opportunities that allow them to stay and succeed here.

Protecting Wyoming's water is another challenge. I would support our agencies with additional resources to meet this challenge.

Wyoming can capitalize on its strong work ethic, independent attitude, abundant natural resources, low-tax environment.  I will support policies that strengthen our energy and agricultural sectors, promote responsible economic growth, improve workforce development, and ensure state government lives within its means.

I will focus on practical solutions, local control, and responsible stewardship of taxpayer dollars.
column6:
Wyomingites struggle with rising insurance costs, long travel distances for care, and shortages of healthcare professionals.

 We must support rural hospitals, clinics, EMS services, and long-term care facilities that are often the backbone of healthcare in our communities. Also, we should continue reducing barriers to recruiting and retaining healthcare professionals, including nurses, physicians, mental health providers, and emergency responders.

We should review regulations and mandates that unnecessarily increase healthcare costs.   It is also important to support the efforts of the Wyoming Department of Health to manage and distribute the Rural Health Transformation funds.

My work experience has given me firsthand insight into the challenges facing patients and providers. As a legislator, I will work to strengthen Wyoming's healthcare workforce, support rural healthcare infrastructure, promote innovation.
column7:
Wyoming's public lands support ranching, energy production, recreation, wildlife habitat, tourism, and the outdoor traditions that define our way of life. I do not support broad efforts to sell public lands that would reduce public access or limit opportunities for future generations.

As a legislator, I will support multiple-use management that balances responsible energy and mineral development, healthy forests and rangelands, wildlife conservation, and public access. I also support stronger coordination among federal agencies, state and local governments, and private landowners to ensure decisions reflect local knowledge and Wyoming values.

The goal is balanced stewardship. By emphasizing local input, responsible management, and long-term planning, we can ensure Wyoming's public lands continue to benefit the people who live here today and future generations.
column8:
Property tax relief is warranted, but it must be balanced with the reality that counties, cities, schools, and special districts rely on those revenues to provide essential services.
 As a legislator, I will support a thoughtful approach that provides meaningful tax relief while carefully evaluating the impact on local governments and taxpayers. 
I would advocate for a state backfill or stabilization framework to mitigate revenue losses in counties with limited alternatives, particularly those without strong mineral production or tourism-based economies.
Any property tax relief should be accompanied by transparency and accountability regarding how lost revenue will affect services such as public safety, roads, senior services, and local infrastructure. We must avoid simply shifting costs from one group of taxpayers to another or creating long-term budget shortfalls.
Wyoming taxpayers deserve both tax relief and responsible government that lives within its means.
column9:
The Legislature should not attempt to direct the economy but instead create the conditions for private-sector growth, support local decision-making, and ensure Wyoming remains a place where businesses can invest, expand, and create good-paying jobs.
The Legislature should focus on maintaining Wyoming’s competitive advantages—low taxes, reasonable regulation, reliable infrastructure investment, and a strong commitment to property rights. These factors are essential for attracting and retaining businesses and families.
The Legislature can help by investing in workforce development, career and technical education, and partnerships between schools, community colleges, and industry. A skilled workforce is one of the most important factors in long-term economic growth.
column10:
Wyoming’s campaign finance system should be regularly reviewed to ensure transparency, public trust, and fairness in elections. Overall, I believe voters have a right to know who is funding political campaigns and how money is being spent.
If improvements are needed, they should focus on modernization, efficiency, and clarity so that Wyoming voters can easily see who is influencing elections and make informed decisions at the ballot box.

column2: Born in Kemmerer, WY. Raised in Big Piney, WY Currently live in Casper, WY since 2000.
column4:
I am a lifelong Wyomingite, educator, coach, entrepreneur, husband, and father. For more than two decades, I have taught and coached in Casper while also operating small businesses. Those experiences have given me firsthand insight into the challenges facing Wyoming families, students, workers, and employers.

My wife, Amy, and I raised our family in Casper because we believe it is one of the best places in the country to live. I am running because I want future generations to have the same opportunities Wyoming provided me.

I believe in local control, strong schools, responsible government, protecting our public lands, supporting a healthy business climate, and creating opportunities that allow young people and families to build their future right here in Wyoming. If elected, I will bring a practical, common-sense approach focused on listening, solving problems, and delivering more results and less drama.
column5:
Wyoming's biggest challenge is creating enough opportunity that our young people can build their future here instead of feeling they have to leave the state. Affordable housing, workforce development, access to healthcare, and a strong economy are all connected to that challenge. At the same time, Wyoming's greatest opportunity is our people, our natural resources, and our quality of life.

As a legislator, I will work to strengthen education, support responsible economic growth, reduce unnecessary barriers for businesses, and expand opportunities for career and technical training. I also believe we must protect our public lands and Wyoming way of life while continuing to support the industries that power our economy. My focus will be on practical solutions that help families thrive, keep communities strong, and ensure future generations can live, work, and raise a family in Wyoming.
column6:
Healthcare affordability and access are major challenges, especially in rural Wyoming. We need more healthcare providers, stronger support for rural hospitals and clinics, and expanded access to telehealth so people can receive care closer to home. I also support efforts to increase competition, improve price transparency, and reduce unnecessary regulations that drive up costs for patients and providers.

As our population ages, we should invest in services that help seniors remain independent, including home healthcare, preventative care, and transportation assistance. The goal should be a healthcare system that is accessible, affordable, and focused on the needs of Wyoming patients and families rather than bureaucracy.
column7:
I generally oppose efforts to sell or transfer public land because access to them is an important part of our economy, outdoor heritage, and quality of life. As a lifelong Wyomingite, I understand the importance of public access for hunting, fishing, recreation, and tourism.

At the same time, state trust lands have a constitutional responsibility to generate revenue for education.
