I believe managers, especially the State Lands and Investments Board, should consider the full value of these lands and not only the dollars they generate. Their benefits to wildlife, recreation, local communities, and future generations must be taken into account. The best approach is balanced management that protects public access, supports responsible multiple use, and ensures Wyoming citizens receive the greatest long-term benefit from both state and federal lands.
column8:
Wyoming homeowners deserve meaningful property tax relief, especially seniors and families facing rising costs. At the same time, counties, municipalities, and special districts rely on property tax revenue to provide essential services such as schools, roads, public safety, and infrastructure. Because Wyoming's counties have different economies, tax bases, and service needs, a one-size-fits-all approach is unlikely to work.

As a legislator, I would support responsible tax relief while carefully evaluating the impact on local governments and exploring ways to maintain essential services. I believe local communities should have a strong voice in these decisions, and any long-term solution must balance taxpayer relief with the need to keep our communities strong and well-served.
column9:
The Legislature has an important role in creating an environment where businesses can grow, workers can succeed, and young people can build their futures in Wyoming. That means investing in education and workforce development, supporting infrastructure, reducing unnecessary barriers to business growth, and ensuring Wyoming remains a competitive place to live and work.

I do not believe government should pick winners and losers, but it should be a partner in creating opportunities. If we want our young people to stay in Wyoming, raise families here, and strengthen our communities, we must continue creating good-paying jobs and pathways to success while preserving the Wyoming values that make our state such a special place to call home.
column10: I believe campaign donations should be transparent and reported in a way that allows the public to see who is supporting candidates and how campaign funds are being spent. Wyoming's current system includes contribution limits and disclosure requirements that promote accountability and public trust. While I am always open to improvements that increase transparency and make information more accessible to voters, I believe any reforms should be practical, easy to comply with, and focused on informing the public rather than creating unnecessary burdens for candidates and citizens who wish to participate in the political process.

column2: Bondurant, Wy. Physically lived there 20 years. Born Wilmington DE. While on active duty, Wyoming and Bondurant was my claimed residence beginning in 1978..
column4: 30 years active duty, US Navy. Nuclear Submarine Force. BS US Naval Academy, MSE The Catholic University of America. Eagle Scout. Active in observing and speaking to our legislature.
column5: Water in the Colorado watershed. Energy development to include nuclear. AI and data centers. Regarding the watershed, there will come a time when it is in court. Wyoming must be prepared for this. Continued development of coal, oil and natural gas is important to our economy and tax receipts. Nuclear is on the horizon but must be developed. Data Centers are coming and Wyoming must decide how many and where they belong.
column6: Increased staffing. The Program that pays education costs in exchange for future practice in Wyoming is very good and must be supported. Better use of internet and tele-medicine is important. Facility support should be considered.
column7: Public access to public lands must be maintained (eg hunting, fishing and recreation). I find roadless areas too extensive for good forest management.
column8: There are several ways to support these elements of county services. Probably the simplest is sales tax. As electric power generation expands taxation there may be useful. Property tax relief should be carefully applied to residential properties.
column9: The Legislature must ensure that bureaucracy rules are minimized. Bureaucracies always tend to expand their reach and authority, the legislature must pay attention to that. Bureaucracies can expand to fill the money available. Too many "good ideas" can spoil an economy.
column10: Reform is not needed. The people should be able to donate to their candidate of choice. The only thing that needs to be carefully monitored is out of state donations.

column2: I have lived in Laramie, Wyoming for the past 14 years. I was born in Cincinnati, Ohio but grew up in Pennsylvania. I moved to Laramie 14 years ago when I was offered a faculty position at the University of Wyoming College of Law.
column4: I was a practicing lawyer in Pennsylvania for over 20 years when I decided to pursue a career in academia. I have taught in law schools in Michigan and Indiana before accepting a position at the University of Wyoming College of Law 14 years ago. I retired from that position two years ago and now hold the rank of Professor Emeritus. I specialized in teaching legal writing and trial advocacy. I served a two-year term as President of the Legal Writing Institute (an organization of over 2000 legal writing professors around the country and into Canada). I have co-authored a groundbreaking book on legal writing (now in its third edition). My combined 25 years of experience in teaching legal writing prepared me well to help write statutes as a member of the Wyoming legislature. Before I retired, I served one year as Chair of the University of Wyoming Faculty Senate. I have learned to collaborate with others, which is a vital skill in working with other legislators to get bills passed.
column5:
1. Affordable housing
2. Job opportunities

These two issues are the main drivers of outmigration of our young people. We need to help facilitate new industries and new, good-paying jobs to attract young workers, while also doing what we can to drive down the cost of housing. Since infrastructure is one of the primary costs in driving up housing, any assistance the state or local governments can provide to drive down the cost of infrastructure will help reduce the cost of housing.

3. Building stronger communities

We also need to insure that our local communities are welcoming and accepting of all people, old and young, gay or straight, regardless of political affiliation or religion. Diverse communities are strong communities and are places where people can thrive. There is much the legislature can do to promote strong and diverse communities.
column6: Expand Medicaid. Support rural hospitals and health care delivery, including EMS services. Find ways to address the maternity deserts that are cropping up all over the state. Continue or expand support for WWAMI so that more young doctors locate in and hopefully remain in the state.
column7: Absolutely not. I will support all efforts to let our federal representatives know that sale of federal land is a terrible idea, even if that land is transferred to state ownership. At the state level, I will continue to resist any effort to sell state land to private interests. I will consider supporting land swaps, where private land is traded for state land of equal or greater value. I will also support public/private partnerships to enter into conservation easements or other efforts to protect wildlife or migration routes or otherwise preserve open spaces in perpetuity.
column8: I have proposed to the Joint Revenue Committee a new way of assessing land. The model is called "base year assessments." After a countywide fair-market-value assessment, all tax assessments would be frozen at that "base year" value for some period of time (say, 5 years) before being re-balanced. During the five year freeze, local governments would be given broader authority to adjust the millage rate to generate the revenue they need to provide the services that the public wants. It would be up to the local elected officials to have conversations with local residents to balance the need for services against the burdens of local taxes. And in the re-balancing years, local officials would be required to reduce the tax millage rate so that the total revenue generated in the rebalancing year is within a few percentage points of the previous year's revenue, so there would be no windfall in tax revenue due to accumulated inflation in real estate values.
column9: At a minimum, the legislature should insure a level playing field so that all businesses, large and small, have equal access to resources and are not unfairly burdened by taxes or fees. But I am also open to targeted programs of loans, grants, or tax incentives to help attract new businesses, since providing jobs for our young people is also a high priority of mine. Those programs must be administered by a non-partisan board of citizens, who would evaluate all proposals on a cost/benefit basis according to established criteria. The amount of funding to support those programs should be appropriated by the legislature and adjusted annually after the effectiveness of each program is evaluated.
column10:
I am in favor of "bright money" while I am steadfastly opposed to "dark money." I define "dark money" as money that cannot be traced back to any individual or group of individuals. Thus, most political action committees (PACs) would be considered bright money, since they must report their donors to the state.

I was one of the founders of Wyoming Promise, a Wyoming PAC dedicated to calling for a constitutional amendment to ban dark money from Wyoming politics. I continue to advocate for that amendment in my role as a state legislator. Dark money is drowning out the ability of ordinary citizens to have their voices heard both in Cheyenne and in Washington. We need an amendment to the United States Constitution to overturn the Citizens United decision, and I will continue to work to get such an amendment proposed and ultimately ratified.

column2: Casper, I have lived in Casper and Wyoming for 6 years.  I was born in Baltimore, MD
column4: Self-employed license insurance agent since 1975.  Volunteered my time working on healthcare insurance reform for 35 years.  Served for 7 years as a field auditor, Pennsylvania Auditor General's Office in the 1970s and 1980s
column5: Growth of government.  Rising taxes and cost of living.
column6: Encourage Congress to repeal the "Patient Protection and Affordable Care Act" and let Wyomingites buy the insurance they want and need.  Promote Association Health Plans so that small business such as contractors, ranchers and farmers in the same industry can ban together and negotiate plans with insurance companies.
column7: Limit the sale of public lands.  Increase the transfer of public lands from federal management to state management.  We know best on managing our lands.
column8: Reduce or eliminate residential property taxes; the most unfair tax as a home general does not produce income; just expenses.  Backfill the reduction from state funds.  Change the distribution percentage of sales tax on the first 4% to 50% state and 50% local.  More rural counties that have limited commercial sales tax revenue should receive adjustments.
column9: Government's role should be limited to infrastructure and not taxpayer paid subsidies to companies.  Government should not be in the business of private business and should not compete with our local businesses.
column10: Yes.  There should be an earlier filing before citizens vote so that they are better informed.  I have pledged to only receive donations from Wyomingites and political action committees based in Wyoming.  I have limited donations to $250 per person/$500 per married couple and $250 for Wyoming political action committees.  This is a self-imposed pledge.  But I would not support a government-imposed limit as the U.S. 1st amendment gives anyone the right to contribute what they want provided the candidate wants to accept it.  The answer to money in politics is transparency.

column2: Gillette Wyoming 58 years born in Gillette
column4: I am a fourth-generation resident of Wyoming. I own several small businesses and have to children both grown. I have 12 years of experience as a Campbell County Commissioner in my younger days and 5 years of experience as a Wyoming State House member. I am Chairman of Corporations, Elections and Political Subdivisions, Chairman of Management Audit and sit as a member of the Minerals Business and Economic Development Committee. I have also served on Travel Recreation and Wildlife as well as the Select Committee on Capital Finance and Investing. My years as a commissioner allow me to weigh local government needs and partnerships while giving me budget experience. The 5 years as a member of the House and the number of Committees I have served on give a wide span of learning important sectors of Wyoming economy and needs. My decision making includes research, public testimony and conservative principles.
column5: Wyoming continues to face challenges for future revenues which have typically come from our minerals industry/ Those revenues due to past "climate" administrations have been on the decline. With our current national need for energy dominance Wyoming should reduce restrictive regulations for our legacy industries. I have supported legislation creating bonding pools for small oil and gas operators, lowered severance taxes twice on coal, and will continue to strive removal of the low carbon regulations on power plants. I have voted for education bills that give more choices and enable our classro0ms to have the wages and tools to support Wyoming kids. Now that we have calibration we need to concentrate on additional changes if needed and ensuring revenues for the future. Our roads are a priority as we look to maintaining our infrastructure nod maintenance. Budgeting should align with the purpose of limited government and its priorities for our citizens.
column6: Healthcare is an issue for most rural communities. Cost is a result of patient expectation, increased technology and expense to meet the expectation and the insurance industry as well as Medicaid and Medicare that pay for that cost ultimately increasing for the patient. The rural healthcare funds could help in providing small communities with the base level of care and EMS service for next level regional transport.
column7: The federal government should transfer land and minerals to the state in an effort to equalize the eastern states entry into statehood. The west is at a disadvantage in developing both minerals and land use. Wyoming can better manage our lands and resources for multi-use and solve many outstanding land use issues in the process. I do not support the sale of public lands unless it resolves locked areas, reduces, the checkerboarding and makes sense for Wyoming citizens in the use of resources and public access.
column8: When we passed property tax relief it was the height of inflation and costs of insurance, medications, food and energy. Folks were deciding if they had to skip eating or paying property tax. The House voted in every proposal it sent to the senate backfill for local governments which we carved out in budget priorities during the supplemental budget. All those attempts were defeated by the sente or line item vetoed by the governor with $100 million going to one company through energy matching funds instead of loan program for fire damages to local communities. Direct distribution to cities towns and counties helps but does not take into account local improvement and service districts or water districts. Further property tax reductions need to include a solution for these entities. Every revenue and taxation move has a ripple effect and the Revenue Committee is working hard to find answers I look forward to seeing this session.
column9: Government should use regulation reduction, tax relief when appropriate and building up infrastructure such as roads, water and sewer, and other infrastructure helping communities to prepare for growth. We should not be picking individual companies for cash infusion. Every job we bring in that is not paid for costs us over $20.000 each in services we provide. This leads to employee shortages, housing shortages and other issues that government continues to be expected to pay for. Water issues, waste repositories, grid problems are serious consequences we need to address and decide on. We should build upwards on the resources available and abundant in Wyoming. This includes some parts of the nuclear chin like uranium mining and processing. Working with industry and energy for our trona mines and new coal plants. Chasing industries that only present increased challenges but contribute little actual revenue need more thought.
column10: Campaign donations are protected by the Supreme court ruling and the free-speech rights of the constitution. It would be great to get money out of politics but challenging facing the use of donations as a voice to represent our views and votes as citizens. I try to keep that principle in mind as we have worked on dark money bills and PAC transparency in the past. I list all campaign donors on the secretary of state reporting forms and like the fact that they are easily accessible to the public.

column2: I was born in November of 1971 in Virginia where my father was station in the Army.  Our family returned to Pine Bluffs Wyoming in 1972 where I have lived for 54 years
column4:
I am a fourth-generation farmer and small business owner, representing the people of House District 10. Raised in Pine Bluffs, I graduated from Pine Bluffs High School in 1990 and earned a bachelor's degree from the University of Wyoming in 1995. After college, I returned home to farm with my brother and help grow several local businesses.

My commitment to service includes 16 years as President of Laramie County Fire District 5, election as Mayor of Pine Bluffs in 2021, and appointment to the Wyoming House of Representatives in 2025. I also serve on the Board of Directors for the Wyoming Municipal Power Agency and represent Eastern Laramie County through Cheyenne LEADS.

My wife, Kara, and I have built our life in Pine Bluffs and are raising our daughter, Keefer. I believe in limited government, fiscal responsibility, strong communities, support for agriculture and small business, and protecting Wyoming's way of life for future generations.
column5: Wyoming’s biggest challenges are affordability, healthcare access, workforce shortages, housing, infrastructure and protecting our core industries such as energy and agriculture. Our opportunities include: energy, agriculture, tourism, small business, outdoor recreation and a strong quality of life. As a legislator, I would focus on practical, conservative solutions: keeping taxes low, supporting local control, investing wisely in infrastructure and workforce, and making sure state government lives within its means.
column6: Healthcare is especially difficult in a rural state with long distances and small populations. Wyoming should support rural hospitals and clinics, expand telehealth, grow our healthcare workforce, reduce unnecessary regulation and look for ways to increase competition and transparency in pricing. I also believe we must carefully review federal opportunities that improve rural care without creating unsustainable long-term obligations for the state.
column7: I generally oppose the sale of public lands. Public lands are an important part of Wyoming's heritage and support recreation, grazing, hunting, tourism, and economic development. Once public lands are sold, they are often lost to public access permanently. However, I recognize that limited and carefully considered land exchanges can sometimes be beneficial when they improve management, enhance public access, resolve boundary issues, or support critical infrastructure projects. Any proposed exchange should be transparent, provide a clear public benefit, and protect Wyoming's long-term interests.
column8: People need property tax relief, especially homeowners on fixed incomes, but counties, schools, fire districts and local governments also rely on that revenue. I support targeted, responsible relief that protects residents without creating budget crises for essential services. The Legislature should look at exemptions and caps while being honest about impacts on local governments and special districts.  While recent legislation has addressed tax relief, we should consider a long term solution that would likely require a complete overhaul of the system.
column9: The Legislature should not pick winners and losers, but it should create the conditions for growth: low taxes, reasonable regulation, reliable infrastructure, workforce training, housing availability and support for Wyoming’s core industries. Economic development should be locally driven, transparent and focused on long-term private-sector jobs.
column10: My values are simple: be transparent, follow the law and never let a donation influence a vote. People deserve to know who is supporting candidates and how money is being spent. Wyoming’s system should be easy for the public to access, simple for candidates to comply with and strong enough to maintain trust in elections.  I believe our current system provides for those things so don't think there is a need for reform.

column2: I have lived here in Albany County for over 23 years. My house is about 10 miles south of Laramie, in House District 46. That's been my home ever since I moved to Wyoming after retiring from the military.
column4:
Education: BS, MS, PhD in electrical engineering, licensed Professional Engineer (PE) in Wyoming

Experience:  
30 years military service (Navy and Air Force, enlisted and officer).  Retired as a Lieutenant Colonel.  Leadership instructor, firearms instructor, R&D engineer, faculty member at the U.S. Air Force Academy.
23 years teaching as an engineering faculty member at the University of Wyoming, including 6 years as Dean of the College of Engineering and Physical Sciences.  Recently retired from UW.

Skills: More than four decades of real-world leadership experience for both small and large organizations, managing people (as many as 300+ people), budgets (as much as $758 million), and efficiently achieving goals and objectives while balancing available resources.  

My leadership training and experience have given me the ability to keep my ego and personal views out of decisions, avoid micromanaging, and trust competent people to do their jobs.  And always---integrity first.
column5: Wyoming has great opportunities. Our core industries (energy, agriculture, mining, tourism, etc.) are making great strides, small businesses are expanding, and entrepreneurs are starting new businesses in the state. Wyoming has the most attractive business environment in the country, and our economy is poised for growth.  But there are challenges.  We must pay close attention to public concern about proposals such as those for datacenters and windfarms, and closely evaluate the advantages and disadvantages of such proposals both individually and as part of the big picture of what we want for Wyoming. We also want our children to be able to stay in the state, which means both a growing economy but also affordable, available housing. As a legislator, I would learn about and listen to all sides of these issues and others, get input from my constituents, ignore any pressure from out-of-state groups or narrow, personal agendas, and work on legislation that benefits Wyoming and our people.
column6: I recently spent an entire day at the 2026 Wyoming Healthcare Policy Summit, and learned a great deal about the challenges facing the Wyoming healthcare landscape.  There are currently six rural hospitals in Wyoming that may have to close within a few years due to funding issues.  We have both a shortage of healthcare professionals, and a structural challenge of our medical facilities facing significant fixed costs, low patient populations, and inadequate reimbursement from sources such as Medicaid and Medicare.  We have to find innovative ways to attract and retain more healthcare professionals to the state (and this also involves the problem of insufficient affordable, available housing), and explore ways we can help medical facilities survive with low patient counts and low reimbursement. This is not an easy problem to solve, but it must be a high priority for the legislature.  Quality of life in Wyoming depends upon it.
column7: In general, no I don't support that. There may be rare special circumstances that can be evaluated on a case-by-case basis, but overall I believe public lands should stay public, and they should be accessible to the public. Responsible use and stewardship of our public lands is something all citizens of Wyoming should expect and demand. We must work hand in hand with federal organizations such as the BLM and the Forest Service, and with our federal Congressional delegation, to ensure we protect our public lands.
column8: Essential government services funded by property taxes cannot be allowed to wither and become ineffective due to loss of funding as a result of property tax reductions. While back-filling the losses incurred by cities and counties if property taxes continue to be reduced might seem to be a possible solution, it can’t be a permanent solution. Broadly disseminated, honest, and balanced communication to the population is essential on this topic. The voters must be made aware of the need for a reasonable level of property taxes, and convinced not to vote for an arbitrary reduction with little or no consideration to the unavoidable consequences. It's important for all of us to keep in mind that taxes are necessary to fund government services that can’t be funded or performed by individuals, but not more than that. Taxes should be broadly based, nondiscriminatory, and only at a level needed to fund those essential government services.  Nothing more than that.
column9: I agree that the legislature can help stimulate economic development, but we must proceed with caution. We don't want to get into the trap of picking winners and losers, but we must also be cognizant that so many other states actively engage in legislative programs to attract industry to their state. If we do nothing, Wyoming will lose out.  That being said, keeping government out of the way, maintaining a favorable tax structure, and encouraging  expansion of both our core industries and small businesses in general, along with enabling the creation of new businesses... all that together will lead to the best way to expand our economy. Over-regulation, regulatory overreach, unfunded mandates, and an encroaching “nanny state” (mostly from the federal level) are some of the biggest obstacles to economic prosperity for Wyoming. The legislature should be alert to these obstacles and find ways to reduce them whenever possible.
column10: Funding for political campaigns should be as transparent to the voters as possible.  Big donors hiding behind anonymous, typically out-of-state, organizations that try to manipulate Wyoming politics can only hurt Wyoming in the long run.  I would support whatever the Wyoming legislature can do to make campaign funding more visible to the voters.  One possible problem currently is the deadline for reporting campaign donations is so close to the election (both primary and general) that few voters are able to use that information in time to consider that in their voting decisions. We should look into ways to make that information available in a more timely manner for the voting public.

column2: I currently reside in Casper, Wyoming. I have lived in my current home since July 2019. I have lived in Wyoming since 1978. I was born in Broken Bow, Nebraska.
column4: I am uniquely qualified to serve Senate District 27 because of my professional experience and a strong record of community service. I work as a paralegal in family law helping families through difficult situations and gaining practical knowledge of the legal system and working with the statutes that are considered and possibly passed on the Senate floor. I am working toward my Master's in Mental Health Counseling to further help our community. I am serving in the Republican party and volunteer on nonprofit boards to engage in different facets of our communities. As a wife, mother, and Wyoming woman, I understand the values and challenges facing our communities and will bring principled, conservative leadership to Cheyenne.
column5:
Wyoming faces serious challenges in mental and physical healthcare access, the rising cost of living, and the need for a stronger, more diverse economy. 
As a legislator, I would support efforts to expand access to mental and physical healthcare services, recruit and retain healthcare providers, and remove barriers that keep care out of rural communities. I would also fight for responsible policies that will help Wyoming families such as encouraging business growth, job creation, and economic diversification so Wyoming can remain a place where families thrive and future generations can stay and build lives.
column6: We need to recruit and retain providers to all of Wyoming. This can be accomplished by reducing unnecessary regulations that make it harder to practice in Wyoming. Provide incentives for providers to care for individuals in rural areas. Promoting price transparency, support competition and looking at possible medical liability reforms to help bring down the cost of malpractice insurance.
column7: No, I do not support the sell or transfer of public land in Wyoming. Land management decisions should be made by the people of Wyoming. We are the owners of the land. I will support strong state and local input, protect multiple use, defend access for recreation, grazing, and responsible energy production, and making sure that our land is managed in a way that respects our heritage and way of life. Decisions should be made openly, with meaningful involvement from local communities, so that Wyoming voices are heard and our natural resources are managed responsibly.
column8: Any solution to this must balance the homeowner and property owner with the need for counties, schools, and local services to remain funded. I support thoughtful, responsible tax relief that helps families with rising costs, while also respecting local control and recognizing the different revenue structures and needs across Wyoming. I would look for solutions that provide relief without creating unintended harm for essential services of public safety, roads, and education. Working with local governments, taxpayers, and community leaders to develop fair and flexible policies for each Wyoming community.
column9: The Wyoming Legislature has a limited but important role in economic development. The legislature should help encourage a stable, business-friendly environment where private enterprise can grow. The legislature can help by reducing unnecessary regulation, support infrastructure, encourage workforce development, and adopt policies that help businesses invest, hire, and succeed in Wyoming.
column10:
Campaign donations should be legal, transparent, and never allowed to influence the voice of the people I am elected to serve. My campaign will be conducted with honesty, integrity, and full compliance with the law. 
Wyoming's system for monitoring campaigns should be reviewed regularly to ensure that it is transparent, easy for the public to understand, and candidates can clearly and transparently comply with campaign disclosures.

column2:
I currently live in Cody, Wyoming. I was born in Cody, grew up here, and have spent nearly my entire life in Wyoming.

After graduating from high school in 2004, I moved to Las Vegas to attend UNLV’s William F. Harrah College of Hospitality, where I prepared for my future role in our family business, Blair Hotels. After graduating in May of 2009, I attended real estate school, earned my license, and built a successful real estate career in Las Vegas.

In 2014, I returned home to Cody to carry my share of the family business and continue building my life in the community that raised me. Other than my time in Las Vegas from 2004 to 2014, I have been a lifelong Wyoming resident.
column4:
I am a fifth-generation Park County resident, a third-generation hotelier, and a father to two young children — my son, Quin Jude, and my daughter, Brittania Adalyn “Ruthie” Blair. I was born and raised in Cody, and other than my time in Las Vegas attending UNLV’s William F. Harrah College of Hospitality, Wyoming has always been home. At UNLV, I graduated with a 3.96 GPA, completed Honors studies, and earned a Marketing minor.

I serve as Chief Marketing Officer of Blair Hotels, a family business operating five Wyoming hotels, including three in Cody. My work has given me practical experience in business leadership, budgeting, workforce challenges, tourism, marketing, and economic development.

My public service includes two terms on the Wyoming Office of Tourism Board, where I served as Vice Chair. I currently chair the Park County Travel Council and Cody Fireworks Committee, serve with the Cody Community Foundation, I serve on the committee of PEAKS to Conga and ride it each year.
column5:
The greatest challenge facing Wyoming is protecting our way of life while preparing responsibly for the future. That challenge is made harder by outside influences that do not understand Wyoming, our values, or the people who built this state.

We must protect the freedoms we hold dear: free speech, the Second Amendment, property rights, local control, and the pursuit of liberty and happiness. At the same time, we have to make sure Wyoming remains strong, competitive, and affordable for the next generation.

As a legislator, I would focus on accountability, fiscal discipline, and common sense. Every tax dollar should be spent carefully, with zero tolerance for waste, fraud, or abuse. We should make sound investments in industries that strengthen our state’s finances, encourage business, support working families, and defend Wyoming against federal overreach and the erosion of our rights.
column6:
The Legislature should focus on practical solutions that lower costs, keep care close to home, and strengthen the providers we already have.

In Wyoming, access is often just as important as affordability. We need creative, conservative solutions that improve quality of life while minimizing costs and maximizing efficiency. One example is helping seniors age in place. Supporting family caregivers, home health services, transportation, meal delivery, senior centers, and basic home modifications can help older Wyomingites stay safely in their homes longer. That is better for families, better for communities, and often far less expensive than institutional care.

We should also expand telehealth, support rural clinics and hospitals, improve EMS coverage, recruit and retain healthcare workers, and remove unnecessary barriers that make care harder to provide. The goal should be simple: better access, lower costs, stronger local care, and responsible use of taxpayer dollars.
column7:
No. I do not support selling or transferring public land in Wyoming. Public land belongs to the public, and it should remain available for the benefit of the people; not sold off, locked up, or handed over to special interests.

Pride in the land is a core Wyoming value. Our public lands support hunting, fishing, grazing, energy, tourism, recreation, conservation, and the freedom to enjoy the outdoors. They are also part of the heart and soul of what makes Wyoming special.

The best path forward is responsible, Wyoming-first management. That means keeping access open, respecting multiple use, involving local voices, protecting wildlife and natural resources, supporting agriculture and energy where appropriate, and pushing back when federal agencies make decisions without understanding the people who live here. Public land should be managed for the people, by the people.
column8:
Property tax relief is important, but Wyoming should not pretend every county has the same economy or the same options. The answer is not a blanket approach from Cheyenne. It is giving counties room to use the strengths they already have.

In Park County, that strength is tourism. The lodging tax is overwhelmingly paid by visitors, not local families. It helps market our community, support local businesses, strengthen jobs, and bring outside dollars into our economy.

That matters. Roughly 30% of Park County’s sales tax is paid by visitors, helping lower the tax burden on the people who live and work here year-round. Those visitor dollars support local services and infrastructure without relying as heavily on residents to cover the cost.

As a legislator, I would support real property tax relief while protecting local tools that allow counties to solve problems their own way. Local solutions beat one-size-fits-all government.
column9:
The Wyoming Legislature should play an important role in economic development by creating the conditions for businesses, workers, and communities to thrive. Government should not pick winners and losers, hand out unfair advantages, or subsidize business models that cannot stand on their own.

The Legislature’s role is to create the conditions where business can succeed. That means strong infrastructure, reliable roads, reasonable regulation, low taxes, access to workforce training, support for trades and agriculture, and a business climate that rewards hard work and investment.

Economic development should start with Wyoming businesses, Wyoming workers, and Wyoming communities. The state should remove barriers, protect opportunity, and make smart investments that strengthen our long-term economy, not use taxpayer dollars to fund favored projects while hard-working Wyomingites pick up the tab.
column10:
Wyoming campaigns should be driven by Wyoming people, Wyoming communities, and Wyoming interests. I am concerned when outside money flows into local races, we risk outside agendas that do not reflect our values or our way of life. Elections should not be bought by those with no real stake in the communities affected by the outcome.

I will always value local support over outside influence, and believe campaign finance rules should help voters see who is trying to shape Wyoming’s future.

If reform is needed, it should be practical and focused. Reports should be clear, public, and easy to search. Rules should be simple enough for grassroots candidates to follow, and penalties should target fraud and intentional violations, not honest mistakes. The goal should be trust, not additional bureaucracy.

column2: Rock Springs, since 2021.  In Wyoming since 2019.  Previously lived in Cody, WY 2001-2004
column4:
I am an Army Special Operations veteran and a military-trained Physician Associate specializing in Orthopedics. For most of my adult life, I have served as either a protector or a healer.

My wife Tracy and I have been married for nearly three decades. We have four children and three grandchildren. We moved to Rock Springs in 2021 and previously lived in Cody from 2001-2004 and 2019-2021.

I currently serve in the Wyoming House of Representatives on the Labor, Health and Social Services Committee and the Transportation, Highways and Military Affairs Committee.

Before serving in the Legislature, I spent years caring for patients and helping families through difficult medical decisions. In the Legislature, I have focused on protecting constitutional rights, supporting parents, improving healthcare access, promoting fiscal responsibility and ensuring government remains accountable to the people.

I am running for reelection because Wyoming is worth protecting.
column5:
Wyoming's biggest challenge is preserving our way of life while keeping the state affordable for working families, retirees and young people. Rising property taxes, government spending, healthcare costs and workforce shortages are concerns I hear regularly from constituents.

Our greatest opportunities remain the industries and people that built Wyoming: energy, agriculture, small business, natural resources and a strong culture of independence.

As a legislator, I will continue working to reduce the tax burden on Wyoming families, protect private property rights, support reliable and affordable energy production, improve healthcare access and reduce unnecessary regulations.

Wyoming does not have a revenue problem; it has a spending and prioritization problem. Government should focus on its core responsibilities, live within its means and respect the taxpayers who fund it.
column6:
As a healthcare provider, I have seen firsthand the challenges facing patients, providers and rural hospitals.

The Legislature cannot solve every problem in healthcare, but it can remove barriers that drive up costs and reduce access. We should continue expanding telehealth where appropriate, encourage more healthcare professionals to practice in Wyoming and reduce unnecessary regulations that make care more expensive.

We should also increase price transparency so patients know what they are paying for and can make informed decisions about their care.

Many rural communities struggle to attract providers. We should look for practical ways to support healthcare delivery in those areas while maintaining quality care close to home.

Healthcare decisions should be made by patients and their providers, not bureaucracies. My goal is simple: lower costs, increase access and ensure Wyoming families can receive quality care when and where they need it.
column7:
I do not support the sale of Wyoming's public lands and would vote against legislation that broadly authorizes their sale.

Public lands are important to Wyoming's economy, culture and way of life. Hunters, anglers, ranchers, energy producers, outdoor recreationists and local communities all rely on access to these lands.

At the same time, I believe Wyoming should have a stronger voice in how federal lands within our borders are managed. Decisions made in Washington, D.C., often fail to account for the realities faced by the people who live and work here.

Land management should follow multiple-use principles that balance recreation, grazing, wildlife, energy production and responsible stewardship.

The people closest to the land generally understand it best. Wyoming's interests are best served when local communities have a meaningful role in land management decisions.
column8:
I strongly support meaningful property tax relief and ultimately believe Wyoming should work toward eliminating property taxes on primary residences.

People should not spend a lifetime paying off a home only to face the possibility of being taxed out of it. Property ownership is one of the most basic rights we have.

At the same time, counties, municipalities and special districts provide services that citizens depend upon. The answer is not to continue raising taxes. The answer is to prioritize spending and focus government on its core responsibilities.

Wyoming has significant financial resources and reserve accounts. Before asking taxpayers for more, government should carefully examine where money is being spent and whether those expenditures are truly necessary.

I believe Wyoming can provide meaningful property tax relief while maintaining essential services through responsible budgeting, spending discipline and long-term planning.
column9:
The Legislature's primary role in economic development is to remove obstacles, not create new programs.

Government does not create prosperity. Wyoming workers, ranchers, energy producers, small business owners and entrepreneurs do.

The Legislature should maintain a stable regulatory environment, protect private property rights, keep taxes low, support reliable infrastructure and ensure businesses have the freedom to grow and hire.

I do not believe government should be in the business of picking winners and losers. When government starts favoring certain industries or businesses, someone else ends up paying the bill.

The best economic development policy is simple: lower taxes, fewer unnecessary regulations, affordable energy and a government that stays focused on its proper role.
column10:
I have chosen not to accept direct campaign contributions from PACs or professional associations. My campaign support has come from individual citizens who believe in the principles and policies I advocate.

That is a personal decision, and other candidates are free to make different choices within the law.

Transparency is the most important part of any campaign finance system. Voters should be able to easily see who is contributing to campaigns and how campaign funds are being spent.

Wyoming's reporting requirements already provide substantial public disclosure. If reforms are considered, they should focus on improving transparency and public access to information without creating unnecessary burdens that discourage citizens from running for office.

Ultimately, informed voters are the best safeguard for accountable government.

column2: Cheyenne, Wyoming. I was born in Wheatland and have lived in Cheyenne since i was two weeks old.
column4:
I am a lifelong Wyomingite, born in Wheatland and raised in Cheyenne. My career is defined by executive leadership and community advocacy. As CEO of the Cheyenne Animal Shelter and founder of Black Dog Animal Rescue, I have managed large-scale operations, balanced complex budgets, and built statewide coalitions. I hold a Master’s in Nonprofit Management (MNM) and am a Certified Animal Welfare Administrator (CAWA).

My civic dedication runs deep. Appointed by Governor Gordon to the Wyoming State Board of Veterinary Medicine, I have worked directly on statewide regulatory oversight. My leadership as past chair of the Wyoming Nonprofit Network and Leadership Cheyenne, combined with my experience in Leadership Wyoming, gives me deep insights into our local communities. I have a proven track record of bringing people together to achieve meaningful results, and I am ready to bring that transparent, fiscally responsible leadership to the Wyoming Legislature to serve Cheyenne.
column5:
Wyoming’s greatest challenge and opportunity lie in securing a vibrant future for our young people and creating communities where every family can thrive. True long-term fiscal stability doesn't just come from balancing budgets; it comes from investing in our people. Currently, we face the critical challenge of retaining our youth, who too often leave the state in search of better housing options, accessible healthcare, and diverse professional paths.

By shifting our focus toward sustainable community development, we can turn these challenges into monumental opportunities. As a legislator, I will bring my executive experience in building broad coalitions to advocate for public policies that strengthen local infrastructure, protect our unique quality of life, and ensure our public services are dependably funded. By prioritizing the well-being of our citizens and empowering our youth to build their lives right here, we will create a more resilient, prosperous Wyoming for all.
column6:
To ensure healthcare stability, the Legislature must prioritize a pro-people framework that leverages every available funding source. I support utilizing the proposed healthcare perpetuity fund to secure predictable, long-term investments in our medical infrastructure. We must take immediate action to protect our vulnerable communities, putting people first by directly addressing the devastating closures of rural hospitals and the critical lack of maternal healthcare and labor and delivery centers across the state.   

Furthermore, we need to direct robust state funding straight to hospitals to keep their doors open. The Legislature must also work closely with insurance providers to ensure equal coverage and access to hospital-based surgeries, ensuring patients aren't penalized for needing hospital care over an ambulatory surgery center. By stabilizing funding, we can ensure every Wyomingite has access to the care they deserve.
column7:
I do not support the sale or transfer of public lands. Our public lands are Wyoming’s greatest legacy, and responsibly managing them is critical to protecting our wildlife, preserving our heritage, and maintaining our thriving outdoor recreation and tourism industries. To ensure these lands are managed in the best interest of Wyomingites, the state must prioritize collaborative stewardship over privatization.

A specific, proven policy initiative I will continue to champion is the robust allocation of state funds—matched with federal infrastructure grants—specifically dedicated to wildlife crossing corridors and game fence construction. Projects like these directly protect migration routes, significantly reduce vehicle-wildlife collisions, and keep our ecosystems intact. By actively supporting these targeted conservation investments, the Legislature can practically safeguard our natural resources while keeping public lands open, accessible, and safe for all.
column8:
I do not support further property tax reductions. While relief for struggling homeowners is important, we must recognize that Wyoming taxes directly fund the essential public services our communities rely on. Past state analysis highlights that the average Wyoming resident receives upwards of $13,000 per year in public benefits and services—far exceeding what the typical household pays in taxes. Further eroding this revenue stream threatens the very fabric of our communities.

Instead of cutting resources, our focus must be on maximizing these investments to support our people where they need it most. We must protect this revenue to ensure our aging population has the ability to age with dignity at home, access critical healthcare, and secure dependable support for their daily needs. By maintaining strong public funding, we can stabilize our local emergency services, senior centers, and care networks, keeping Wyoming a safe, supportive place to live for a lifetime.
column9:
The Legislature’s role in economic development must be pro-people and rooted in meaningful collaboration. State entities like the Wyoming Business Council should serve as supportive partners, working hand-in-hand with our municipalities rather than imposing top-down mandates. The state’s role is to provide a helpful framework, robust resources, and guidance tied directly to the unique benchmarks and goals identified by individual communities themselves, ensuring we protect the distinct character that makes our towns special.

Instead of one-size-fits-all solutions, we must utilize state infrastructure and workforce training funds to invest heavily in human capital—supporting homegrown entrepreneurs, improving child care access, and building attainable housing. By empowering local leaders to guide their own economic futures, we can create an environment where young people choose to build their lives right here, strengthening Wyoming from the ground up.
column10:
Wyoming’s system for monitoring campaign finance requires meaningful reform to ensure absolute transparency and protect the integrity of our elections. My chief concern centers on the rise of independent expenditures by third-party organizations. Currently, hidden contributions—such as paid political door-knockers and dark-money operations—influence our local races without their expenses ever appearing on a candidate's individual filing. Voters have a right to know exactly who is funding the messages hitting their doorsteps.

To restore trust, all contributions to PACs and candidates must be clearly, promptly, and transparently reported to the public. Furthermore, the Legislature should implement strict limits on both monetary and in-kind contributions coming from out-of-state entities. Wyoming elections should be decided by Wyoming citizens, and our campaign finance laws must evolve to prevent outside interests from drowning out local voices.

column2: I live at 808 West Dale Blvd. in Cheyenne. I have lived here since 2008. I have lived in Wyoming all of my life, except for a short stint in Denver from 1981-1982 and Anchorage in 1990. I was born in Cheyenne, Wyoming.
column4: I graduated from Cheyenne East High School in 1997. When I was growing up, my family had a small ranch south of Encampment Wyoming. I spent every weekend and summer there. That is where I developed my love of the mountains. The ranch borders Medicine Bow National Forest, which was a very big back yard! I received a BS in Chemical Engineering from the University of Wyoming. I worked in the oil business for 10 years and for a chemical manufacturing company for 12. I worked for several small consulting companies before starting my own consulting company, Hanlon Process Consulting, LLC, in 2013. My background gives me unique insight into the industries that drive Wyoming's  economy. As a consultant I helped multiple companies operate more safely, efficiently, and profitably. The state of Wyoming needs the same kind of support.
column5:
We are challenged by a constitutional mandate and supreme court rulings to fund a quality public education.Developing the holistic funding and oversight mechanisms to ensure that we provide every child with the best education possible without a contentious and divisive disagreement every year is a challenge I am ready for.

Wyoming has the opportunity to become the "power house" for the rest of the country. We need to listen to the people who live and work here and develop the resources with Wyoming jobs and quality of life as our primary objectives. We can do that. The challenge is to develop those resources in a way that allows our children and grandchildren to stay here because they have outstanding job opportunites and a way that causes them to want to stay here because of the quality of life.
column6:
We can take advantage of the federal support available to us to maximize coverage and reduce premium costs. Wyomingites have paid the federal taxes that fund these programs. It is foolish to reject the funding and make life difficult for so many Wyoming families. We can work with Wyoming insurance companies to establish creative programs to reduce their risk and pass that savings on to Wyoming people. Those are easy steps that will require very little state funding.

When we take those necessary steps, there will still be working Wyoming families that can't afford their health insurance premiums. It is a more difficult and contentious task to create programs to ensure all Wyomingites can afford their health insurance but the resources are available. If we have the will, we can provide affordable insurance to everyone who wants insurance.
column7:
Everyone in Wyoming (with the possible exception of a few politicians who have been influenced by East Coast agendas) opposes the sale or transfer of public lands. No other issue brings us all together as one community in the same way. I stand with all my friends and neighbors who want to keep public lands in public hands!

To manage the lands in the best interests of the people of Wyoming, we must ensure that management practices are science-based and that the agencies that manage state and federal public lands have the financial resources to manage the lands well. At the same time, we should ensure that we take maximum advantage of new technology to reduce management costs.
column8: The legislature must ensure that the tax structure developed by the state has a high enough "ceiling" that each local government can provide the services that their constituents require.
column9: The purpose of government is to secure collective stability, protect individual rights, and manage systemic risks, creating a predictable framework where people can safely cooperate and prosper. If the legislature takes that purpose seriously, they will always have a central role in economic development.
column10: The system for monitoring campaign donations and expenditures seems to work pretty well. It would be nice if all campaigns were funded the same, but that isn't the way the world works and it certainly isn't something that I have the power to change.

column2:
I live in Casper, Wyoming, where I was raised and where my family and I have chosen to build our lives.  I was born in Laramie while my father was attending school there and moved to Casper around age 5.  Other than leaving Wyoming for my education and medical training, I have spent virtually my entire life in Wyoming.

After graduating from high school in Casper, I pursued my medical education through the WWAMI program and continued with my surgical training at Dartmouth, Stanford University, and Harvard Medical School, where I served as an Instructor of Surgery.  My goal was always to bring that knowledge and experience back home.  After training ended in 2015, I returned to Casper to practice surgery and have been here ever since.

Today, I am a surgeon, small business owner, husband, father, and am deeply invested in the Casper community.  Wyoming has given me so many opportunities, and I am committed to helping ensure future generations can build successful lives here as well.
column4:
I am a Wyoming-raised surgeon, small business owner, husband, and father. After growing up in Casper, I pursued my education through the WWAMI program and completed medical training at Dartmouth, Stanford, and Harvard before returning home to practice surgery in Wyoming.

Today, I own and operate Precision Surgical, caring for patients from across Wyoming while employing Wyoming residents. Through my work, I see firsthand the challenges facing our state including healthcare access, rising costs, workforce shortages, and the pressures facing small businesses.

Most importantly, I am living many of the same experiences as the people I hope to represent. My wife and I are raising children, helping care for aging parents, planning for the future, and managing the rising costs of everyday life. I am running because I believe Wyoming needs practical, thoughtful leadership grounded in faith and service, focused on strengthening families, communities, and opportunities for future generations.
column5:
Wyoming faces challenges and opportunities. Rising costs pressure families, retirees, and small businesses, making affordability a top concern. We must maintain a strong economy, support job creators, and ensure government spends taxpayer dollars responsibly.

We also need to strengthen educational and career pathways that encourage young people to stay in Wyoming or return after training. Expanding opportunities in skilled trades, technical education, and professional careers will help build the workforce our communities need.

Affordable, accessible healthcare remains a critical priority, especially in rural areas where workforce shortages and rising costs threaten access to care.

Finally, Wyoming's public lands, natural resources, and outdoor heritage are central to our economy and way of life. We must protect these assets while ensuring they continue to benefit future generations. My focus will be practical, conservative solutions that strengthen Wyoming families and communities.
column6:
Healthcare costs and access are among the most important issues facing Wyoming families. As a practicing surgeon, I see these challenges every day. If elected, I would be the only physician serving in the Wyoming Legislature, bringing firsthand experience caring for patients and recruiting healthcare professionals to our state.

We must strengthen Wyoming's healthcare workforce by supporting the recruitment and retention of physicians, nurses, and other providers, especially in rural communities. I also support continued investment in programs such as the Rural Health Transformation Program, which helps preserve local access to care and better coordinate services across Wyoming.

To improve affordability, we should encourage greater competition and innovation in the health insurance market, increase transparency, and reduce unnecessary administrative costs. Wyoming families deserve affordable coverage and access to high-quality care close to home.
column7:
Wyoming's public lands are one of our state's greatest assets. They support hunting, fishing, recreation, grazing, energy production, tourism, and our way of life. I do not support broad efforts to sell or transfer public lands out of public ownership. Once public access is lost, it is often impossible to restore.

As someone who enjoys Wyoming's great outdoors, I understand firsthand the importance of protecting access for future generations. Public lands belong to the people of Wyoming and should be managed in their long-term interests, not for short-term gain.

I support a balanced, multiple-use approach that protects public access while allowing responsible recreation, grazing, conservation, and resource development. My priority will be ensuring Wyoming's public lands continue to benefit the people who live, work, hunt, and recreate here.
column8:
Property taxes have become a major concern for many Wyoming families, especially seniors and those living on fixed incomes. No one should be forced to sell or lose a home they spent a lifetime paying for simply because property values have increased faster than their income.
I support property tax relief for homeowners while recognizing that property tax revenue is necessary to fund essential services such as law enforcement, roads, fire protection, and infrastructure. Wyoming's counties have different economies and revenue sources, therefore, a one-size-fits-all approach may not work.
As a Senator, I would work to balance taxpayer relief with funding of necessary local services. We must find opportunities to improve government efficiency, reduce spending and unnecessary burdens on property owners while preserving the services that Wyoming communities rely upon. Wyoming residents must be able to keep their homes and we must maintain strong local communities.
column9:
The Wyoming Legislature should focus on creating the conditions for economic success, not picking winners and losers. That starts with maintaining a stable tax and regulatory environment that allows small businesses to grow and create jobs.

We must also invest in the infrastructure that growing communities need, including housing, transportation, energy, and broadband. Economic growth depends on our ability to attract and retain both employers and workers.

Workforce development is equally important. Wyoming should continue expanding educational pathways, apprenticeships, technical training, and professional opportunities that help young people stay in Wyoming or return after completing their education.

Finally, we should remain open to emerging industries and new technologies while thoughtfully evaluating their long-term impacts. The goal should be sustainable growth that creates opportunity, strengthens communities, and preserves Wyoming's quality of life for future generations.
column10:
Citizens have a right to support candidates and causes they believe in. I believe voters have a right to know where campaign funding comes from. Transparency is key to public trust in the electoral process.
Wyoming requires candidates to disclose contributions and expenditures. Voters can review that information before casting their vote. I believe transparency is more important than creating restrictions that could discourage citizen participation in the political process.
If reforms are considered, they should focus on making campaign finance information easier for the public to access, while preserving the rights of individuals to support the candidates they believe will best serve Wyoming.
Ultimately, I believe elected officials should be accountable to the voters they represent. The best safeguard against undue influence is an informed electorate and a political process that allows citizens to evaluate candidates based on both their positions and their actions.

column2: I live in Laramie County, near Cheyenne. I've lived in what is now HD43 for almost 16 years, and in Laramie County since 2002. I was born in Chicago.
column4: As the incumbent representative for House District 43, I serve on the Corporations & Revenue Committees. During my time in office, I have sponsored or cosponsored 86 bills, 17 of which are now law. Before serving in the Legislature, I spent much of my adult life in retail banking, working in banks & credit unions. I worked my way up to executive management, retiring as Executive Vice President of a local credit union, with a strong background in personal finance, budgeting, lending, compliance, human resources, & financial analysis. I was a loan officer for many years & have experience in real estate, commercial, & consumer lending. I have a BS in business management with a minor in accounting. I've led local financial seminars & hosted two radio shows in Cheyenne to help people understand money management. I understand the financial pressures Wyoming families & small businesses face, & I believe government should budget and spend responsibly, transparently, & accountably.
column5:
Wyoming’s biggest challenge is keeping government limited, transparent, and accountable to the people who pay the bills. State government must control spending, root out waste, and limit taxpayer dollars to necessary functions. Families are being squeezed by rising costs, including property taxes, and government must not make that worse.

We also face serious challenges in education, election integrity, energy, and growth. Economic development should benefit citizens, not special interests, and Wyoming must protect its energy economy, water, and private property.

Government spending, contracts, records, and court decisions should be open to the public. I oppose government using NDAs and believe the judicial branch needs far greater transparency.

Wyoming must do better for children. Parents must remain central in education, test scores must improve, and we must protect children, born and unborn.

I don’t work for the government. I work for you.
column6:
Wyoming currently spends almost a billion dollars annually on healthcare through Medicaid, CHIP, behavioral health, aging services, and other programs. The question is not simply whether government can spend more, but whether those dollars produce better access, lower costs, and better outcomes.

The Legislature should focus on transparency, competition, and accountability. Patients must be able to understand prices before care. We should reduce barriers, support rural providers, expand telehealth where appropriate, and audit state programs for waste and duplication.

We must protect vulnerable citizens, including children, seniors, people with disabilities, and those who truly cannot provide for themselves. But we must do that in a way that preserves personal responsibility, protects taxpayers, and does not build a larger, less accountable system.

Healthcare policy should be judged by results: Can Wyoming families get care? Can they afford it? Are taxpayers getting value?
column7:
I do not support backroom deals or blanket efforts to sell off Wyoming’s public lands. Government at all levels must be transparent in all land sales, transfers, exchanges, and management decisions. That means public notice, full disclosure of appraisals and terms, and input from affected citizens.

Wyoming land should be managed for Wyoming people, not federal bureaucrats, political insiders, or special interests. That means protecting public access, private property rights, grazing, energy and mineral development, wildlife, water, recreation, and the long-term value of state trust lands.

The Legislature should require clear records, open meetings, accessible maps, disclosed negotiations, and accountability for any state land transaction. Citizens should know who benefits, what the land is worth, what access is gained or lost, and whether the deal serves the public interest.

Good land management starts with sunshine. Wyoming people deserve to see the facts before decisions are made.
column8:
Property tax relief must start with spending discipline. Counties are not all alike, but taxpayers are being asked to fund government growth faster than their income has grown. Paying rising property taxes over a lifetime is unsustainable.

Laramie County is not poor. Its FY2025 budget showed more than $292 million in total available revenue, including years of substantial cash carryforward. At the same time, major data center development could add many millions to the local tax base. Since data centers are industrial in use, they should be taxed at the industrial rate, not treated as ordinary commercial property nor receive any tax breaks of any kind.

As a member of the Revenue Committee, I helped craft the 25% residential property tax reduction & will continue looking for responsible ways to provide more relief. Local revenue needs matter, but government must go on a spending diet before asking homeowners for more. We do not have a revenue problem as much as a spending problem.
column9:
The Legislature’s role in economic development must be limited, transparent, and focused on core infrastructure, not picking winners and losers. Government should provide a fair tax and regulatory climate, roads, water, power, broadband, and public safety, then let private businesses compete.

The Wyoming Business Council needs real oversight and evaluation. It is a state agency, yet uses corporate language like “CEO,” manages grants, loans, & investments to non-Wyomingites, and has not proven taxpayers are getting results. Economic development must not be welfare for the well-connected.

I supported Speaker Neiman’s approach to move unspent Business Ready Community money into county consensus grants for capital projects, maintenance, & public infrastructure. That is a better model: local agreement and public projects.

Before spending more, the Legislature should require full reporting, ROI analysis, & a hard look at what should be kept, transferred, privatized, or eliminated.
column10:
Campaign donations must be transparent, lawful, and easy for citizens to review. I do not believe candidates should be controlled by PACs, lobbyists, or out-of-state interests. Voters deserve to know who is funding campaigns and whether elected officials represent citizens or donors.

The data I reviewed shows 4,516 PAC-related contributions from mostly unions, over 16 years, totaling $2,170,461.88, with an average contribution of $480.62. Based on contributor location, at least $1.84 million came from non-Wyoming PACs, not counting one $50,000 RGA entry with no city/state listed. Governor candidate records totaled 238 contributions and $593,931.63, averaging $2,495.51.

Wyoming’s system needs reform. Reports must be easier to search, download, and compare. PACs rarely support conservative candidates, and voters should be able to see patterns in PAC giving, independent expenditures, amendments, donor locations, and cumulative totals.

column2:
I live in the North Cheyenne area, in my current house for 26 years. 
My parents brought me here at the tender age of 4- 69 years living in Wyoming almost makes me a native.
Boise City, OK
column4:
I am a retired general contractor who helped build southeast Wyoming, with projects in Laramie, Pine Bluffs, Wheatland, and Cheyenne. After attending Colorado State University for one year in 1973, I left deeply disappointed by what I saw as socialist teaching and became even more committed to free-market principles.

My years as a general contractor taught me how to stretch a dollar, meet a payroll, and survive the boom-and-bust cycles Wyoming has endured. Those lessons shaped my approach to public service. As a legislator, I understand that government should spend wisely and responsibly. I do not see taxpayer dollars as someone else's money - I see them as hard-earned dollars entrusted to us by the people of Wyoming.

I am proud to stand with the Wyoming Freedom Caucus. I am a Christian, committed to the Constitution, happily married to Christine, 5 children, and 9 grandchildren. and firmly believe in the right to life. My values are simple: God, Country, and Family.
column5: Responsible growth, an excellent education for all of our children, and the protection of our  Constitutional Rights.
column6: I believe that one's own health is one's own responsibility, and many of the decisions we make in life are not healthy and not necessarily the best for us. We need to change the structure of the health insurance industry.  Health insurance should offer catastrophic insurance coverage, and a runny nose should be paid for by the patient. I have yet to find a doctor who will not accept immediate payment with a substantial discount.
column7: I am adamantly against the sale or transfer of state lands.  The legislature does not have much control over land sales; all land sales are supposed to be conducted through the auction process according to the state Constitution. I am concerned about land sales that do not benefit the citizens of Wyoming.
column8:
Local needs need to be locally controlled.  The legislature has added a grant office to help local governments take advantage of available grants.  There are other ways that local governments should be able to examine and manage local needs.  This question is complex in that the balance of local control and revenue is generally upset with legislative interference.  

For example, Niobrara County most recent financial statements (Comparative Statement of Activities 2025) show that overall revenue decreased $512 thousand dollars.  However, the decrease was from reductions in grant funding.  Property tax revenue actually increased because of increased mineral production.   

Many counties found that lowered property taxes actually stimulated other economic activity.  Some counties are thriving in the current environment.  Some have been feeling a pinch.  In each case, the decisions at the local level are key.
column9: It is not the legislature's role to pick winners and losers.  Economic development that would affect the entire state is beyond the Wyoming Business Council's capability.  It is within the Governor's office and the legislature.  For instance, universal choice in education would attract new citizens and new industry to Wyoming.  Education quality will also bring new economic opportunities.  This is especially true in higher education.  The new president of UW should develop areas of excellence to attract economic development and work closely with the governor in these efforts.  My parents moved to Wyoming so that I could attend better schools.  The quality of education is far more important than simply spending more dollars.  In both of the last two sessions, I have voted for higher teacher pay.  We need that to translate to better opportunities for students.
column10: I have voted for transparency at every opportunity.  The systems in place for reporting campaign finance in the Secretary of State's office are adequate.  There needs to be better reporting and controls for lobbying.  There is also a greater need for candidate disclosures in Wyoming.  I have seen instances where conflicts of interest are disregarded.  The voters would be better served with greater transparency.

column2: I live in Johnson County Wyoming with my wife and two children. Like over one-half of the current Wyoming residents I was born in another state, in my case Virginia, but I moved here first as a young man and again permanently over five years ago.
column4: I am a retired big game and small game biologist who spent three decades managing game populations. For the last six years, I have worked as a National Director and Expert for Gun Owners of America protecting American's Right to Keep and Bear Arms. I have B.S. and M.S. degrees and over 30 years of experience in governmental policy. I am a strong believer in the United States and Wyoming Constitutions and in the right of all people to be left alone by an out-of-control government.
column5: Some of the biggest challenges facing Wyoming include: young people leaving Wyoming for other states, maintaining enough water for current and future needs, federal overreach into every aspect of our lives, and threats against our core economic engines of coal, oil, gas, other mining, agriculture, etc. As a State Legislator I would work to reduce taxes to keep Wyoming affordable and fight regulations that stifle free enterprise and all businesses.
column6: We need to remove unnecessary regulations that make healthcare more expensive. Many people forget that Obamacare changed American healthcare in catastrophic ways, and the state needs to work to get back to a system that allowed doctors and companies to operate without the excessive government rules and costs. I had a doctor friend who retired rather than spend millions to comply with the excessive regulations forced upon doctors by Obamacare.
column7: I do not support efforts to sell public lands. I do believe federal lands need to be managed better - especially in terms of forest management which has been neglected for decades. I would work for state actions that could facilitate better cooperation between the state and federal government to ensure Wyoming residents benefited from public lands for hunting, fishing, hiking, camping, and other recreation. I would also work towards land swaps when those were beneficial for both the private landowner and the public. These would not be forced upon anyone, but there are cases where both the private landowner and the pubic could benefit from trading lands.
column8: I would work to cut waste, fraud, and abuse to ensure our state workers can be compensated fairly and that we can fund essential services like roads, bridges, police, fire, and rescue.
column9: The government should not pick winners and losers in business. It is wrong to use taxpayers money to support industry X while ignoring industry Y. We need the Wyoming legislature to remove unnecessary taxes and regulations on all businesses to allow those businesses to thrive on their own. For example, I have a close friend with a large company in Buffalo, and he would hire several high-paying positions immediately if not for unnecessary state restrictions on his business.
column10: I ran in 2024 and raised 98% of my money from Wyoming donors. My opponent, the current incumbent, took tens of thousands of dollars in Political Action Committee (PAC) money. My 2024 records prove that I stand with the citizens rather than with the power-brokers.

column2: I currently live in Casper, Wyoming. I am proud to have been raised on a small farm outside of Buffalo. Wyoming has always been home. After graduating from Buffalo High School, I left the state twice to pursue my education, but like many Wyomingites, I came back because this is where I wanted to raise my family. Wyoming’s values of hard work, personal responsibility, family, and community shaped who I am, and protecting those values is why I chose to serve.
column4: I hold a doctorate in educational leadership, am a mother of two college students, and am the current State Representative for House District 57. I was raised with Wyoming values — work hard, live within your means, help your neighbor, and leave things better than you found them.
