My professional career has been dedicated to education, serving as a reading teacher and now as a curriculum director working to improve outcomes, solve problems, and support the communities I serve. As a legislator, I believe government works best when it governs closest to the people. My campaign was built on a simple idea: give the voice back to the people. Through my HD57 survey initiative, I ask constituents where they stand on important issues, publicly share the results, and use that information to guide my votes. Representation should be focused on the people we serve.
column5: Wyoming’s greatest challenge is protecting what makes our state special while preparing responsibly for the future. We must address rising costs, property taxes, workforce needs, healthcare access, and economic stability, as well as the immediate challenge of water resource management, all without losing our Wyoming values. I believe solutions come from listening to the people closest to the issues — not assuming government always has the answer. Wyoming has incredible opportunities: strong communities, natural resources, innovation, and hardworking people. As a legislator, my focus is on supporting responsible budgets, protecting individual freedoms, maintaining local control, and creating opportunities so that future generations can continue to call Wyoming home.
column6: Access to affordable healthcare is a significant concern facing Wyoming families. In a rural state, we need practical solutions that improve access while recognizing that every community has different needs. The Legislature should continue to look for ways to reduce barriers, support rural healthcare providers, increase transparency, strengthen the healthcare workforce, and encourage innovation, such as tele-health. Healthcare decisions impact families, seniors, businesses, and taxpayers, and their voices need to be included. My commitment remains the same — listen to the people of House District 57, review the information, and work toward solutions that serve my community.
column7: I do not support the selling of Wyoming’s public lands. They support livelihoods, agriculture, energy industries, wildlife, hunting, recreation, and way of life. Decisions about land management must prioritize the people who live, work, and raise families here. I believe local voices should be at the center of conversations about land management because Wyoming people understand Wyoming land. It’s crucial that we protect these lands for future generations and ensure they remain accessible for everyone.
column8: Property taxes impact families across Wyoming, especially those on fixed incomes and those concerned about being priced out of their homes. At the same time, we must recognize that local governments rely on revenue to provide essential services. The challenge is finding responsible solutions that provide meaningful relief while understanding the impacts on our communities. My role as a representative is not to push my own agenda but to represent the people of House District 57. Through my survey initiative, I gather input from constituents and use it to guide my votes on issues such as property tax reform.
column9: I believe the government’s role is not to create winners and losers but to create an environment where Wyoming businesses, workers, and communities can succeed. Wyoming’s future depends on honoring our traditional industries while also being open to new opportunities. Economic growth should happen in a way that protects Wyoming values, supports local communities, and keeps decision-making close to the people.
column10: When I accept PAC donations, I always make it clear that my votes are dedicated to the people of House District 57. I believe that transparency and accountability are fundamental in public service. Voters have the right to know who supports candidates and how campaigns are financed. Campaign finance systems should be clear, transparent, and accessible to the public. Any reforms should aim to enhance trust while ensuring that everyday Wyoming citizens can still participate in the electoral process.

column2: 680 Road 11, Powell, Wyoming. Since 1989, 73 years, Greybull, Wyoming
column4:
Born in Greybull, Wyoming, Atrtended Basin Schools, 1971 Rocky Mountain College, Billings, MT, Graduated in 1975 With a Double major, 
Married in August 1975 to Caroline Sanborne, We have two children.
1975-1982 Hired for first teaching job. Dubois Fremont County School District #2
Taught Math 7-12, Coached Football, Girls Basketball, Track, Middle School Wrestling, 
Athletic Director (5 years for 2 hours a day, the rest was for teaching)
Municipal Judge for 3 years Dubois
1982-2006 Hired by Park County School District #1 Powell, Wyoming
High School Math instructor, Coached Football, and Wrestling
In 1989 received my Masters in Curriculum and Instruction from the University of Wyoming.
2006-2018 Hired by Park County School District #1 as the Curriculum Coordinator
2018 – 2026 Retired 
2019 – 2022 Senator for District 19 (Our District)
2023 – Appointed to the State Board of Education
column5: Biggest challenges: Adequate workers for jobs, Affordable housing, Public Land, Education: Addressing them: survey needs for jobs and educate for them, Finding ways for citizens to afford reasonable housing, Do not allow the sale of public land, Continue to be innovative and move towards the Profile of a Graduate which was developed by surveys to citizens of the state for input. The profile addresses those qualities.
column6: Affordable healthcare comes with more insurance companies in the state so their is competition. And hospitals getting more pay for Medicare and Medicaid. Currently getting 45 to 60 cents on the dollar for reimbursement isn't adequate. Certainly less coverage in the hospitals is not the answer.  Accessible starts with finding ways to keep hospitals above the profit line and not continuing to fall below it. We want good doctors but we complain about the cost. Which way do we go. Good Doctors or cheaper costs?
column7: I do not support the sale of public lands and will do everything in my power to protect our public lands for our use and enjoyment. We have to be diligent in our efforts to manage and oversee those maintaining the lands.
column8: First of all, I am opposed to the 50% reduction in property tax. We are the second lowest property taxed state in the United States. Property tax goes to the citizens of the counties and the municipalities and education. Our students are our future so cutting education doesn't make sense. Property tax has not increased because the government has raised it but because people coming in are willing to pay more money for land and homes than what we have paid and they see that as a good deal. It seems they just don't want to pay the tax. We don't either but we are not sad when we see our homes valued at 3 or 4 times what we paid for them. Why is it the responsibility of the state to pay for something we are not willing to pay for or for us to be cutting the condition of our roads and other needs in the county and the municipalities. It is a situation where we either pay for the property tax to cover costs or we accept the loss of those quality of living things the property tax provides.
column9: Incentives to bring in businesses in is a must and yes, economic development should have a role in our Legislature. It never hurts to evaluate a program and look for changes that would enhance the success of economic development. The state needs to consider paying a little so there is a bigger gain in the end. We don't have the transportation and ability to transport people and materials in and out. if we don't find ways to accommodate businesses why should they come. Cheyenne is growing leaps and bounds and because they have I80 and I25 passing through plus a large airport close by. the rest of us can't say that and then we expect businesses to just want to be here. What incentive is there to get them here. Economic development will allow for better options for the businesses to feel welcomed to the state. Without economic development we will continue to just get by.
column10: Yes, Wyoming needs a few reforms but, campaign donations should be at the top. The amount coming in from who knows where and the size of those donations clearly leaves the middle class citizens who would consider running out of the picture. Then it becomes a rich mans government and only addresses their challenges in our state.

column2: I currently live in Laramie, where I have lived continuously since August 2006. I first moved to Wyoming with my family in 1985, and was away for graduate school and work from 1997 to 2006. In total, I have lived in Wyoming for about 31 years. I was born in Ann Arbor, Michigan.
column4:
I grew up in Casper, graduated from Natrona County High School, and attended the University of Wyoming as a Trustees’ Scholar. At UW, I received a BA in International Studies, captained the Cowboy Debate Team, and completed an MS in Chemical Engineering. I earned a PhD in Chemical Engineering and an MS in Applied Physics from the University of Washington.

Heather and I returned to Laramie in 2006 to raise our four kids. I am a faculty member in the UW Honors College where I have taught since 2007. My career has crossed engineering, advanced and emerging technologies, diplomacy, higher education and public policy, including service as an AAAS S&T Diplomacy Fellow at the U.S. Department of State.

I have represented Senate District 9 since 2011, including 12 years as Senate Minority Leader. I am the ranking member of the Education and Minerals Committees and chair the Select Committee on Blockchain and Financial Technology.
column5:
Wyoming’s biggest challenge is that our economy and tax structure too often push away the people we most need to keep, including our young people, skilled workers, entrepreneurs and families building their futures. At the same time, we attract too many people drawn by low taxes and low public expectations, who then resist the schools, healthcare, infrastructure and community investments that make a state thrive. That weakens Wyoming.

Our opportunity is to reverse that cycle and build communities where Wyoming kids can stay, work, start businesses and raise families. That means strong public schools, high-quality healthcare people can afford, starter homes, reliable infrastructure, outdoor access, personal freedom, and a government that supports people without trying to control their lives. I will keep working for a Wyoming that protects personal freedom, invests in opportunity, and gives our kids real reasons to build their futures here.
column6:
Wyoming is failing on healthcare, and we need to admit it and change. First, Wyoming must expand Medicaid. We are one of only 10 states that still have not, leaving roughly 19,000 Wyomingites without coverage while rejecting well over $1 billion in federal support that could strengthen families, providers and rural hospitals. Refusing expansion has shifted costs to hospitals, counties, employers and families.

Wyoming is a small-population, rural state, and private insurance alone will not solve our problems. We need stronger rural healthcare, including hospitals, clinics, EMS, mental health, obstetric care, developmental disability support, long-term care and telehealth. We also need more providers.

The Legislature should protect healthcare choice, not interfere with it. Personal medical decisions should be made by patients in consultation with doctors and providers, not dictated by insurance companies, second-guessed by politicians or controlled by the state.
column7:
I strongly oppose selling, transferring or privatizing public lands in Wyoming. These lands belong to the people, not billionaires, private interests or politicians looking for budget fixes. Some leaders, including in Wyoming, continue looking for ways to sell off or transfer our public lands. We have to fight those efforts directly.

Public lands are part of Wyoming’s identity, economy and way of life. They support hunting, fishing, grazing, tourism, wildlife, clean water, outdoor recreation and the freedom to access the places that make Wyoming special. State and federal lands should be managed for multiple use, long-term stewardship and public access. I support practical land exchanges, like Pilot Hill, when they clearly increase public benefit by protecting or expanding public access.

These lands are not inventory to liquidate. They are an inheritance. Our responsibility is to manage them wisely and pass them on to our children, grandchildren and their grandchildren.
column8:
Property taxes have risen because assessed valuations have increased dramatically, creating pressure for many Wyoming homeowners. But property taxes are essential. They fund K-12 education, community colleges, counties, municipalities, special districts, hospitals and other important local services. Cutting property taxes always means cutting important services.

Wyoming already has significant relief programs for homeowners, seniors and people in need. We should improve those tools while looking at fair ways to pay for them, including additional taxes on high-value second homes and other properties that can better absorb the cost.

The proposed 50% residential property tax initiative on the November ballot would be devastating for Wyoming. It would not solve the real problem. It would undermine the everyday services that hardworking people across our state rely on. We need to defeat it and then focus on modernizing and diversifying Wyoming’s revenue structure.
column9:
I support a strong, positive role for the Legislature in economic development, but that role should not be picking winners and losers or simply writing checks to favored industries. Our job is to build the conditions where Wyoming people, communities, entrepreneurs, workers, and businesses can succeed.

That means investing in the foundations of a strong economy, including schools, workforce training, housing, healthcare, childcare, broadband, transportation, energy infrastructure and quality communities. It also means helping where help is needed and getting out of the way where government creates barriers that serve no public purpose.

I support the work and role of the Wyoming Business Council, Laramie Main Street and the Laramie Chamber Business Alliance. The Legislature should empower organizations like these to carry out their missions, build local capacity, support entrepreneurship and create real opportunities for Wyoming people.
column10:
I have never taken a single dollar of PAC money. That is intentional. Wyoming’s campaign finance system, like the national system, is legalized and structured corruption. It favors wealthy donors, PACs and special interests over citizens, and then pretends no harm is done unless someone proves a quid pro quo.

Disclosure is fine, but it does not fix the problem. If wealthy interests can spend unlimited money to dominate elections, disclosure does not make it fair. It only shows who bought influence after the damage is done.

Wyoming’s election integrity is exceptional. The corruption is not voter fraud. It is campaign finance. We focus on the wrong problem when we attack honest elections while ignoring the money shaping them. Voters should watch who finances whom. Outside groups cloaking themselves in words like freedom, liberty or progress should be judged by who funds them and what they really want. Our government should belong to Wyoming people. We need to take our state back.

column2: I have lived in Jackson since 2015. I currently live on the west side of town in a condo I purchased in 2018. I was born in Atlanta, Georgia.
column4: I am a software engineer and small business owner, and I have had the honor of representing the most of the Town of Jackson in the Wyoming House since 2019, currently serving as House Minority Leader. I earned a computer science degree from Carnegie Mellon and spent a decade writing software before running for office.
column5:
Polarization has kept us from working on Wyoming. It has kept us from ensuring communities can thrive. It has threatened our access to the outdoors. And it has brought the vitriol from DC to Wyoming.

As Minority Leader I work across the aisle, because supporting Wyoming's communities has never been a partisan project.
column6: The single most effective thing we could do is still pass Medicaid Expansion. Even after HR1, it would still reduce much of our uncompensated care which essentially gets passed on to all other payers.
column7: No.
column8:
The public passed "Amendment A" to allow us to tax primary homes differently from other properties. Instead of implementing the option the citizens of Wyoming gave us, we've made an endless confusing shotgun approach to relief.

We need to clean up all of these exemptions and properly tax primary residences differently from other property, while keeping those most vulnerable to property taxes in their homes with programs like the refund program.
column9: If we truly believe in thriving communities and ensuring our kids can grow up and start a business or find a great career, we will need to be part of making that happen.
column10: Yes. The people should know who spends money on our elections.

column2:
I was born in Monticello, Utah
We have lived in Wyoming for 46 years and,
We have lived at our current address for 31 years
column4:
My wife Rebecca and I have been married for 47 years come August, with six children, all graduating from a university, five from the University of Wyoming.
We have 23 grandchildren. Only 2 of the five children live in Wyoming.
My brother, father and I owned a construction company serving the petroleum industry for 35 years, starting with 3 employees and growing to 100.  
All  the afore mentioned background prepared me well to serve in the legislature.  I am in my 14th year of service to the citizens of House District #54 and the State of Wyoming.
During my time in the legislature I served 8 years on the Appropriations Committee, four years on the Minerals Committee, four years on the Labor & Health Committee, 10 years on Tribal Relations, two years on the Transportation Committee and as well several select committees and task forces.
column5:
1. Healthcare, including mental health and emergency medical services is the biggest challenge we have.   

2. Over the past 4-years we have seen a significant erosion for private property rights.  People are now subject to the will of neighboring land owners when make  a decision on how they want to use their property or who they can sell their property to.

3. We must find additional funding for our highway system. What was an annual shortfall of $400-million dollars 10-years ago has now grown to $600-million annually.  We are not able to build the new roads needed nor replace bridges that are nearing the end of their life cycle.  We are merely trying to maintain existing highway infrastructure.
column6:
To begin with we need to understand some of the data. For example Wyoming ranks 4th in the number of hospitals per 1,000 people with 29 hospitals in the state in 2024, and it ranks 8th in the nation for the same time period for the number of staffed beds available at 1875.  These numbers are much better than the states around us.  We rank as the 14th highest state for medical cost per capita.   If you average out the state we have one hospital for every 18,000 residents, and Colorado on the other hand has 96,000 residents supporting each hospital.
Wyoming with its large geographic area and small population struggles to have a hospital close to every community and the low number of people supporting the hospital means they don't have the patient volume to keep prices down, and because the prices are higher residents often leave the state to have elective surgeries further exasperating the problem.
When you look and mental health the problem become even more complex.
column7: We have always had the ability through a variety of ways to sell public lands or transfer lands through an exchange process. I think the objective should be to make sure there is a public benefit in the transaction.  A case in point was the sale of the Kelly parcel in Teton County to the federal government.  People in Wyoming supported the disposal of this irreplaceable parcel to the National Park Service feeling the process was very transparent and the financial benefit to our K-12 schools far exceeded the benefit received by leasing the property for grazing.  I believe there is an argument to be made to not reduce the amount of public lands and not increase the amount of public lands.  The citizens of the state have listed the protection of public lands as a top priority.  I am committed to protecting public lands, and making sure the public is a net benefactor of any sale or trade.
column8:
The legislature has responded to the public's desire for property tax relief by passing seven bills (2023 - 1, 2024-3, and 2025-3) in an attempt to provide relief.  We need to give the legislation some time to see if the bills produce the intended results.  It takes a little time for the medication to take affect, and we need to avoid over-prescribing which could have adverse impact on the entities that rely of property tax to provide services the people want including K-12 funding, county government, special districts i.e. hospital, cemetery, fire, and ambulance.
The next step is up to the voters.  If they vote for the constitutional amendment to reduce personal property tax by 50%, they will in effect be acknowledging that reduction will require a reduction of services in their county and they are saying with that vote they are willing to accept that reduction in services moving forward and should not expect the legislature to backfill the lost revenue with State General Funds.
column9:
Wyoming is in competition with every other state in the nation when recruiting business or industry to our state.  Without a central agency  like the Wyoming Business Council to field relocation questions and direct them to the appropriate city, town, or county, it is left up to the relocating business or industry to determine which city, town, or county they should reach out to. And perhaps even more important is the help provided to business and industry regarding our permitting process and assistance to navigate the requirements of our state agencies. No one is required to use the Business Council, but most everyone does.  
I also think the "Energy Matching Funds" have been incredibly successful in attracting business and industries that are associated with our core industries. The creation of the energy dominance fund is also critical and will contribute to Wyoming's long term stability.
column10: Historically Wyoming has been a model of public participation in the election process.  People whether individuals or businesses can support the candidate of their choice in a variety of ways including financial support.  Financial contributions are recorded in the Secretary of State's office and is available to the public.  I would like to eliminate the PAC money originating in Wyoming or other states that are not required to identify who the contributors are and operate independent of any candidate.  I think this kind of money has planted seeds of contempt in the campaign process and stifled the practice of mutual respect among opposing candidates.

column2: I live in Cheyenne, I have lived here for 51 years. I was born in Colorado.
column4: My background is in Criminal Justice. I have worked with juveniles and adults.
column5: What I find to be a challenge to Wyoming and it's citizens is the lack of available mental health care and the lack of access to all healthcare. As a legislator I would address these situations by pointing out how much this lack of care costs the state both financially and in human costs.
column6: I realize that Medicare expansion is not popular in Wyoming as most Wyomingites don't want the federal government involved any more than necessary, however, what is the value of the health of Wyoming citizens? We need to take another look at Medicare expansion in Wyoming.
column7: Sell or transfer it to whom?
column8: I would love to pay less in taxes myself, but again, at what cost? The money has to come from somewhere and it all boils down to the same pocket, which is the pocket of the taxpayer. I really think it is a circular discussion, six on one hand, half a dozen on the other hand.
column9: Economic development is clearly an important issue, especially considering the previous question. Instead of limiting higher education and spouting off about indoctrination, Wyoming needs to work toward integrating continuing education, both University and Trade Schools as the norm in our state. Without a critically thinking, educated populous, we have no chance at success in economic development.
column10: This is my first campaign, and a very broke one at that. I don't know how campaigns that receive large amounts of funds are monitored. I believe in being held accountable for the funding that I do have, and it is simply fiscally responsible and ethical to be transparent with donor dollars. Constituents should know where their donations are being applied.

column2:
I currently live in Thermopolis, where my family and I have lived since 2020. I was born in Montana and have spent my entire life in the West. I first came to Wyoming while working in the mining industry in 2011, and over the years have lived and worked in several Wyoming communities.
My military service and work as a government contractor took me overseas for extended periods, but Wyoming is the state where I have lived the longest. My wife, Natalya, and I chose to make Thermopolis our home, where our daughters attended school and where I am an attorney, and currently serve on the Hot Springs County School Board member, and as the municipal attorney for both Thermopolis and Shoshoni.
column4:
I am a Navy veteran, engineer, attorney, small rancher, small business owner, and member of the Hot Springs County School Board. I served as a Navy SEAL with SEAL Team Three and later returned to the Navy Reserves, retiring in 2025 after additional deployments.
After earning a degree in Geological Engineering from Montana Tech, I worked in mining and engineering throughout the West, including Wyoming. I later earned my law degree and currently practice law, representing local municipalities. My wife, Natalya, and I live in Thermopolis, where our two daughters attended school.

With a wide range of professional experiences in military service, mining, engineering, law, agriculture, and local government, I have firsthand insight into the opportunities and challenges facing Wyoming families, businesses, schools, and communities.
column5:
Wyoming's biggest challenges include maintaining essential services, attracting and retaining a skilled workforce, expanding access to housing, diversifying our economy, and addressing long-term fiscal pressures facing local governments. At the same time, Wyoming has tremendous opportunities through our mineral and agricultural resources, outdoor recreation, and strong communities. 
As a legislator, I will support policies that encourage economic growth, strengthen education, and help young people build careers in Wyoming. We must also address housing shortages that make it difficult for workers, retirees, young families, teachers, healthcare professionals, and first responders to live and work in our communities. Finally, we must be responsible stewards of taxpayer dollars while ensuring local governments have the flexibility and resources needed to serve their citizens. My focus will be on practical solutions that support working families and preserve Wyoming's quality of life.
column6:
Access to affordable healthcare is a major challenge, especially in Wyoming's rural communities. We need to strengthen and retain our healthcare workforce by supporting training programs, expanding opportunities for healthcare professionals to live and work in Wyoming, and reducing barriers that make it difficult to recruit providers to rural areas (such as housing).

The Legislature can also support responsible use of telehealth, improve access to mental health services, and work to preserve local hospitals, clinics, and emergency medical services that are essential to rural communities. We should look for ways to increase competition and transparency in healthcare pricing so patients can make informed decisions and costs can be better controlled.

There is no single solution, but by focusing on workforce development, rural access, innovation, and affordability, we can help ensure Wyoming families receive the care they need close to home and at a price they can afford.
column7:
I do not support the wholesale sale or transfer of public lands. Wyoming's public lands are a vital part of our heritage and provide opportunities for hunting, fishing, recreation, grazing, mineral development, and tourism. These public lands are a part of Wyoming culture and belong to the people. They should should remain accessible for future generations and for all Wyoming citizens- not the just the billionaires who will end up with it if it's sold. 

I support responsible multiple-use management that balances conservation, recreation, agriculture, and mineral production. As a legislator, I would advocate for greater input from local communities and stakeholders, encourage active land management to reduce wildfire risk, create more grazing opportunities, and improve land health. All of this is only possible if the people who live, work, and recreate on these lands are actually part of the decision making process.
column8:
Families across Wyoming are feeling the effects of rising costs, and the government should be just as mindful of spending taxpayer dollars. The Legislature has already provided significant property tax relief through the recent 25% residential property tax reduction and other existing exemptions. 
I do not support the proposed ballot initiative for an additional 50% cut to residential property taxes. Local governments, schools, hospitals, fire districts, libraries, and others depend on property tax revenue. Such a large reduction could have serious unintended consequences for local services and infrastructure. Wyoming already has the third lowest tax burden in the Nation. 
As a legislator, I would assess the effects of the current cuts and focus on responsible budgeting, spending, and preserving the property tax relief already enacted, while ensuring communities get the resources they need. I believe local governments should have a strong voice in decisions that directly affect them.
column9:
The Legislature has an important role in economic development. The government cannot create prosperity, but it can support the infrastructure, workforce development, and business climate that allows communities to succeed.
I believe Wyoming should continue to support economic development efforts, including organizations such as the Wyoming Business Council, while holding them accountable for results and ensuring taxpayer dollars are used effectively. Programs should be regularly evaluated and improved. Wyoming should be actively recruiting more industry- especially around mining, manufacturing, and water conservation technology companies that will benefit our rural communities. Cheyenne is way too far removed from the people of Wyoming- some of these offices need to be regional. 
My approach is to support practical, accountable economic development that strengthens local communities and respects local decision-making rather than imposing one-size-fits-all solutions from Cheyenne.
column10:
I believe campaign contributions are a form of political participation, but transparency is essential. Voters have a right to know who is supporting candidates and how campaign funds are being spent. I am committed to complying with Wyoming's campaign finance laws and to providing timely and accurate disclosure of contributions and expenditures.
Wyoming's campaign finance system is generally built around transparency, and that principle should remain the foundation of any reforms. I am open to improvements that make reporting easier to understand, increase public confidence, and ensure the rules are applied fairly and consistently. Any changes should balance transparency and accountability with the ability of citizens to participate in the political process.

column2: I live in Kemmerer. I have lived in Kemmerer for the past 53 years. Born and raised in Wyoming 64 years.  Born in Rawlins
column4: I have served 32 years in the Wyoming Court System as elected Clerk of the District Court, where I have worked to uphold the integrity of the Judicial system and ensured accountability and fairness under the law. Currently a member of the Wyoming Game and Fish Commission being president from 2022-2023. Prior served 8 years on the Wyoming Board of Parol, the last two being chairman. Served on the state committee for Lincoln Bicentennial.
column5: The State's economy depends on oil, coal, and natural gas to generate significant tax revenue, but consistent long-term market changes and price swings make them too susceptible to outside influence that we cannot control.  Economic diversification is a key ingredient to bring some balance; we need to build new industries utilizing Wyoming's pre-existing mines and current industry as anchors.  Advanced manufacturing and Technology working hand and hand with our businesses is a great opportunity. Create tax incentives for businesses that bring better paying jobs.
column6: As a legislator I would champion Wyoming's heath care system. Recruit physicians, nurses, mental health providers. Keeping our local hospitals in business and clinics assessable is a must. Paying for the medical services in rural areas is an obligation and I believe a big part of living in our great state. We need to expand primary care, increase price transparency increase insurance competition and expand telehealth care. Let's not forget our great EMS professionals we need to recruit new personnel and train them adding incentives.
column7: I would never support any selling or transferring public land in Wyoming. Public land is Public. Everyone that lives in Wyoming enjoys outdoor recreation and our magnificent wildlife.  We are entrusted to protect and preserve or public land for future generations and wildlife.
column8: In Wyoming, property taxes do not mainly fund the state government, they fund local communities. They pay for county roads, sheriff's offices fire districts, libraries, emergency services, senior centers, weed and pest districts, and many of the everyday services people rely on without even thinking about them. Everyone loves cutting taxes myself included. Tax relief should focus on our seniors and fixed income citizens first and foremost. Economic Diversification and keeping our younger population in the state would help the government short falls and create revenue. Cutting taxes and laying the burden on the local governments does NOT solve any problems it just creates new ones.
column9: The legislature should help economic development not hinder it.  Let the Business council, Wyoming Business Alliance, city chambers and other economic development organizations lead the charge, they are the professionals who know what is best for the state. As a legislator I would listen carefully and make conscious and thoughtful decisions on funding.
column10: I see no problem at this time as long as we maintain full transparency and we monitor it closely, if needed we can create new legislation.

column2: I’m a lifelong Wyomingite born right here in Rock Springs, raised in Uinta County, and settled in Sweetwater County for the past twelve years. My family’s roots have been in this soil for generations.
column4: I’m a working-class Wyomingite, blue collar manual labor. I’ve spent years showing up for democracy as an election judge spending nights counting your ballots, traveling to other cities to be a delegate for Wyoming Democrats, phone banking, door knocking, and calling state and local officials who answered me with copy and paste form that amounted little more then silence. I follow the news, pay my bills, and still believe the process belongs to us. I've grown tired of watching decisions made about us, I've realized the people who live the laws should help write them. I’m not a career politician, just a Wyomingite who’s tired of seeing our voices left outside the Capitol doors. I’m stepping up so working wyomingites, the ranch hands, miners, teachers, cashiers, all have a seat at the table where their futures are written. If you've juggled bills, swung a hammer, or waited tables; you’re as qualified as any lobbyist to shape Wyoming’s next chapter for the better.
column5: The biggest and broadest issue facing Wyoming as a whole is our dependence on the boom busted revenue. If mineral prices crash, severance tax vanishes and we slash schools, healthcare, and childcare leaving a barren system for growing families and an economic environment that can't support our graduates. I’d support enacting a 5% corporate profits tax on large out of state C-corps (currently its zero). Not raising taxes on the people struggling but on large corporations that use our labor and resources and help us reduce reliance on sales tax. The goal is to modernize Wyoming’s tax code so it supports diversified, long term growth, in addition to our extraction sector. Create a Stay & Thrive package with that revenue that would target critical pillars, such as universal broadband, health care, child care, education and public services. With these vital investments and fair corporate contribution, we can build the vibrant, supportive communities necessary to keep our young people home.
column6: Healthcare costs are suffocating because our boom bust budget leaves hospitals under funded and workers under paid. Wyoming is one of ten states still refusing a corporate income tax, so we can’t build clinics, forgive rural nurse loans, or expand Medicaid wrap around funds that keep small hospitals solvent. I’ll fight for a 5 % profits levy on large out-of-state C-corps; the healthcare slice helps fund: loan forgiveness for providers who stay in frontier towns, subsidizes telehealth so ranch families see a specialist without a 300 mile drive, and caps out of pocket costs on chronic care prescriptions. No Wyomingite should delay care to avoid debt. I drive to Salt Lake regularly, burning leave time and motel money; I want to stay home, visit a local doctor, and sleep in my own bed. With fair corporate revenue we can keep hospitals open and paychecks here instead of exporting patients and their dollars across state lines.
column7: As a legislator I support public lands remaining in the public domain; they belong to every Wyomingite, and once they’re gone, they’re gone for good. I would fight to ensure public lands serve Wyomingites by supporting revenue sharing from recreation leases and resource extraction that goes directly to local needs and infrastructure, not just the state, so the local community can use the money where it needs it without begging Cheyenne. State and federal contracts should favor local hiring, so when the BLM needs a fence built, a Rock Springs welder gets the paycheck, not a Colorado corporation. I’ll also push to create citizen oversight boards in every county, made of the people who live there, ranchers, hunters, tribal members, hikers, with real veto power over major land use decisions; if it doesn’t work for the people who ride, hunt, ranch, or pray on that ground, it doesn’t happen.
column8: Property tax relief sounds great until small towns can’t keep their lights on and high schools are talking about cutting sports. I won’t support blanket cuts or hikes that slam fixed income neighbors. Instead, let’s keep owner occupied rates flat, give counties a menu: modest voter-approved increases on second homes, and a 3 % vacancy tax on empty second houses and shuttered storefronts that sit idle season after season. Every dollar from those measures and from a cut of a 5 % corporate profits levy on large C-corps flows back into the community. Let the communities decide how to use the funds, save athletic programs, keep the clinic open, fix the roadways. One size never fits a state where assessed values swing from Jackson condos to small single family homes; local control plus fair corporate and vacancy contributions keeps Wyoming’s promise of low taxes without stripping the services that let communities thrive.
column9: The Wyoming Legislature should absolutely act as a facilitator in economic development. We must diversify beyond volatile severance taxes by developing knowledge intensive sectors. Focus on digital technology, embrace energy diversification, and agricultural technology that solve problems for resource industries while building standalone capabilities supporting us through boom bust cycles. The goal isn’t moving away from extracting resources but adding industries that don’t depend on the commodity cycles. The Legislature's role should help transition Wyoming from a resource dependent economy to one that also retains graduates and competes nationally in other sectors, keeping our youth here.. When we broaden our economic base, we strengthen every community from coal towns to agricultural centers ensuring Wyoming thrives no matter what markets do elsewhere.
column10: I value elections where ideas beat bank accounts, not the biggest wallet. Wyoming should cap campaign spending based on registered voter counts for legislative races and set firm limits for statewide offices. Require every PAC or nonprofit to disclose the real, living donors, no more shell game front groups. Provide all qualified candidates with digital ad credits and broadcast airtime so ranch hands, teachers, and miners can run without courting millionaires. Public office should go to the person who best shares our values, not the one with the richest donors. By limiting overall spending, forcing full disclosure, and giving everyday candidates an equal microphone, we ensure every voter hears every voice. That keeps elections in the hands of neighbors, not out of state special interests, and restores trust that your representative is working for you, not for the loudest checkbook.

column2: Gillette 48 yrs. Born in Michigan
column4: Live in Gillette 48 of my 55 years. I’ve worked in the coal industry for 30 yrs. 20 of that in management responsible for large work force and large budgets. Served in this community in various volunteer roles and have owned several businesses in the past and currently have a business that my wife manages.
column5: Biggest challenges/opportunities in Wyo is responsible economic development. A lot of big projects, data centers processing plants want to build in Wyo. I am for diversification but it needs to make economic sense and protections need to be in place for our communities.
column6: There is only one health care insurance provider in Wyoming. We need to work at getting more options for the people of Wyoming. Free market competition should  lower prices.
column7: No. Public needs to stay public. We need to make sure the federal and state agencies in Wyoming are held accountable and make sure they are staffed properly to do the job.
column8: We need to revisit the SF69 of 2025 that targeted a reduction of 50% on the increase between 2020-2024 which was a time period of very in inflation on home Assessments. The blanket approach does work. Voters in Wy don’t only want but desire strategic and permanent tax relief while still protecting essential services.
column9: Providing attractive infrastructure. Set up regulations and policies that hold companies accountable.
column10: I think it is adequate.

column2: Wheatland Wy all my life, all my life, Wheatland
column4: I am born and raised in Wheatland I worked as a mechanic and welder for 12 years after that I became a pastor at Impact Ministries. I served on a number of different boards through the years and I have now served house district 4 for the last 6 years.
column5: One of the biggest challenges today in Wyoming is diversity of economy and keeping our young people in the state. CTE is the backbone of Wyoming's workforce. We need to inform our youth of the job opportunities in the state and how they can make a living in these feilds. We also need to keep tax structure beneficial for industry so that we can keep businesses in our state.
column6: Health care has to become a private market again. Obama care has killed health care and made it finically unattainable for many people. We dont need more govornment trying to fix what the government broke in the first place. Also we need to adress the insurance/PBM issue for pharmaceuticals .
column7: No I am not for selling public lands. I feel that we have had a transfer system in place for many years that has worked and we don't need to allow it to open up any more. We need to as a state make sure that we don't allow property sale or transfer to be used for the finical gain of a select group of people.
column8: I agree every county is different, As i have been in this discussion of property tax I feel that it is time to consider the sales tax increase and elimination of property tax on residential. this might not be possible but needs to be looked at further. it is important to understand that we cant give tax relief to some and not all that is unconstitutional.
column9: We need to assure that the regulatory framework for the state is favorable to economic development. We also need to make sure that the infrastructure of our communities is capable of supporting development.
column10: I only take donations from people. I have not taken pac money and so feel that is the best way to stay transparent.

column2: I live on the family farm west of Lingle. I have lived here my entire life (46 years). I was born in Scottsbluff, Nebraska.
column4:
I am a lifelong Goshen County resident and third-generation family farmer from Lingle. 
I graduated from Lingle-Fort Laramie High School and then attended EWC before earning my undergraduate and law degrees from the University of Wyoming. 
I currently practice law in Torrington while actively working on my family’s cattle feeding and farming operation near Lingle. My hands-on experience as an agricultural producer and attorney gives him practical, firsthand knowledge of the challenges facing Goshen County families, including private property rights, water rights, taxes, and regulatory burdens.
I served for ten years on the Lingle-Fort Laramie Conservation District Board, and I am an active member of the Torrington Rotary Club. I have been married for 22 years to my wife Caren. We have three daughters, Natalie, Charlotte, and Lydia. I am a strong supporter of local youth activities and regularly attend my daughters’ sporting events.
column5:
The two greatest opportunities before Wyoming today are our people and our resources.
The world is hungry for the energy, agricultural products, and natural resources Wyoming produces, positioning our state to power the economy of tomorrow. At the same time, we are blessed with a talented, energetic generation of young people.
As a legislator, I will champion policies that retain our youth in Wyoming. I support strong education and career training, expanded trade and technical programs, and a pro-growth regulatory environment that allows businesses to thrive and create opportunity for our families.
By keeping our kids in Wyoming and responsibly developing our resources, we will build a stronger, more prosperous future.
column6: In our vast rural state, too many families face high prices, long travel distances, and shortages of doctors and specialists. As a legislator, I will work to make care more affordable and accessible by expanding telemedicine so Wyomingites can access providers even in rural communities, strengthening our healthcare workforce through targeted incentives to attract and retain doctors, nurses, and specialists, and combating high costs by requiring clear pricing and eliminating unnecessary regulations that drive up costs.
column7:
I do not support the wholesale sale of Wyoming's public lands to private interests. These lands belong to the people and must serve the needs of the people. I support a multiple use strategy including energy development, agricultural use, recreation, and hunting.
As a legislator, I will support transfer of management authority over suitable federal lands from Washington to the State of Wyoming. Our state has a proven track record of responsible stewardship and local control resulting in decisions that reflect the needs and values of Wyomingites rather than national environmental agendas.
column8: The government has constitutional obligations to the public. Taxes should never be more than is necessary to meet these obligations. As a legislator, I will work closely with our local leaders, special districts, municipalities, and school districts to ensure that tax revenues collected are narrowly tailored to meet our mandated obligations.
column9:
The Wyoming Legislature's role in economic development is to create an environment where businesses, entrepreneurs, and families can thrive without picking winners and losers with taxpayer subsidies. Government's job is to get out of the way. We should focus on fair taxes, reasonable regulations, quality infrastructure, and a skilled workforce. As a legislator, I will support regulatory reform, workforce education and development, responsible resource development, pro-growth tax policies, and strategic infrastructure investment.
True economic prosperity comes from free enterprise and innovation, not government programs.
column10: When it comes to campaign donations, transparency, integrity, and respect for free speech is important. I welcome support from individuals, businesses, and organizations who share my commitment to high moral values, fair tax policy, and responsible resource development. Additionally, voters have a right to know who supports a candidate. Therefore, I am committed to prompt and full disclosure of every donation I receive.

column2: Gillette, 20 years, 20 years.
column4: I have been representing the hard working people of Gillette for three terms.  I’m a Christian and veteran naval officer. I married my college sweetheart Sage. We raised two children in Gillette and our daughter lives here with her family.  My family and friends are the primary reason I wish to serve the people of Campbell County.
column5: Today the families in House District 31 are facing inflation that is cutting into their budget everyday.  I have advanced real and lasting tax relief for the people of Wyoming and I will continue to do that as well as striving to ensure that state and local governments do not add to the cost of achieving the American dream.
column6: The One Big Beautiful Bill will provide nearly $1 billion to Wyoming for Rural Health Transformation.  I authored the bill determining how this money will be spent.  Wyoming must take advantage of this unprecidented opportunity and expend these funds wisely to increase access and the sutainability of our healthcare industry.
column7: I have no interest in selling public lands.  If Wyoming residents want to have the public lands managed for their best interests, then the government closest to the people of Wyoming should be the government to manage those lands.  The recent Rock Springs Resource Management Plan(RMP) proved that the people in New York and California have no business dictating how our lands are utilized.  That federal control will lead to zero human access if we continue to allow the federal government to prioritize so called environmentalism above hunting, fishing, camping, and mineral utilization.  I believe the state should take all lands that are public and that our constitution should require that public access be maintained at or above current levels.
column8: I would ensure, as I already have through my work as chairman of the House Appropriations Committee, that local governments have access to steady streams of revenue other than residential property taxes.  I led the way in redistributing sales tax, already being collected for the state, to local governments.  This is exactly what good conservative governance looks like.
column9: The state should first and foremost create a business freindly environment by having the lowest tax  burden and the least restrictive regulatory burden.  The Wyoming Business Counsel should be the chief advertising and promoting arm of the state when it comes to developing new business relationships.  The infrastructure needed to support new economic devleopment should be streamlined so that local governents can effect change as quickly as they desire to see new businesses come to their communities.
column10: I believe that the current system honors both transparency for those who wish to see who is funding whom and the first amendment rights of individuals who wish to participate in this representative government.

column2: I live in Cheyenne, and have lived here since 1979, except for a short stint away to earn my bachelor's degree.  I was born in Columbus, Georgia, while my father was stationed in the US Army.
column4: I am a retired attorney that still serves my community as a lawyer.  I served as an administrator in the Child Support Enforcement, SNAP and TANF programs for the Wyoming Department of Family Services for several years.  I most recently represented debtors in the National Debt Relief program.  I'm active with St. Paul’s Lutheran Church in Cheyenne as the choir director and member of the Gifts Committee and Foundation Board.  I served for 16 years as an elected Trustee to the Laramie County Community College Board of Trustees.   I serve on the Board of Directors for My Front Door, a local housing program.  I also act and direct for the community theatre and play saxophone for local band ensembles.  I'm married to John and enjoy a blended family of four children and six grandchildren.  I want to bring real Wyoming values and people into the leadership of the state.  I strongly support public education, public land and water, and the vision of adequate and affordable health care for all.
column5: I asked Wyoming voters on my Facebook page what they saw as the three top issues facing Wyoming's people.  The top three responses were Protecting Public Education, Affordable and Accessible Healthcare, and Affordable and Accessible Housing.  The cost of living and protecting our public lands and water were also named multiple times.  I would address protecting public education by making the constitutionally mandated funding a top priority.  We need to elect legislators that value education.  I come from a family of teachers and school administrators, and I served on the LCCC Board of Trustees for 16 years.  I saw the value of education for individuals and communities.  Medical bills was the #1 reason my clients faced financial crisis.  I would address a Wyoming solution to the ACA enhanced premium tax cuts, and continue to explore Medicaid expansion.  I would address affordable and accessible housing by proposing property tax breaks to new home buyers.
column6: First, we need to explore a Wyoming solution to the federal cuts to the Affordable Care Act's enhanced premium tax cut.  We also need to continue to look at Medicaid Expansion and the services Wyoming Medicaid will cover.  I want to work with hospitals, doctors and insurers to discover solutions to these issues.  No one should go into financial crisis because they got sick. I would like to find out more about Wyoming becoming self-insured for healthcare so that insurance companies do not need to purchase secondary insurance, such as the programs in Florida and New Mexico. This could reduce the cost of healthcare.
column7: I do not support efforts to sell or transfer public land in Wyoming.  Our public lands are valuable to our health and culture.  I met a young couple who said one reason they moved to Wyoming was enjoyment of our public lands.  Tourism and outdoor recreation are the second-highest drivers of our economy.  Our public lands attract business development and talented workers.  Our lands help diversify our economy.  We must be respectful stewards of the gifts of our lands.  We need to guarantee that our State Lands and Cultural Resources agency stays adequately funded.  We also need to elect state officials to the State Lands and Investment Board (SLIB) that value our public lands and support their management in Wyoming people's best interest.
column8: I oppose a statewide elimination or 50% reduction in property taxes.  These cuts benefit the wealthy, high-value property owners in the state.  An owner of a $3 million-dollar home will benefit much more than an owner of a $300,000 home, and yet will expect the same services as good schools, police protection, trash removal, and maintained streets.  Our valuable public schools and reliable public services would suffer.  Reducing property taxes and increasing sales and other taxes is not the answer.  I want look at reductions for low-income households and for new home buyers.  We can use property tax cuts for those who need to buy a home and who need to remain in their home.
column9: I am a strong proponent of small business development.  The new small businesses I see in Cheyenne are started by folks who are much younger than me and want to build a future in Wyoming.  The Wyoming Legislature can reduce barriers to small business development and can create a culture of growth and vision for the future.  Our leadership can create a business-ready and welcoming environment.
column10: Every candidate must report campaign donations to the Wyoming Secretary of State.  The Secretary and staff receive these reports and should take action if irregular or illegal activities occur.  I cannot say at this point that Wyoming's system needs reform; but we need to elect a Secretary of State who will monitor campaign donations and take action against any illegal or corrupt action.

column2: Lived in Laramie for almost 12 years now and was born in Manhattan Kansas
column4: I have worked in the private sector in engineering for the majority of my life and believe I will bring a new perspective and common-sense approach to the house that I use in the private sector.
column5: Some of our biggest challenges facing our communities are the K-12 funding, the influx of big tech coming in and issues with our housing. I am looking to find other avenues of funding for K-12 and how we can uphold our constitutional duties. For the issues of big tech I want to see more transparency in how they intend on using infrastructure and will be good stewards of not just the infrastructure but the environment. As far as housing I would push for a common sense approach to a renters and home owners bill of rights.
column6: I believe we have a system that works currently; however, as a state, we need to make sure that healthcare is a priority. There is no reason that someone should be denied medical attention they need because of financial status, and in turn, they should not be discouraged from reaching out for this help just because of the costs that they will incur. As a state, we need to take care of the one's in need through programs that assist individuals in need.
column7: I do not, I don't see why giving up our greatest asset is a solution for a long-term problem. Not to mention land locking our public lands is something that should not be done since the public should have access to public lands. I will look at other streams of revenue that make sense over the long term and not just in the short term.
column8: I will be looking into how we can generate other forms of long-term revenue to offset any tax relief. This is a very delicate balance between fixed-income individuals and the need for property tax revenue.
column9: There are currently many avenues the state takes to boost economic development, some of which just need to have more transparency on the decisions the agencies make for the regions they will impact.
column10: I feel that as long as there are checks and balances, and donations are watched over and looked at there isn't an issue. We as a state just need to make sure there is transparency in all that we do when running for office, be it at the local to state levels.

column2: I live in Cheyenne.  I've lived in both Cheyenne and in Wyoming for 44 years.  I was born on F.E. Warren Air Force Base in Cheyenne
column4:
I'm a lifelong Wyoming resident, a volunteer firefighter, and dedicated public servant.
I have earned degrees from Laramie County Community College and the University of Wyoming.

My life has been built around service coming from a military family. As a firefighter of 24 years, I've answered calls when my neighbors needed help most. I served as a Department of Defense contract firefighter to support our troops during Operation Iraqi Freedom. My experience as a firefighter taught me the importance of teamwork and accountability. It shaped my commitment to serving others with integrity, courage, and common sense. My various positions in the fire service, currently as a battalion operations captain and command staff, have given me experience in leadership and making sound decisions under pressure.

I understand the importance of hard work, personal responsibility, and living within your means. My budgeting experience will guide me during fiscal conversations and budgetary matters.
column5:
One of the biggest challenges Wyoming faces is that both the State and its citizens continue to face increased costs that started several years ago. Wyoming families are paying more for groceries, housing, insurance, utilities, and everyday necessities. At the same time, state and local governments are dealing with rising costs to provide essential services. We can't simply spend our way through these challenges—we have a responsibility to prioritize needs over wants, eliminate waste wherever possible, and ensure every taxpayer dollar is used wisely. This may yield savings for tax payers.

One of the biggest opportunities for Wyoming is our economy.  Wyoming's energy, agriculture, mining, tourism, and small businesses are the backbone of our state. We should continue supporting these industries while creating an environment where entrepreneurs and new businesses can succeed. A strong economy means more good-paying jobs, a broader tax base, and greater financial stability for our state.
column6: Access to affordable healthcare is one of Wyoming's biggest challenges, especially in our rural communities. The Legislature should work to reduce healthcare deserts by encouraging a growth in heatlhcare systems and servicecs in underserved areas. We should also look for ways to remove unnecessary barriers that limit competition among healthcare providers while maintaining high standards of care. Increased competition can improve access, shorten wait times, and help control costs for patients. The same principle applies to health insurance—we should encourage a more competitive insurance market that gives Wyoming families and small businesses more affordable choices. Our goal should be a healthcare system that is accessible, sustainable, and focused on the needs of patients, regardless of where they live in Wyoming.
column7:
I do not support selling public land unless there is an incredibly valid reason to do so. 

Before considering any proposal to transfer ownership, we must carefully evaluate the financial impact, long-term management responsibilities, public access, and protections for future generations. Wyoming taxpayers deserve a full accounting of both the benefits and the risks before any major decision is made. As long as these concerns are adequately addressed and a transfer is beneficial to the taxpayer and the State, I would support transferring public land.

I support increasing Wyoming's voice in the management of our public lands and ensuring decisions are made with meaningful local input. Public lands are an important part of our heritage, economy, and way of life.
column8:
I believe property tax relief should be pursued in a way that is targeted, sustainable, and balanced with local government needs. Homeowners are feeling the pressure of rising property valuations and increasing tax bills.

At the same time, counties, cities, fire districts, school districts, and other special districts depend on property tax revenue to provide essential services like public safety, infrastructure, and education. If we reduce that funding without a plan, we simply shift the burden to those entities that rely on property taxes to fund services or force cuts that hurt communities.

The goal should not be to shift pressure from taxpayers to local governments, but to make the system more fair, predictable, and sustainable.  Reductions in property tax revenue should be paired with responsible and stable replacement funding.

I support property tax relief, but it has to be done thoughtfully, with respect for both taxpayers and the local services they rely on every day.
column9:
The Legislature should keep the state predictable, competitive, and business-friendly, while avoiding unnecessary regulatory or tax burdens that discourage investment.

At the same time, economic development in Wyoming cannot be “one-size-fits-all.” What works in one county may not work in others. That’s why local control and local expertise are important so communities have a strong voice in shaping their own economic future.

The Legislature can help by investing in infrastructure like roads and broadband that support both rural and urban growth.  It can support policies that attract and retain small businesses and entrepreneurs.  It can and should ensure taxpayer dollars used for economic development are transparent, accountable, and results-driven.

Ultimately, the Legislature’s role is to remove barriers, strengthen core industries, and create an environment where Wyoming businesses can succeed and new opportunities can grow.
column10:
Wyoming’s system for monitoring campaign donations and expenditures should be simple, transparent, and low-burden for both candidates and the public. Candidates should be able to comply with reporting requirements without unnecessary administrative hurdles, while voters should be able to easily access and understand where campaign funding comes from and how it is spent.

Transparency is essential to public trust in elections. A strong system is one that allows citizens to clearly see who is financially supporting campaigns and provides timely, accessible information without requiring extensive effort or expertise to navigate.
If reforms are needed, they should focus on usability, clarity, and accessibility of reporting—ensuring the system is both practical for candidates and meaningful for voters—while maintaining accountability and confidence in the integrity of Wyoming’s elections.

column2: My home is in Lander at 787 S 4th St. I have lived in this house all my life except when I was in college and a three-year period when I worked for the Illinois Commerce Commission. I was born in Lander.
column4:
I am an economist (PhD) with a long record of public service in the Wyoming Legislature. Except for the years I was working away or going to school, I have lived in Wyoming my whole life. I have had the great privilege of working around the world on public policy issues involving governance and democracy, natural resources and public utilities. 

With my son and his mom, I built and operate a successful business in Lander.  I have made a payroll for 30 years. 

I am proud to be a thoughtful and effective legislative leader where I have served on the Revenue, Corporations (Corporations, Elections and Political Subdivisions, and Tribal Committees, frequently as Chairman. I am known for being constitutionally minded, fair and thorough. 

I love Wyoming, especially her sturdy people, great outdoors and immense opportunities.
column5:
The most immediate threat to Wyoming has been this short-term turn to populism advanced by new proponents under the Republican banner.  Newer members of the Legislature, elected in a populist wave, have produced conflicting and often unconstitutional policy directions for Wyoming.  Witness the reckless property tax measures, the attacks on public education and our institutions of higher learning, and blatantly unconstitutional legislation surrounding women’s health.  

More than ever, steady experienced hands are needed that have respect for civility, process, separation of powers, and our constitutionally-defined legal framework.  An economist by training, I am a longtime successful legislator with a record of service and respect for our institutions of government.
column6:
Healthcare is important to everyone, but the working poor suffer the most. Expansion of health services for the working poor would make peoples' lives better and improve productivity overall while putting money back in our communities to support healthcare facilities for all.  I have been a tireless advocate for the expansion of Medicaid. In my own community I directly support the healthcare services needed for the poor.

Most of rural Wyoming is facing a crisis with respect to emergency medical services. The staggering cost of providing the safety net service including air transport has challenged traditional funding methods. More needs to be done but I did sponsor and pass legislation to allow for the creation of EMS districts. Hopefully the rural transformation funding will go a long way towards addressing these problems across the state.
column7:
No. Our public lands are a treasure that we need to maintain for future generations. Limited exchanges are needed on occasion in very specific circumstances. 

Our Office of State Lands does a pretty good job of managing our state lands. It’s not easy when you consider the competing interests of surface uses, minerals, recreational and open space. The five elected officials ultimately decide significant policy in this area.  

Probably the worst job we are doing is to not account for the cumulative impact of gigantic wind and solar development on our ecosystems and landscapes. 

We share a common interest with federal land managers and good relations are critical. Federal royalties are a significant source of state revenue.
column8:
The populist relief efforts have produced bad public policy and that has not made Wyoming better. Property taxes fund local governments and education - not state services.  These shortsighted efforts have increased local government funding disparity.  

Severing the connection between citizens and paying for those local government activities is myopic populist policy and terrible fiscal practice. Backfilling by the state is also a bad idea - everyone would vote away their taxes if they could get someone else to pay to fund their services.  

There is a need for thoughtful relief based on a person’s economic circumstance.  Inconsistent wealth is an ongoing issue with local government funding.  Property tax reductions can make the disparity worse.
column9:
Most recently government's role came to light in a populist driven attack on the Wyoming Business Council. 

Most of our communities lack facilities and expertise necessary to attract business interests. If low taxes were the complete answer, Wyoming would be overwhelmed with new economic activity.  We are facing a rapidly aging population, the slowest population growth in our region, and slower relative grown in GDP per capita.

Excepting minerals, most new business recruitment imposes more burden on government than they return in taxes.  Fueled by tax breaks and lax policies, data centers and renewable energy have implications for water, wildlife, landscapes and the reliability of our electrical system.  An inadequate siting process means that we are not fully considering the cumulative impacts of these super-industrial scale developments.

The states that are growing emphasize quality of life, robust essential services like education and healthcare, and amenities.
column10: I have never taken a PAC contribution for legislative races until this year. I made this decision reluctantly because the cost of races has skyrocketed and rational policy promoters are being outgunned by unaccountable third-party ideologues from in and out of state. I am a seasoned legislator that knows how to stand up to political pressure and look towards the long term for Wyoming, not the shallow populism comporting as conservative ideology.

column2: I was raised on a farm in Star Valley, Wyoming.  I currently live on a ranch north of Green River, Wyoming and have lived on this ranch for 32 years.
column4: I am a retired Oil and Gas Manager and a Chemical Engineer by trade.  I have served in the Wyoming Legislature since 2021.  I currently serve as House Majority Floor Leader, Chairman of the House Minerals, Economic Development & Banking Committee, and Co-chair of the Select Committee for School Recalibration.  I am a member of the Joint Legislature Management Council, Wyoming House Rules Committee, 14 State Energy Council Executive Committee, and University of Wyoming's Energy Resources Council Board of Directors
column5: Our minerals industry has lost 17% of their employees over the past several years as fossil fuels have been under attack.  Wyoming is rich in natural resources and we need to unleash the potential that Wyoming has to supply energy to the nation.  I have been working to bring a new coal-fired power plant to Wyoming to demonstrate the newest scientific and technological advances which allow coal to be used with very little emissions.  Through my efforts locally, and with the Trump administration, Wyoming could get 2 or 3 new coal-fired power plants in the near future.
column6: Our most vulnerable citizens are our Senior Citizens.  I have been an advocate for taking care of our aging population and providing support for them to remain in their homes as long as possible.  I have sponsored budget amendments to provide in-home support for seniors and to help them stay independent as long as possible.
column7: Public lands need to remain public.  In the past, Wyoming has not been involved in the public land decisions that impact our state.  I want Wyoming citizens to have more input into these decisions that impact us locally.  Jointly with the Federal Government, we be more effective managing our pubic lands, but we, as Wyoming, need a seat at the table
column8: Not until true conservatives gained leadership positions in the Legislature 2 years ago, did Wyoming taxpayers finally get property tax relief.  To make up for much of the lost revenue to local government entities, we increased the Direct Distribution funds by nearly 50% last session. In this manner, we were able to bring relief to the Wyoming taxpayer while still providing the necessary revenue for local services.
column9: Many of our communities need infrastructure repairs and improvements.  You cannot bring new businesses into a community if the wastewater treatment plant or potable water supply is insufficient.  Many towns need street improvements.  I have been working on a Consensus Block Grant Program for local communities to support infrastructure improvements and repairs.  With this program, local communities decide how to spend the grant money instead of a few bureaucrats in Cheyenne making those decisions
column10: I rarely accept money from Lobbyists, Political Action Committees or groups that are trying to influence policy.  Those donations usually have strings attached.  I believe the Wyoming Legislature needs to stay focused on the needs of the citizens, and not paid lobbyists.

column2: Teton County is home. I have owned a home there since 1986. Full time residence since 1980 born Klamath Falls OR
column4:
Husband, Dad, Business owner (1980) community volunteer,
Jackson planning commission 
Jackson Town Council
Teton County Commission 
Wyoming House
Wyoming Senate

column5: To me our challenges are the desire to have a way of life we love in a time when all is changing around us. To take advantage of opportunities and to do it on our terms. Thanks to years of hard work by our predecessors we have the financial capacity to do anything! Not Everything but anything.
column6: As our population ages this question is at the top of our list. No single answer but we are working this summer to drill down and move forward.Nothing should be off the table.
column7:
I have a record on this one and I can say the key is working with all parties. The beautiful thing is we still have the vast majority of our state in public hands. The energy industry, the Tourism industry, the Agriculture industry 
All have a critical role to play. Conservation and wildlife protection along with industry are not mutually exclusive. We should recommit ourselves to being the National leaders in this area.
column8:
Four years ago the number one issue for my constituents was tax reform. Today every homeowner has the ability to access programs to provide relief. Now we must keep watch and adjust where necessary to fund local governments, and all special districts. I do not believe the ballot measure on taxes is good idea. 
The State is listening and working to strike that balance
column9:
Today we as a State compete in a global market place. We must leverage our strengths whenever possible. Folks need to remember government working for economic development has been going on for a while. I would ask anyone who has an issue with that to ask if the railroad project had not been backed by the government would we be a state? 
How we allocated our resources in this field has been and should be questioned. It should not be defunded.
column10:
I have told every donor to me the same thing. “Your support is welcome but if you expect anything more than a thank you might be disappointed.” 
 That said a main focus for us should be pay legislators. We are at a crossroads here. To serve in the Legislature is a great honor. It also comes at a great cost. Too many of us can’t afford to serve. This lowers the pool of candidates. It also increases the chance of dark money moving into the process itself.
We saw it unfold just this past session. We as a state need to look at this very hard.

column2: I live in Wright, Wyoming and have been in Wright and Wyoming for 20 years. I grew up in Wells, Minnesota
column4: I have been an educator for 31 years with 20 being in the Campbell County School District.
column5:
As a legislator, I’ll tackle rural healthcare by backing smart, stable funding that keeps our local clinics and hospitals open without hitting property owners with higher taxes. We need to expand telehealth so folks in remote areas can connect right with specialists, and I’ll push for state matching funds to make that happen. I also want to grow our medical residency programs and offer student loan forgiveness to get doctors and nurses to put down roots in our rural counties.

When it comes to keeping our workforce strong and supporting our schools, I'll fight for predictable, inflation-adjusted funding so our districts can recruit and keep great teachers.
