I’ll also work to connect our K-12 schools, community colleges, and local businesses to build better technical and career training pipelines. This gives our young people a clear path to great Wyoming jobs right here at home.
column6:
To make healthcare more affordable, we have to cut through the red tape and look at practical solutions. First, we need to take a hard look at prescription drug costs. By forcing more transparency on pharmacy benefit managers, we can make sure discounts actually get passed down to folks at the pharmacy counter.

We should also give small businesses more flexibility to pool together and buy group insurance, which would drive down monthly premiums for working families. Finally, we need to cut the administrative paperwork cluttering up our rural clinics. When doctors spend less time filling out state forms, they can spend more time treating patients, which lowers overhead costs for everyone. By focusing on common-sense transparency, cutting waste, and boosting our local community health centers, we can keep high-quality care within reach without breaking the bank.
column7: When it comes to our public lands, I’m 100% against selling or transferring them to private interests. These spaces are a massive part of Wyoming's heritage, tourism, and outdoor lifestyle. To make sure they're managed in the best interest of everyone, the Legislature needs to foster strong, common-sense partnerships with local ranchers, conservationists, energy producers, and federal agencies. We need a balanced, multi-use approach that fully supports our traditional industries like agriculture and energy, while firmly protecting open access for hunting, fishing, and outdoor recreation. By keeping local Wyoming voices at the head of the table, we can protect our beautiful landscapes and support our economy for generations to come.
column8:
Balancing property tax relief with local revenue needs is all about finding a middle ground. We need to protect homeowners without cutting the legs out from under the vital services our counties rely on, like our local law enforcement, emergency services, and roads.

To do that, we should expand the state's property tax refund program so it gives immediate, meaningful help to seniors, veterans, and working families who are really feeling the squeeze right now. We also need to put a sensible cap on how much annual assessments can jump so people aren't blindsided by massive tax spikes. To make sure our communities don't lose out, the state should use its budget surpluses to backfill that revenue for the counties. This keeps our local emergency services and roads fully funded while giving everyday Wyoming residents some much-needed breathing room.
column9: The Legislature definitely has a big role to play in economic development, but it shouldn't be about the state picking winners and losers with your tax dollars. Instead, our job is to build a rock-solid foundation so local businesses can thrive and new industries actually want to set up shop here.
column10: I truly feel that the system we have in place right now works well. From our end on the campaign trail, my team and I make it a point to input all of our donation and expense information weekly. Keeping up with it regularly like that makes sure the reporting doesn't turn into some massive, daunting task right before the deadlines, and it keeps us completely accountable to the public in real-time. Because our current rules do a great job of keeping things transparent and are straightforward enough for candidates to manage responsibly, I don't see a need for major reforms right now.

column2: Powell, Wyoming, 34 years, New York
column4:
Living in Powell for 34 years, I am rooted in the community.
I have spent 20 years as Powell Mayor and City Council Representative. I also served as President of the Powell Chamber of Commerce. Those roles were working positions that put me at the table for real decisions affecting real people.

Beyond city government I have represented Powell on the Wyoming Association of Municipalities board, served on the Park County Travel Council, and aided citizens from the Yellowstone County Assistance Network board. I have been part of projects ranging from building homes with Habitat for Humanity to developing the city’s high speed internet infrastructure, bringing a gluten free oats processing facility to Powell, building the community aquatic center, and championing a Shoshone River boat ramp south of town.

What connects all of that is a simple philosophy: show up, do the work, and remember that the best outcomes happen when a community works together.
column5:
Wyoming faces challenges with an economy heavily dependent on mineral revenues that fluctuate, a workforce shortages and communities working hard to remain viable as demographics shift.

The opportunity is that Wyoming still has choices other states have already lost. We have no income tax, manageable debt, and industries — energy, agriculture, and tourism — that the rest of the nation depends on.

As a legislator I would focus on three priorities: local control — the people closest to a problem are best equipped to solve it, protecting Wyoming's public lands for the hunters, ranchers, recreationalists and energy producers who depend on them every day; and ensuring that the revenues generated in communities like Park County find their way back to those communities in the form of roads, water infrastructure, and services that make them places worth staying.

Wyoming's future is bright if we make smart decisions now. I intend to be part of making those decisions well.
column6:
When the nearest specialist is hours away and premiums consume a growing share of a family's income, that is a real problem with real consequences for working people.

The Legislature should focus on policies that increase competition and transparency in healthcare pricing, reduce administrative burdens on rural providers and critical access hospitals that are already operating on thin margins, and explore workforce incentives that attract and retain medical professionals in underserved rural areas.

I am also open to honest conversations about what other states have done to address rural access issues, evaluated on their actual outcomes rather than their political labels.

What I am not interested in is top-down mandates that add cost and bureaucracy without improving care. The goal is more providers, more options, and more affordability — and getting there requires listening to the healthcare professionals and patients in our own communities first.
column7:
I do not support the broad sale or transfer of Wyoming's public lands, and I say that as someone who has spent years championing outdoor recreation and public land access in Park County.

Public lands are part of Wyoming's identity and its economy. They support hunting, fishing, grazing, energy development, and tourism. Transferring those lands to private ownership or to underfunded state management would put access at risk.

I have real concerns about federal land management decisions that are made far from Wyoming by people who have never set foot here. The interest of Wyoming's people is not always reflected in federal agency priorities, and the Legislature should be an aggressive advocate for local input into how these lands are managed — grazing allotments, access roads, energy permitting, and wildfire management included.

The goal should be keeping public lands public while insisting that Wyoming's voice is heard loud and clear.
column8:
Property tax relief is a concern for many Wyoming families, particularly long-term residents on fixed incomes.

Local services — roads, emergency response, schools — depend on a revenue base that cannot be legislated away from Cheyenne without consequences. A one-size-fits-all property tax solution risks solving one county's problem while creating another's.

My approach would be to pursue targeted relief — protecting primary homeowners and long-term residents from runaway assessments — while preserving local governments' ability to fund the services their communities need. Programs that cap tax burden as a percentage of income, exemptions for primary residences, and phase-in protections for significant assessment increases are all tools worth examining.

I would resist legislative mandates that strip local communities of the revenue flexibility they need to govern themselves responsibly. Local control must mean something, and property tax policy is where that principle gets tested.
column9:
The Legislature's most valuable contribution is creating the conditions where private enterprise can take root and grow. That means keeping taxes low and predictable, maintaining a regulatory environment that doesn't punish investment, and building the infrastructure — roads, water systems, workforce pipelines through our community colleges — that allows businesses to succeed once they arrive.

The Legislature doesn't create jobs — but it absolutely creates the environment where job creators choose Wyoming over every other option on their list.
column10: I believe in full transparency. Voters deserve to know who is funding campaigns and in what amounts. Wyoming's current reporting system is functional, but I am open to reforms that improve timeliness and accessibility of disclosure data. What I am not interested in is a system that creates barriers for ordinary citizens and small donors while doing nothing to address the larger influence questions. Dark money is influencing Wyoming elections therefore, some regulation should be considered. Transparency and accessibility should go together.

column2: I’ve called Rock Springs home for the past 22 years and have lived in Wyoming for the same amount of time. I was born in Denver, Colorado.
column4:
Rock Springs has been my home for 22 years. It’s where my husband and I raised our son and built our life. As an entrepreneur, I’ve spent years building businesses, developing programs and serving my community.

Politics was often the discussion around my family’s kitchen table, and I volunteered on my first campaign at the age of 16. That interest in public service has stayed with me throughout my life, including serving as Chairman of the Sweetwater County Republican Party. Those experiences taught me about the issues facing our communities and the value of steady, accountable leadership.
column5:
Wyoming’s greatest strength is its people and the industries that built our towns and communities. Our challenge is protecting our way of life while planning wisely for what lies ahead.

In the Legislature, I will work to protect Wyoming’s energy and mineral industries, keep taxes low, hold government accountable, support the infrastructure and public safety our communities rely on, and defend local control. I believe government should do the basics well, spend taxpayer dollars responsibly, and stay focused on the issues that matter most to Wyoming families and businesses.
column6: Healthcare affordability and access are real challenges in Wyoming, but there is no single legislative solution. I believe the Legislature should work with healthcare providers and communities to identify practical solutions that improve access while keeping costs under control. That includes supporting rural providers and removing barriers that make it harder for Wyoming families to get the care they need.
column7: Wyoming’s public lands are central to our economy, outdoor heritage and way of life. I support responsible multiple-use management that balances energy and mineral development, grazing, recreation, wildlife and public access. Wyoming should have a strong voice in decisions affecting our public lands, and I support policies that allow responsible development while ensuring these lands continue to support jobs, local communities and future generations.
column8: As the discussion has shifted from property tax relief to property tax reform, we must recognize that taxpayer relief and local government funding are not competing goals. Wyoming families deserve a fair and sustainable tax system, and our communities deserve the ability to provide the essential services people rely on every day. Good stewardship requires us to pursue both at the same time.
column9: The Wyoming Legislature should focus on building a strong foundation for economic growth, not picking winners and losers. If we want the private sector to invest in Wyoming, we must be willing to invest in ourselves by maintaining and building the arterial infrastructure that makes growth possible, including roads, water, sewer and utilities. Combined with a stable, predictable business climate, those investments create the foundation for businesses to grow, create jobs and strengthen our communities.
column10: I believe Wyoming’s campaign finance laws should promote transparency and public trust while protecting the constitutional rights of citizens to participate in the political process and support the candidates and causes they believe in.

column2: I currently live in Casper, WY.  I have lived here nearly 6 years.  I was born in Truckee, CA which is near Lake Tahoe.
column4:
I am a husband, father, business person, and community volunteer.  While in business, I managed various roles from account servicing, compliance, and delivering incentive payments to internal and external business partners.  In this role, I worked with auditing staff from all levels of government.  I also managed staff in each of the roles I held.

I have also been involved in various community service efforts.  I was the head of a mens group within my church, which on an annual basis put on a fundraiser for a local food bank and participated in a program to provide winter coats to children in need.   I am volunteer National Ski Patroller up at Casper Mountain. Our role is to maintain safety and provide basic first aid and safe transport of injured skiers to next level care.
column5: The biggest challenges for Wyoming is reliance on mineral revenues which tend to fluctuate, the need to diversify economic opportunities, and spending at all levels of government.  I would start by making sure our regulatory statutes are not contributing to these challenges and we are promoting policy to encourage opportunities.  We need to closely watch spending to make sure essential services are the first to be funded without increasing taxes to support them.  And challenge government to look at different ways to provide services more efficiently and cost effective - ie. Internet Delivery, New Technologies.  I want to demonstrate to the public that government provides high level of service in the most efficient possible way.
column6:
The first effort is looking at the ability to deliver services.  I believe Telehealth is one option that can assist and we want to make sure there are no impediments in the use of this option. Another is looking into options to incentivize traveling nurses and other specialty providers that will encourage them to come provide services in Wyoming. 

Finally, we need to look at underserved areas and find ways to encourage healthcare delivery - perhaps tax incentives, targeting loan forgiveness, and promotion of rural medical training tracks.
column7: I don't support efforts to sell or transfer public land in Wyoming.
column8: I support the most recent legislative efforts of property tax relief as they have attempted to balance relief to the tax payer while providing funding for local revenue needs.  The legislature has offset cuts with backfill of other revenue.  I would look to make these measures more permanent which will help the state and local governments plan growth within a consistent framework.
column9: I think the Legislature should create an economic, regulatory and educational environment that would incentivize business to develop in Wyoming and encourage existing business to strongly consider moving operations and jobs to our state.  And I think there are many opportunities to do so.  I think the current method used has not shown solid results and it appears all involved realize change in this process is necessary.
column10: I believe in complete transparency when it comes to campaign donations.  I think the current Wyoming system works well with the exception of the timing.  I think the timing of disclosure be shown earlier than a week before in-person voting.

column2: I was born and raised in House District 11, and moved back six years ago to be near my parents and siblings. I've spent stretches outside Wyoming over the years for school, my career, and military service, but Wyoming has been home my whole life, and it's where I've chosen to raise my own family.
column4: I went to Deming, Miller, McCormick, Central High, and LCCC. I worked in the trades in my teens and 20s, then earned a bachelor's in business and a master's in engineering at the University of Colorado, with doctoral work in economics at the University of Hamburg. These days I direct the Wyoming Center for Business and Economic Analysis at LCCC, teach economics and public policy there, and serve as a space operations officer in the Air Force Reserve. Outside of that I'm a small business owner, I coach cross country at East High, and I help out with the Unaccompanied Student Initiative, the Children's Museum, and Rooted in Cheyenne. On both the civilian and military sides, the research I do and the decisions I make carry real consequences, and I'll bring the same discipline and expertise to my work in the legislature.
column5: One of the best parts of running for office is going door to door and learning what matters to voters in HD 11, and many of those issues aren't the ones making headlines. Cost of living, data centers, and property taxes come up, but so do sales taxes, a shrinking workforce, and an instance where someone listed a constituent's address on a registry with no proof required. From talking with neighbors and employers and digging into the research, I see real opportunities to lower costs, expand our workforce, and keep our kids safe. We can address the fees and rules that drive up the cost of housing. We can broaden the economy with new industries without relying on taxpayers to foot the bill. We can support a variety of educational pathways that build our future workforce. The biggest challenge is looking past the noise to find what truly matters. It starts with listening, because the most important issues to me are the ones HD 11 cares about.
column6: Healthcare is expensive here partly because demand keeps rising while supply stays thin. Wyoming is rural, providers are spread out, and there aren't many options. Much of what the Legislature can do is on the supply side, and we've made real progress: we repealed our Certificate of Need rules and joined the compacts that let out-of-state nurses and doctors practice here. The next steps are to extend that approach to more of the professions we're short on, expand telehealth so distance matters less, and grow our own providers through our community colleges. We can also require real price transparency, so families see what care costs before they're billed. None of this is a silver bullet, but more providers, more competition, and clearer prices move things in the right direction.
column7: No. I don't support selling off our public lands or a wholesale transfer to the state. Around ninety percent of Wyomingites use public land to hunt, fish, work, and get outside, and once it's sold it's gone for good. Last year Washington came close to forcing the sale of millions of acres across the West, and Wyoming voters showed up to say no. The Legislature was right to pass a resolution opposing it, and I'll hold that line. Federal law already has a careful process for the rare case where a specific parcel should change hands for a clear public purpose, with public input. That's where any disposal belongs, not in a budget bill or a sales quota. Where I'd push is management. Wyoming should have a stronger, earlier voice in how these lands are run under multiple use, so grazing, energy, recreation, and conservation keep coexisting.
column8: This is where my day job and homegrown knowledge of Laramie County both come in. The recent spike in tax bills wasn't a rate hike, it was inflation pushing up home values and assessments with them. Voters were right to want relief, and the state has now put real reductions in place, including help aimed at those who needed it most. We tend to picture one Wyoming economy, but no two counties are alike. Some lean on minerals, some on sales tax, some have very little to work with. So relief can't be one-size-fits-all, and it can't quietly shift the burden onto local businesses or the next town over. My approach is to protect homeowners without blowing a hole in the services people count on, and to hold the line on spending so we aren't forced to backfill with a shakier, more volatile tax. When revenue jumps just because prices went up, that windfall shouldn't automatically become more spending. Relief and discipline have to go together.
column9: The Legislature's job is to set the table, not pick the winners. We do the most good by keeping the climate competitive for everyone: a simple tax structure, sensible regulation, reliable power and water, and a workforce trained for the jobs we actually have. I'd rather lower the barriers for the hundred businesses already here than write a special deal for one. Where I'm cautious is bespoke giveaways, the lifetime tax credit or discounted land for a single company that our existing employers never get. Those distort the market and hand taxpayers the risk. The better model is the one we've already seen work in Cheyenne, where major investments come in and pay their own way instead of leaning on local taxpayers. The piece I care most about is workforce. I work at our community college, and matching real training to real jobs is the most durable economic development there is. Build the people, and the businesses follow.
column10: Voters deserve to know who's paying for and backing the campaigns asking for their vote. I'd rather run on what matters to HD 11 and what I actually believe and let voters see exactly where my support comes from. Sunlight does more good than complicated limits the well-connected always find a way around. On the system itself, the reports can be hard for an ordinary voter to find and make sense of. That's where I'd focus reform: keep existing rules but provide clearer, timelier, easier-to-search reporting, so the information is actually usable and not just technically filed. Transparency only works if people can see it. Give voters the facts and trust they're capable of weighing who stands behind a candidate.

column2: I have lived in the North-West side of Laramie County and been in Wyoming for about 15 years. I was born in Minnesota but moved to Colorado as a teenager where I spent most of my career.
column4:
My career has been in technology, computing facilities, construction and management. I started at 12 when I designed and built my first computer. I received my degree in computer science and began in a computing center. My "big picture" skills were recognized, and I proposed, designed and constructed larger and larger projects. I chaired a computing steering committee at a 1500+ employee company with multiple divisions and subsidiaries. I led teams to propose, design, construct and manage million-dollar technology projects with a focus in networking, computing and data centers. During that same period, I worked with city, county and state boards to create plans, construct and manage technology projects. I retired early from my career due to personal reasons. 

I bring a unique knowledge and perspective that can benefit the people and the state. I understand the challenges of issues from both sides and have strived to develop strategies where both parties can achieve their goals.
column5:
Responsible Business Development - As a community we need to understand the pros/cons of business development coming into the area and state. 

Promote Public Education/Healthcare - Without well supported education, healthcare and public support we will continue to see people leave the state for areas with better resources.

Retention of Public Lands and Funding Them - Let's face it, many of us have come to Wyoming to have better access to wilderness areas within the state. We need to save these lands and fund them.

Removal or Reduction of Property Taxes - As with many aspects of tax reduction, it all sounds good initially. But when you look at who it benefits, what stresses it puts on existing public systems and what would need to be done to keep the same level of services available to the public, the tax reduction can look not as bright and shiny.

The government should be a good steward to the public interests and balance economic growth with public functions and operations.
column6:
Like other states, Wyoming can initiate programs which help its citizens with healthcare without major increases in taxes. These programs require the different government segments to work together to develop a functioning and sustaining program. 

Healthcare along with education is of primary importance to the state to retain and expand its population and workforce.
column7:
No. Public lands are a promise to the people by the government to maintain this proud country. Wyoming has some of the most beautiful areas and wildlife in the country which is why many citizens have moved or stay in this state. The government should also provide and maintain adequate funding to these areas to retain the beauty and operation for future generations.

Selling or the transfer of public lands breaks this promise to the people.
column8:
This is one of my top issues. it is irresponsible of the government and people to cut property taxes without understanding the impacts to public services we enjoy.

Wyoming's property taxes are well below many states in comparison which already strains the local service systems. Cutting them even further without adequate backfill funding sources is a design to push people away and out of the state. 

The government's job is to help support its citizens and local services is a large function of that support. I personally would like fire and rescue, public education, police and the many other services to stay and even be enhanced in my community.
column9:
The government is the stewards of the public and the public pays for the government.
It's the government's responsibility to ensure the state stays viable by bringing in new businesses and diversifying the economy. 

History has taught us that having a single large business or businesses in the same sector can mean devastation if the business or business sector wanes or collapses. 

Economic development in many business sectors makes for a strong economy which helps all people and the community as a whole.
column10:
I believe with all the major issues needing to be addressed in the state, campaign reform could be addressed but its priority is low compared to them.
The state is dealing with issues which could heavily impact it and its citizens. These issues should take priority before campaign donations and expenditures are addressed.

Thank you for allowing me to voice my opinions and address these questions.
My desire is to represent the public and their interests as best as I can if I am elected to office.

column2: I currently live in beautiful West Side Laramie. My husband and I bought our home here in 2018 but I have lived in Laramie since 2014. I was born in Pueblo, Colorado.
column4:
It is an honor to currently be serving my third term. I serve on the Agriculture and Public Lands Committee and the Travel, Recreation, and Wildlife Committee. I have previously served on the Education and the Judiciary Committees. 

During my time in the legislature, I have passed laws protecting public land access and various bills aimed at government transparency and accountability. In 2024, after years of community organizing, I helped pass legislation that strengthened oversight of law enforcement in Wyoming. 

I am a nonprofit coach for organizations aiming to build grassroots power. I believe deeply that power is inherent in the people and I work to engage communities on how they can participate in their local governments. I earned my Ph.D. in Experimental Psychology at the University of Wyoming and I enjoy wildlife photography. You may have seen some of my recent work of Rebecca Bextel handing out checks to members of the Freedom Caucus on the Wyoming House Floor.
column5:
The greatest issues facing Wyoming is healthcare, the heartbreak of our young people leaving due to lack of opportunities, and out of state extremists funneling resources into Wyoming to divide us and dismantle our public institutions. 

The first two struggles can be solved together – funding programs in healthcare would create jobs for young people, support them in growing their families, and support our aging population. We need new programs that expand maternal healthcare, senior services, and rural healthcare solutions so the money Wyomingites put into healthcare is spent in here, not in bordering states. 

The issue of extremism plays a central role in our state legislature. This burden can be solved by the people in the upcoming elections. Outside of civic accountability through the ballot, I am committed to centering Wyoming problems and solutions, and I will continue working on anything that will improve the lives of the people of Wyoming so they have the freedom to prosper.
column6:
Given the recent cuts to healthcare at the federal level, the state should work towards providing insurance and healthcare services to the people of Wyoming beyond Medicaid. We need bold solutions that increase healthcare affordability for everyone while also supporting our critical access hospitals and the healthcare services that currently exist. A state-run healthcare system would provide jobs, increase the number of providers, reduce costs, and make it more accessible.

Short of creating a statewide healthcare system, we should create a public health trust fund that pays for healthcare services provided throughout the state from savings and investments. The state already has programs like this, such as the Wyoming Wildlife & Natural Resource Trust Fund which pays for conservation projects using income from investments. These funds could pay to keep our rural critical access hospitals and maternal services afloat while also helping increase services like community health clinics.
column7:
Hell no, I do not support efforts to sell off our public land. The idea that the federal government could transfer lands to the state for management is just a foot-in-the-door technique towards public land sales. The state does not have the resources to manage these lands and would ultimately sell most of them off. Once they’re gone, we will never get them back and our future generations will not have the same opportunities we do to enjoy wide open spaces and abundant wildlife.  

I support thoughtful, community-led efforts towards public land swaps, which means that a local community is able to trade property for public land which would increase public access.
 
In 2023, a bill I sponsored was passed which made it illegal for private property owners to post private property signs on public land and I have been the lead lawmaker towards protecting corner crossing. If re-elected, I intend to work towards a constitutional amendment towards no net loss of public lands in Wyoming.
column8:
The legislature has created a shell game where we have no plan for how to make up for lost revenues to pay for services, so some day the people of Wyoming will be forced to pay through increased sales tax or other means. No plan is a bad plan. Right now, the state has no plan for how to offset the cuts and no appetite for solving this issue. 

In 2024, the legislature addressed the spike in property values through the property tax refund program and the 4% assessment caps. Of course, we may need to bring a solution for how to make the 4% assessment cap constitutional if the courts find that it is not. I believe property tax cuts should only be available through means tests – where the people who need relief get it, and the billionaires who use Wyoming as a tax haven don’t.

The legislature also passed a constitutional amendment that allows for various tax rates for different types of properties which would allow us to increase property tax rates on data centers.
column9:
If the free market and low taxes were going to solve our economic diversification problem, it would have done so already. The state needs to help solve some of the problems that keep businesses from coming here – namely our housing shortage, unaffordable and inaccessible healthcare, and opportunities for young families such as childcare and preschool. 

Aside from structural changes to help businesses be able to support their workforce here, we also need to further support our economic development programs such as the Wyoming Business Council. With the current climate of Wyoming’s legislature being hellbent on tearing down the WBC, we are not going to be able to attract new business. The legislature should take a more supportive role towards supporting the people behind our small businesses and the programs on the ground that help them grow.
column10:
I believe that money in politics is corrupting our entire system. I don’t take AIPAC money and I never will. I prefer our campaign is funded by small donations from working class people who already share my values. 

When it comes to PAC money, my policy is that I will take a contribution and I will NOT hesitate to vote against their interests when they don’t align with my community’s values. This stance has meant that I can spend less time raising money and more time doing work on behalf of my community.  

Wyoming has a serious campaign funding problem – out of state interests have long viewed us as a cheap and easy place to influence. Wyoming should require more frequent reporting of campaign donations, especially since the reporting deadline is only a week before the election. We should mandate greater transparency in reporting for independent expenditures such as mailers that are used to sow division and spread lies. Wyoming elections should be Wyoming led and Wyoming funded.

column2: I live in Casper, WY.   I graduated from NCHS in '02.
column4: I am a community memeber that volunteers, a mother of 5, and current state legislator. I am a results-oriented leader who gets answers and is not afraid to ask the hard questions. I am a committed listener and seeker of practical solutions. I remain dedicated to listening closely to constituents and delivering the results Wyoming families deserve.
column5: Residents are facing numerous challenges, including rising costs for food, animal feed, fertilizers, gas, water, property taxes, and utility bills. Relief can come through reforming state spending to collect less in taxes, thereby easing the financial burden on Wyoming families. Water issues present another major challenge this season, with low levels affecting irrigation, tourism, livestock, and residential use. In our arid state, water is life, vital for our businesses and communities. Pray for moisture. Access to quality local medical care is another pressing concern. We lack sufficient providers and continue to lose specialized practitioners. The following section addresses this.
column6: Healthcare affordability is not just a budget issue it affects Wyoming families, businesses, and our ability to attract and retain residents. By focusing on core state responsibilities like  price transparency, rural infrastructure, workforce support, and prevention, the Legislature can deliver meaningful relief without undermining personal responsibility or market dynamics.
column7: Public lands in WY are vital to our WY way of life and tourism industry. I will work to ensure that public lands in WY are lawfully managed by the state in a way that supports healthy wildlife, and outdoor recreation. I am learning more about this topic and welcome resources to read from.
column8: This requires reform not just relief of property taxes. I am in suport of the state paying for education which would reduce all property tax bills, by 43 mills while protecting local budgets, and not shifting the tax burden on the tax payor in another format. The average property tax mill in WY is appox. 72 mills. Thus, this reform leaves money on your kitchen table while local budgets remain unchanged.
column9: The Wyoming state government needs to focus on Wyoming’s infrastructure to ensure economic development can occur. In Wyoming, our roads carry our economy, from agriculture, to oil and gas, to maintenancing those fields/wells/pipelines, minerals, and tourism. Secure, structurally sound water and sewer lines are essential to keeping utility rates affordable for Wyoming residents. When state government focuses on its core functions, we strengthen our ability to attract businesses from out of state. Another business inviting piece of WY that allows for economic growth is our low tax structure that is business firendly.
column10: I believe in campaign transparency. I believe the campaign donations need to be reported as recieved, and quarterly expenses reported.

column2: Currently reside: Sheridan, WY since June 2015. Born: Wenatchee, WA
column4:
I'm a small business owner, mother, and Sheridan neighbor who believes the best government is the smallest one, and the one closest to home. For over a decade, my husband Nate and I have built our life here including two small businesses and children who love Wyoming as much as we do. 

As owner of Whirly Girl Flowers, I've learned to listen first, work with what's available, and tend to the details that matter. My professional experience includes budgeting, bookkeeping, and hands-on operations for human services non-profits, practical skills suited to representing District 29.

I hold an AA from Pikes Peak Community College and a BS from Colorado State University, and I'm active locally with WY Vote Sheridan County, and the Sheridan County Chamber of Commerce.

I'm an independent Republican, registered since 1998, accountable only to my neighbors in Sheridan. I bring no fixed agenda, just a readiness to listen and serve.
column5:
Wyoming's biggest challenges are also its greatest opportunities: a revenue base tied to extractive industries, rising healthcare costs, and the pressure to grow without losing what makes Wyoming worth living in. The opportunity is that we get to thoughtfully choose what comes next, 

As a legislator, I'd focus on responsible economic diversification that builds on what's already rooted here. I'd work to remove regulatory burdens that stifle small business growth, support working families, and ensure that any new development fits who we are and serves Wyoming people, not outside interests.

The best decisions come from listening first. I don't arrive with a fixed agenda. I come prepared to problem-solve with the resources available, set honest priorities, and make hard calls without losing sight of what matters most to the families I'd represent. Wyoming's strength has always come from people who show up and do the job. I want to bring that same ethic to the legislature.
column6:
Every Wyoming family deserves access to honest, affordable care; whether you're self-employed, ranching an hour from the nearest clinic, or quietly struggling in ways that don't always show. As a small business owner without employer-sponsored insurance, this isn't abstract to me. I've navigated those costs firsthand.

The Legislature can help by removing barriers to recruiting and retaining qualified medical professionals, supporting rural access through telehealth and community health infrastructure, and examining licensing and regulatory barriers that limit provider options. 
Real solutions will be practical, locally informed, and sustainable, not ones that look good on paper but break under Wyoming conditions. I'll listen to providers, patients, and rural communities to find approaches that meet people where they are.
column7:
Wyoming's public lands are part of what makes this place worth protecting. They support ranching, hunting, recreation, and the rural way of life that defines us. I do not support broad efforts to sell or transfer federal public lands out of public ownership. Once that land is gone, it's gone, and the people who lose the most are everyday Wyomingites, not outside buyers.

That said, land management decisions should be made locally with Wyoming's interests at the table, not dictated by federal agencies disconnected from local realities. I support giving communities and counties a meaningful voice in how nearby public lands are managed, and I'd work to strengthen that relationship rather than sever it.

Good stewardship means watching over the natural beauty and resources that make living in Wyoming so rich, and passing them on intact to the next generation. That's a responsibility I take seriously.
column8:
Property tax policy has to reflect that different communities have different resources available and needs to fill. I believe in keeping the government as close to home as possible which means local communities should have real input into how relief is structured and how revenue needs are met.

Property taxes have risen faster than many families' ability to absorb them and we need to continue working together to provide relief where possible while also acknowledging the real cost of simply cutting taxes. Relief has to be paired with honest conversations about what it funds: schools, roads, emergency services, and how we backfill those needs responsibly.
column9:
Wyoming's economy is strongest when the government gets out of the way and lets hardworking people build. My role as a legislator isn't to pick winners or engineer outcomes, it's to remove unnecessary barriers, protect property rights, and create conditions where entrepreneurs and small businesses can thrive.

That said, the Legislature does have a role: investing in infrastructure, supporting workforce development, and ensuring our mineral, agricultural, and tourism industries have a stable foundation to operate from. I'd also look at thoughtfully diversifying our revenue base so Wyoming isn't perpetually at the mercy of commodity cycles.

Economic development that fits Wyoming should grow from what's already rooted here, honor our values of independence and hard work, and serve the people already living and working in our communities, not outside interests looking to extract value and move on.
column10:
I'm accountable to my neighbors in Sheridan, no one else. That principle shapes how I approach campaign donations. I've focused on building a grassroots campaign funded by people in this community who know me, trust me, and want a representative who answers to them.

There’s opportunity for reform and transparency as more outside interests pour money into Wyoming through campaign expenditure accounts. The people of Wyoming deserve to know who’s trying to influence their vote

column2: Riverton, WY; 70 years; Los Angeles, CA
column4:
I am a lifelong Riverton resident and have been married to my wife, Tanis, since 1980. Together we have two daughters and five grandchildren. Family and community have always been central to my life and my commitment to public service.

For more than 30 years, I have served on the Fremont County School District #25 Board of Trustees, working with educators, parents, students, and taxpayers to make thoughtful decisions that support our schools and communities. That experience has given me firsthand knowledge of budgeting, policy development, long-range planning, and the importance of responsible government.

Throughout my years of service, I have learned that the best decisions come from listening, finding common ground, and focusing on solutions. I am running for Wyoming House District 55 to bring that same practical, local perspective to Cheyenne and represent the people of Fremont County with integrity, independence, and respect.
column5:
One of the biggest challenges facing Wyoming today is the loss of civility and the growing difficulty of working together to solve problems. Too often, political disagreements get in the way of progress, and important issues are left unresolved.

I also see this as one of Wyoming’s greatest opportunities. Our state has a strong tradition of neighbors working together, respecting different viewpoints, and finding common ground. There is far more that unites us than divides us.

As a legislator, my approach will be simple: do the work. I have learned that good decisions come from listening, studying the issues, asking questions, and working with others to find practical solutions. Not everyone will agree on every issue, but we can still treat one another with respect and focus on what is best for Wyoming.

The people of House District 55, and Wyoming, deserve a representative who is willing to listen, collaborate, and get things done.
column6:
Healthcare access and affordability are critical issues for Wyoming, especially in our rural communities. We must ensure that basic healthcare services remain available while also addressing the funding challenges.
One area that deserves immediate attention is mental health. Wyoming continues to see the effects of rising suicide rates, substance abuse, and addiction on individuals, families, and communities. We cannot ignore these challenges. Investing in mental health services, crisis intervention, prevention programs, and treatment resources can improve lives while reducing long-term costs to taxpayers and the healthcare system.
As legislators, we need to work with healthcare providers, communities, and state agencies to identify practical solutions that improve access to care and strengthen essential services. Healthy communities are the foundation of a strong Wyoming, and ensuring access to basic healthcare and mental health resources must remain a priority.
column7:
No. Wyoming’s public lands are a vital part of our economy, our heritage, and our way of life. They support agriculture, energy development, recreation, tourism, and public access opportunities that benefit Wyoming residents and future generations.

I do not support efforts to sell off public lands. Instead, we must remain vigilant in protecting these resources and ensuring they are managed in the best interests of the people of Wyoming. That means advocating for responsible, efficient, and effective management that balances multiple uses while preserving access and opportunities for those who live and work here.

As a legislator, I will support a strong and proactive approach to public land management, working to ensure that Wyoming’s voice is heard in decisions affecting our lands and that these resources continue to serve Wyoming families, communities, and industries for years to come.
column8:
I understand the public’s desire for property tax relief, but we also have to be honest about the consequences of reducing revenue that funds essential local services. Wyoming’s counties are different, and what works in one community may not work in another.

We’ve put ourselves in a difficult position with recent cuts. We cannot continue a “take from Peter to pay Paul” approach, shifting money from one fund to cover shortfalls in another. Schools, local governments, emergency services, and infrastructure all require stable funding.

I don’t claim to have all the answers, but I believe we need to move quickly to explore innovative funding solutions and new revenue opportunities. We must have honest conversations about how we fund the services Wyoming citizens expect while maintaining fiscal responsibility. Ignoring the problem won’t solve it. It’s time to do the work, examine the options, and find practical solutions that serve Wyoming for the long term.
column9:
Economic development is critical to Wyoming’s future. The Legislature should help create an environment where businesses can grow, invest, and create jobs while allowing communities to shape their own economic priorities.

I believe the Wyoming Business Council is doing a fair job supporting economic development efforts across the state. We need to focus on attracting and growing sustainable industries that provide good-paying jobs and long-term opportunities for Wyoming families.

One of our biggest challenges is keeping young people in Wyoming. Too many leave because they don’t see enough career opportunities here. We should support economic development strategies that strengthen our communities, expand our workforce, and create jobs that allow the next generation to build their lives here.

Strong communities and sustainable job growth are essential to Wyoming’s long-term success.
column10:
I believe voters deserve transparency and honesty when it comes to campaign financing. People have a right to know who is supporting candidates and where campaign money is coming from so they can make informed decisions at the ballot box.

The last election cycle highlighted concerns that should not be ignored. Wyoming voters were flooded with negative mailers, misleading information, and campaign materials that often made it difficult to identify who was funding them. That does not build trust in our elections or our government.

I believe Wyoming should review its campaign finance and disclosure laws to ensure funding sources are clear, reporting is timely, and voters have access to accurate information. Regardless of political affiliation, campaigns should be conducted with integrity and transparency. Elections should be about issues, ideas, and qualifications, not confusion, deception, or anonymous attacks.

column2: I currently live in Casper; my family moved back in 2003. I was born in Laramie and raised in Douglas; residing in Douglas from 1st Grade through 9th Grade. My family moved to Casper in 1988 where I attended and graduated from Kelly Walsh High School and Casper College. I then moved to Laramie and graduated from the University of Wyoming. In total, I have been a resident of Wyoming for over 46 years.
column4:
Serving on the Governor’s Wyoming Wildlife Task Force, I was fortunate to work with Legislators, Commissioners, Landowners, Outfitters, Sportsman, Anglers, and Wyoming Game & Fish. We focused on top-priority wildlife policy issues and provided recommendations to the Legislature and WG&F Commission. I learned the importance of understanding issues from a variety of perspectives, leaning on experts for guidance, and the critical importance of engaging those impacted by the decisions made.
I am a lifelong Republican who believes in local control, fiscal responsibility, and doing right by those who call Wyoming home. I have built a reputation of being a legislator who listens, works hard, and is an effective policymaker. I am actively engaged, accessible, and maintain transparent communication. Whether meeting with constituents, testifying in committees, or working on citizen-driven legislation, I am focused on practical solutions that benefit Wyoming and future generations.
column5: Wyoming is facing several challenges including: shrinking populations, increased cost of living, lack of affordable and accessible healthcare and housing, workforce shortages, and lack of economic development, to name a few. Primary concerns I hear from constituents is the lack of job and career opportunities for our youth and young families, healthcare costs and access, and rising costs of living without comparable increases in wages. Wyoming has a very unique landscape with vast spaces and limited populations; theses factors create unique opportunities for public-private partnerships. We are positioned to significantly diversify our economy and improve overall economic growth. To do so, we need leadership willing to embrace change, say yes to new business, and establish policy that best protects and promotes Wyoming's best interests. I will continue to build relationships, understand the complexities of these issues, and focus on solutions that address Wyoming's needs.
column6: The Wyoming Legislature needs to embrace collaborative efforts between public and private sectors. We can improve healthcare affordability and access by increasing and expanding Medicaid coverage, increasing rural hospital funding via the Rural Health Transformation Program, and expanding broadband and telehealth capabilities. The Legislature can also lower out-of-pocket costs by enhancing insurance purchasing power, passing laws to prevent restrictive healthcare provider contracts and non-compete clauses, and investing in primary care. We should focus on maximizing the Rural Health Transformation Program by funding rural facilities (specifically OB/GYN maternal health services) and training programs; recalibrating state investments toward preventative primary care; and leveraging Wyoming Department of Health healthcare workforce development initiatives.
column7: I do not support efforts to sell or transfer public lands in Wyoming. Our Wyoming Constitution directs the Board of Land Commissioners to manage state trust lands for long-term growth in value and optimum, sustainable revenue production. Alternatively, federal public lands-those managed by federal agencies such as the BLM, BOR, and Forest Service-are required to be managed under a multiple-use mandate, which requires the consideration of all uses and users in management decisions. I co-sponsored SJ009, which opposes any state or federal legislation or policy that promotes the broad or indiscriminate sale or exchange of public lands; demands federal agencies respect existing land management frameworks, county land-use plans and the voices of local communities most directly affected by federal land decisions; and supports adequately funding state agencies so they can efficiently and effectively review land purchases, exchanges or limited disposals under the existing legal framework.
column8: Balancing property tax relief with local revenue needs requires a targeted approach that supports our Constitutions' mandate that taxation be "equal and uniform" within each designated property class. I oppose total property tax elimination, which would starve local municipalities and force the elimination of services like fire protection, emergency medical services, law enforcement, road maintenance, water and wastewater systems, and public schools. I have supported and would support income-qualified refund programs and modest, constitutional statewide sales tax adjustments to protect our local essential services. If additional reductions in property tax revenue are adopted, to offset  those reductions we will need to explore increasing statewide sales and use tax or implementing a state income tax. As Wyoming attracts a significant number of tourists, the sales and use tax approach may broaden the tax base without placing an undue burden on local property owners.
column9: I view the legislature's primary responsibility in economic development as maintaining a business-friendly environment by keeping tax burdens low and reducing regulatory red tape to attract private investment. Recognizing Wyoming's dominance in energy, our legislature plays a heavy role in supporting infrastructure development—such as carbon dioxide pipelines, enhanced oil recovery, and energy matching funds—to ensure our legacy industries remain competitive nationally and internationally. I believe our state government should continue playing a role in supporting our local municipalities by  funding foundational infrastructure, such as statewide broadband, expansion of roads, water and wastewater systems, etc., and community facilities needed for economic development. Efforts should provide a consistent strategy to encourage private sector development of companies that provide higher than average wages, create primary jobs, diversify the economy, and generate tax revenue.
column10: Wyoming's campaign finance system operates largely on an honor system, with little auditing or enforcement unless complaints are filed. Meaningful accountability should start with random audits of candidate filings and reporting. Grassroots campaigning, door-to-door outreach, and relatively modest fundraising can still be enough to run a competitive campaign in many districts, preserving Wyoming's tradition of accessible citizen government. I oppose funding from out-of-state influences and believe citizens elected to serve Wyoming residents should only be allowed to accept locally sourced contributions. I would also like to see our state explore establishing campaign limits at all levels of government - capping total campaign amounts, limiting campaign timeframes, and blacking out dates when contributions may be accepted.

column2: For the last 26 years I have lived in the "new" house my grandparents built in 1950.  My father was in the service in El Paso, Texas when I was born but most of my 70 years have been spent in Wyoming.
column4: I attended public schools and worked right here in Natrona County for most of my life.  My roots go back generations to homesteaders.  The history, resources, and communities that make up Wyoming are what have formed my beliefs and values.  My experience both working and volunteering has helped me develop leadership and management skills that I believe will help me in working with legislators in Cheyenne to craft policies that will help Wyoming families.
column5: Wyoming's strong support for public education, public lands, and our "be neighborly but respect each other's privacy" attitude are a big part of what creates opportunity in Wyoming.  Challenges to public education funding, public lands, and personal liberties all need to be addressed.
column6: Fully funding healthcare in all our communities would improve access.
column7: Public land is an essential part of Wyoming's character.  Many people choose to live in Wyoming because of the unequaled access to public lands.  With tourism being the number two driver of Wyoming's economy, protecting public lands is essential.
column8: Property taxes are the most stable and most critical source of revenue for most counties and municipalities in Wyoming.  Property taxes are a major funding source for public education, fire and police protection, cemeteries, and other special districts.  Paying property taxes is how we pay for the services and amenities that make our communities safe, desirable places to live.  Narrowly crafted legislation to provide relief where necessary could bu used to offset the effects skyrocketing market values have had on valuations and assessments.
column9: The Wyoming Business Council was formed to address this very issue.  As legislators, each of us can play a role in recruiting and encouraging diverse business growth in Wyoming.
column10:
Voters need to know who is paying to support Wyoming candidates.  I do not solicit funds from PACs or corporations.  I accept support from friends, neighbors, and family.
The monitoring system requires reporting on fundraising by candidates one week before the primary election and one week before the general election.  The on line reporting system is not onerous and could be used to report monthly from the time a candidate declares their candidacy.  This would give voters more time to learn about a candidate's funding sources before they cast their ballot.  As it stands, voters may have cast their ballots before any reporting is filed.

column2: I have lived in Douglas Wyoming all my life.  I was born in February 1967 in Cheynne while my dad was serving in the legislature.
column4: I was raised on a ranch south of Douglas but moved to town 25 years ago.  I am a Douglas High School graduate and have a BS in Agriculture Economics from the University of Wyoming.  I have served as a County Commissioner for the past 19.5 years and that has provided me with a wealth of knowledge and experience.  I have advocated for Converse County and Wyoming at the state and national level, including the US Departments of Energy, Interior, Agriculture, BLM and EPA.  Multiple use, streamlined regulations and local control are a regular theme when I am speaking to those agencies. I am the stadium announcer for Douglas Bearcat Football and have been a High School Basketball Official for 23 years.  My wife Tione and I are small business owners and very active in our community.  We have 2 sons and 1 granddaughter.
column5: We need to continue investing in economic development and diversity by appropriately funding and directing the Business council so they can be an effective partner with local entities as they seek out and grow local businesses.  If we fail to stay in the game as some have suggested, we lose, plain and simple.  Economic development is a process not an event.  Our core industries of Tourism, Energy and Agriculture employee and provide a large part of our revenue.  We must continue to research new prospects and build upon existing partnerships to expand those opportunities.  Rare earth elements and the resurgence of uranium have huge potential for Wyoming.  Finally, we need to invest more into our ageing infrastructure.  Much of infrastructure is at an age where if we fail to invest now, the cost will become unbearable later.  Roads, Bridges, water, sewer and the like are basic quality of life needs we must maintain and improve.
column6: First of all, we need to make sure health care is available.  Rural hospitals are in desperate shape and lack of care is real.  The health care transformation proposal from the Dept of Health was trying to help ensure this in the long term.  Unfortunately, the proposal was rejected by the feds. However, some of the ideas in the proposal are worth looking at for state implementation.    Another factor is the limited number of companies offering health insurance in WY, which has appeared to lead to higher premiums.  The real fact is that the problems with health care are larger than Wyoming and real reform and vision is needed at the national level.  We must do what we can, but also pressure Congress to do better.
column7:
* There should not be any large-scale sale or trade of public lands. Public lands in Wyoming are a treasurer and we must protect them.  They provide recreation, wildlife habitat, support agriculture, mineral wealth and a host of other benefits.
Limited trades that block up public land with the goal of providing better access and more efficient management should be considered, but in a restricted manner and only with local input.
column8: Targeted property tax relief has been very effective in Wyoming.  As a County Commissioner I advocated for and voted to implement the highly successful Converse County Property Tax refund program.  Over the last 3 years we have refunded over $620,000 to residential owners in Converse County.  That works well in a county with significant mineral production.  I support letting Wyoming citizens vote on the 50% reduction initiative, however if it passes, local services will be cut in many cities, towns and counties, the math is that simple.  Local governments will have to make very tough decisions, as I do not expect the legislature to be able to fill that gap as they will also need to fill the gap in school funding caused by the reduction.
column9: *As noted before, we must remain in the economic development game.  If we fail to play, we lose.  Some have characterized economic development as picking winners and losers, that is incredibly short-sighted.  It is about creating jobs for our kids and grandkids.  It is about reducing our dependence on mineral production and increasing job diversity.   Activity breeds activity and sound economic development strategies will pay dividends.  One thing the state can do to help is investment into infrastructure to support work force housing.  In many places economic development is hampered by the limited places for new workers to live.  If we can help with the infrastructure, we can lower the cost of the residential lot and get affordable houses for our workforce.  We also need to be sure the state vision includes local input.  Each community will have different desires, and we need to accommodate local visions.
column10: I believe the campaign reporting requirements for candidates is clear and transparent, which is very important.  Filing donation reports a week before the primary allows the voters to know who is supporting each candidate and evaluate accordingly.

column2: Cheyenne Wyoming, born and raised, I have lived here cumulatively for 26 years.
column4: I bring experience in municipal government, cybersecurity, and the legal field. As a paralegal and having written legislation, I have conducted legal research, analyzed policy, and helped draft effective legislation. My background in government operations and technology gives me a practical understanding of today's challenges and the skills to serve our community with integrity and accountability.
column5:
Wyoming faces a shrinking and aging population, limited economic diversification, workforce shortages, and the outmigration of young people seeking opportunities elsewhere. In addition, we are grappling with access to healthcare, reliable infrastructure, and affordable housing.

I believe that addressing affordability is the key starting point. If we can ensure that people can both work and live in our communities, we can better retain young people, help fill workforce shortages, and strengthen access to essential services like healthcare. Improving access to healthcare, especially in rural areas, is critical to keeping families and communities healthy and stable. With a stronger population base, we can also expand our tax base, creating additional resources to invest in infrastructure, healthcare systems, and public services.
column6:
I believe the legislature can address this in several ways. One option is allowing the state to act as a reinsurer for health insurance providers operating in Wyoming, helping to stabilize risk and reduce costs. Similarly, the state could explore creating an opt-in public insurance pool, modeled after how municipalities pool resources to secure coverage.

Such approaches could increase bargaining power, improve market stability, and potentially provide greater flexibility in shaping rates and coverage options, while still preserving choice for consumers.
column7: I do not support the sale or permanent transfer of public lands. However, I do support exploring alternatives such as land exchanges, long-term leasing, and cooperative management agreements that preserve public access while also allowing for responsible local economic development where appropriate.
column8:
I support addressing excessive property taxes through a combination of targeted relief and broader structural reform. This includes providing relief for homeowners, particularly those on fixed incomes, through tools such as homestead exemptions or circuit breaker programs that prevent tax burdens from rising faster than a household’s ability to pay.

At the same time, I believe we should pursue reforms that improve fairness and predictability in property tax assessments, helping prevent sudden or disproportionate increases in tax bills. We should also explore reasonable limits on excessive growth in property tax collections to ensure taxpayers are not overburdened by rapid increases in local levies.
column9:
I believe economic development works best as a partnership that combines the strengths of the public and private sectors bringing together the dependability and consistency of government with the speed, creativity, and innovation of the private sector.
The legislature should focus on creating a fair, stable, and predictable regulatory environment, while guiding investment in essential infrastructure such as roads and broadband and ensuring permitting and licensing processes are efficient and accessible.
The private sector is the primary driver of growth, leading through entrepreneurship, job creation, and innovation, and responding quickly to changing market needs.
When targeted incentives are used to attract investment or support key industries, they should be applied carefully, with clear accountability, transparency, and measurable results.
column10:
I believe campaign finance should be guided by transparency, accountability, and public trust. Contributions should never influence policy outcomes or create the perception that access to elected officials is for sale. Voters deserve to know who is funding campaigns and how that funding may be shaping political priorities.
I support reasonable and transparent campaign donation rules that allow for civic participation while ensuring full disclosure of contributions and expenditures. Public confidence in government depends on clear reporting, timely disclosure, and strict compliance to existing ethics laws.

column2:
My current address is 7504 Schrader Lane, Cheyenne, WY  82009;
We have been at this address for 20 years.
We have lived in Wyoming for 39 years.
I was born in Africa.  My parents were Lutheran Missionaries.
column4:
I am a Christian, Husband, Father, Grandfather, retired American Soldier, and a lifelong public servant, running as a Conservative, non-caucus Republican.  I enlisted in the Army as a teenager and served for 36 years, spending the last eight as the Commanding General of the Wyoming Military Department, where I was responsible for the roughly 3000 men and women of the WY Air and Army Guard.  I responded to multiple disasters within Wyoming and deployed to Kuwait in 2004.  After retiring from the Guard in 2019, I was privileged to lead another group of men and women as the Director of WYDOT for 4 years, retiring in 2023.  The twelve years spent leading a Wyoming Agency provided an excellent opportunity to observe State Government in action and work about 120 bills through the State Legislature.   

Education:  Master's in Public Administration from UW; Master's in Strategic Studies from the US Army War College; BS Education, BA Business / Accounting from Concordia University, Seward, NE
column5:
Challenges:  1) Reliance on fossil fuels and the associated revenue fluctuations; 2) Retaining our youth; 3) Healthcare access, specifically in rural areas. 

Opportunities:  1) Innovation and expansion in the fossil fuels and minerals sector, e.g. rare earths, nuclear power, and uranium; 2) Continued economic diversification; 3) Expand the tourism sector 

As a Legislator, I will work to:
1)	Support our legacy industries (energy, agriculture, tourism) and ensure we support innovation and development in those sectors.
2)	Continue to diversify our economy through retaining our favorable tax structure, working to reduce our regulations while lowering the cost and speeding up the permitting process.   
3)	Work to build thriving communities that create opportunities for our youth. I see this as providing good jobs, affordable housing, accessible healthcare, excellent education, and reliable infrastructure.
4)	Work to better match the skills and interests of our workforce to industry.
column6:
The establishment of the Health Insurance Affordability TF is an excellent first step by the Legislature to address the costs of health care insurance. The results of that task force should provide specific concepts for Legislative action.  In that same vein, I would support the Legislature participating in a multi-sector, multi-level health care task force that involves government, providers, and patients to address health issues across the state.  
Some specific areas I recommend exploring include: 1) Expanding the scope of practice; e.g. leverage the skill sets and expand the legal authority of midwives and nurse practitioners; 2) Growing the use of telehealth; 3) Working to stabilize our rural hospitals through leveraging the federal Rural Health Transformation Program 4) Tackling the uncompensated care issue; 5) Expanding the very successful WWAMI program; 6) Exploring incentives to encourage Medical Professionals to practice in Wyoming such as loan repayment.
column7:
I do not support the sale of public lands and applaud the actions taken by the Legislature to express the same.  Many residents of Wyoming use that land for recreation and hunting, which is one of the key reasons they live and work in Wyoming. Public lands also support our tourism industry, one of our key economic drivers.
We should be cautious about transferring Federal land to the State given the required costs of maintaining those lands, such as fire mitigation.  
I plan to support the management of these lands by: 1) Insisting on local input and respect for land-use plans before federal land decisions; 2) Pushing for adequate funding of the BLM and Forest Service so understaffing is not used as a reason to sell; and 3) Supporting Legislative resolutions such as SJ9 affirming opposition to broad federal land sales/exchanges.
I would also be interested in exploring the ability to execute narrow, locally-backed land swaps that improve public access, but not blanket transfers.
column8:
There is some real tension in this space, compounded by November’s ballot measure to reduce the Property tax by 50%.  My general approach will be to find a reasonable balance based on the following principles:

1)	Property tax provides revenue for essential services at the local level, revenue that is important for creating the thriving communities we need in Wyoming to retain our youth and support our economy.
2)	Government closest to the people is typically the best and should be locally supported.  
3)	I am an advocate for targeted tax relief, rather than broad exemptions.
4)	Local communities should be kept whole, either through backfill or other funding sources.  
5)	I am not an advocate for an increase in Sales tax.  I think it is regressive and would have negative consequences for the optional 2 cents.
6)	I am interested in exploring a “one size does not fit all” approach relative to mill levies by allowing local communities to adjust their levies by vote.
column9:
The State Legislature plays a key role in economic development across the state.

Fundamentally, I think the Legislature is responsible for establishing the business climate and environment so that businesses can prosper in their communities.
  
The government is responsible for providing the road transportation system our economy operates on, oversees and regulates utilities like water, wastewater, power and broadband that are essential for business development, ensures the funding of our education system, which trains the workforce our economy uses, and maintains law and order, which is essential to the stability of our economy.  

The Legislature establishes the tax structure that directly affects who invests in Wyoming and how, and funds capital-type grant and loan programs, such as the Business Ready Community Grant Fund, used across the state to set the conditions for economic development.
column10:
My opinion is that transparency and the ability to see who is donating is critical.  It is also important that we keep the playing field accessible to candidates without deep pockets or PAC funding.  I recommend that we allow the public to see who is funding candidates well before they vote.  

Some reforms that make sense to me include earlier and more frequent disclosure, a closer look at independent expenditure groups, and an easier search function online.

column2: I live in Evanston. I have lived in Evanston for 43 years. I have lived in Wyoming my entire life. I was born in Evanston, Wy.
column4: I’m a 5th generation lifelong resident who grew up on a ranch outside of Mt. View. We also ran a construction company where I learned valuable skills. I graduated from UW with an Education degree and taught and coached in Cheyenne, Lyman H.S. and Evanston H.S.for 40 years. I served 2 terms on the Evanston City Council and 2 terms in the Wyoming Senate. I have served on a number of local boards: Uinta Senior Center Board, EYC (Evanston Youth Board) SWOT (SW Outdoor Trails, Soroptimist Biard,etc.
column5: Economic diversification, housing, health care, and outmigration of our youth.I think we need to look at incentives and better marketing to let people know we are open for business.  We need to tout our low tax burden, and do a deep dive into why we aren’t getting more business and projects coming our way particularly in our smaller communities. We definitely need to be proactive with the housing and look at barriers that are affecting our growth. Healthcare is a tough one but I think the new funds coming from the health transformation could help if we are creative.
column6: We have to be proactive with the health transformation funds but we need to also bump up our Medicaid rates, and support our hospitals as best we can.  Many of our issues are due to CMS regulations and insurance but the Legislature needs to think outside of the box to come up with solutions to those problems.
column7: I am NOT in favor of selling our public lands. They belong to the public. I will support any and all legislation to ensure that our public lands remain in public hands.
column8: I think we need to stop and really get the data from all of the property tax legislation we have passed in the last few years before we move on any other property tax legislation.  We still don’t have enough data yet to see what the real impacts are to cities, counties, special districts, and schools before we jump from the frying pan into the fire.
column9: We should be a part of the conversation. Even if the WBC is reformed or changed drastically the Legislature should have skin in the game along with funding issues. We need to encourage businesses to come to Wyoming and the Legislature can be an important stakeholder.
column10: The system we have probably needs some reforming.  I think it would behoove whomever is the new Sec. of State along with input from the Legislature to take a deep dive into what other states do and come up with the best system possible. I think it’s ok to take donation as everything is much more expensive but those donations should never taint or affect your votes. If you remember always that you are working for the Wyoming people instead of outside interests then your votes can remain ethical and above board.

column2: I live in Gillette, and was raised here. I did move away for a couple of years when I was first married during college but always knew we would move home to raise our family. I have been here in Gillette for the past 23 years and was born in Buffalo Wyoming.
column4: I am a wife, mother of three daughters and a small business owner in Gillette. I've worked in Property and Casualty insurance and several ministry positions over the last 20 years that included, Youth Ministry, Young Adults and Women's Ministry. Currently, I am serving my second term in the Wyoming House of Representatives for House district 3 and am honored to serve and represent my constituents.
column5: I get calls weekly from constituents concerned with non-transparent economic development. They feel they will be left out of a decision that could change their communities, property values and quality of life. Most are not against change, but would like to be including and educated with open communication from their elected officials.
