Legislation that protects people, private property rights, and resources while allowing businesses to flourish is essential and something I'm looking forward to working on. Some economic development may not be right for all communities and the people in those communities should be collectively making that decision.
column6: This is a top issue and one that anytime the government gets involved it tends to skyrocket the price. My own health insurance costs are expensive as a small business owner. We as a nation are living with the consequences of the federal governments decision to meddle in the healthcare prices. One area that could be a starting point here in the state is working on malpractice insurance reform. Currently, physicians including OB/GYN's have extremely high malpractice rates and that has aided in the maternity desserts across the State. This is also a concern with our rural hospitals as well.
column7: Public Lands needs to be multi use although complicated. We see minerals being produced and that revenue flowing into the state and the enjoyment of hunters and outdoorsmen on our public lands. As far as selling state land to the feds, I'm against it. The federal government already owns almost 50% of Wyoming lands and our land is our heritage.
column8: This is an issue that has been wrestled with during every debate for property tax relief. Some counties can weather the decrease and some of our hardship counties could potentially struggle. Every property tax exemption has to be looked at from each counties perspective and we have done that. When the 25% was being debated there was an effort to backfill our hardship counties in the house. After living with the 25% we see that some of our hardship counties didn't see the drastic declines that they thought would come. Interestingly a majority of residential homeowners didn't apply this year, whether they didn't know to or they chose not to. Property tax relief is a fine balance and finding what works takes time, input, and research.
column9: This has been a topic of discussion over the last year and as a member of the Appropriations committee we are discussing this over this summer. There does seem to be a desire to foster an environment to diversify our economy. How the legislature directs and has oversight is important but remembering that each community has to decide how that looks for them. We as a legislature should make sure that people, private property rights and  recourses are protected. We as a legislature should not be pitting businesses against each other or making our businesses that have been around for decades compete with a competitor that they have been forced to subsidize with their tax dollars. Local municipalities and Counties really are where the actual economic development should happen. They know their communities best and local input and partnership from residents is crucial.
column10: I believe in transparency in donations, all donations I receive are filed with the Secretary of State. I currently do not see concerns in how donations are monitored. If you receive a donation you are required to report it.

column2: Ethete, Wyoming        34 Years      All my Life      1960
column4: Attended school at Fort Washakie Elementary and Tribal Boarding School in Chilocco, Oklahoma.  Served in the US Army and started my career in Natural Resources in 1983.  Worked for the Bureau of Indian Affairs and Shoshone National Forest from 1983-1994 and was elected to the Shoshone Business Council in 1994.  Served the Eastern Shoshone Tribe for 21 years before coming to Central Wyoming College in 2016 where I serve as the Coordinator for Tribal Education.  Attended school at Central Wyoming College.  I have three children and two grandsons.
column5: Obviously there are several challenges throughout the state, from rising costs and access to healthcare, education and the exodus of our graduates out of the state, Public lands access and continued care for our seniors.
column6: Medicaid expansion, access to specialized treatments locally, expand mental health services.
column7: I do not support.  Some efforts have to be made at the federal level such as funding much needed positions in natural resource agencies.
column8: We have to be very aware of the funding issues with smaller municipalities and the drastic effects it will have on those communities.  One size don't fit all.
column9: It should have some role but not the major role.  Educating communities on the pathway to economic possibilities for individuals is important and assisting in larger projects through transparency and partnerships.
column10: The campaign system in the nation needs reformed.

column2: I am a 4th generation rancher out of Otto Wyoming. I was born in Cody Wyoming. I have lived here in Wyoming my whole life.
column4: I am a rancher who has been compelled to stand and fight for our rights. I have been forced to fight the State Engineer office, the DEQ, and in the courts to defend my constitutional rights. I have shown I am willing and able to stand, fight and win against government over-reach.
column5:
There are many challenges facing Wyoming today. Cost of living, over spending, and government over-reach.

To fix government over-reach we need to provide a simple mechanism for recourse to hold government officials accountable when they violate the law. We need the people to be able to enforce their rights and receive compensation for injury when government officials violate law without being forced into costly litigation.

Over-spending requires that we relentlessly attack inefficiencies in government. Creating true reform by isolating unnecessary systems and allowing unnecessary individuals to return to the free market for work.
column6:
We need to address the fraud that exists in the healthcare system. Look at cash pay costs verses government subsidized insurance cost. The numbers vary wildly! Look at the cost of medication in the USA verses other countries. 

We must get the corporate fingers out of taxpayer pocket and make healthcare more affordable for our families and seniors.
column7:
The only transfer of public land I would support is bringing management of federal lands back to the state of Wyoming. The people of Wyoming deserve public lands to stay public for the general public’s benefit. We should not sell our public lands. 

Our lands should be accessible to those who want to enjoy nature; God’s most beautiful creation.
column8:
It is difficult to address property tax reform without addressing our overspending. The fear of loss of funding makes people worry about property tax relief.

Let’s look at it from a principled perspective. Private property ownership is fundamental to our democracy. If we must pay the government for land we own under threat of removal, do we really own our private property?

We must address over spending in order to respect people’s private property rights. We should not be forced to endlessly rent our land from the government. That’s not the Wyoming way.
column9: Its role is to establish and maintain a level playing field. Economic development is best managed by the free market. Excessive government intervention is not a far cry from socialism. Taking from some to elevate others. That is not the governments job.
column10: I believe campaign contributions are a form of political speech protected by the First Amendment. My values are simple: full transparency so voters can see exactly who is supporting a candidate, combined with the freedom for Wyoming citizens to voluntarily support the candidates and causes they believe in without excessive government restrictions.

column2: I was born and raised in Cheyenne, WY.
column4:
Senator Evie Brennan is a consistent conservative who is working to preserve Wyoming for her children and their generation like her grandparents preserved Wyoming for her. Evie is a third generation Wyoming native whose great-grandfather homesteaded in Wyoming. Married with four children, she and her husband are raising a new Wyoming generation.

​​Evie Brennan graduated from Cheyenne East High School in 2000. She attended the University of Wyoming where she graduated with a Bachelors of Science in Nursing in 2004.

Evie has worked as a nurse in Cheyenne since graduating college. For the past 12 years she has worked in the ICU. This experience has prepared Evie to work hard, think critically and defend the freedoms of Wyoming.

Evie has served on the Joint Transportation Committee. She is currently a member of the Joint Education Committee, The select committee on School Finance-Recalibration and is the Senate Chairman for Labor, Health and Social Services.
column5: Some of the biggest challenges facing Wyoming today are jobs and our economy. Because of the state’s boom‑and‑bust cycle, we must get serious about diversifying our economy. We have outstanding natural resources and should continue to develop them responsibly while protecting our landscapes and communities. At the same time, we must look 50–100 years ahead and ask: What industries are young people entering, and how can we provide the education and jobs for those careers here in Wyoming? What resource‑based industries can locate here and offer good, long‑term careers for our families? What steps will attract the right companies and industries to our state? By intentionally aligning our legacy industries with emerging sectors and planning and developing strong infrastructure we can build a more stable economy and create well‑paying jobs for the next generation.
column6: Healthcare is a complex system that can be difficult for patients and families to understand and navigate. Much of this challenge stems from the intricate relationships among providers, insurers, pharmaceutical companies, and government programs. We must work to reduce unnecessary complexity and improve transparency so patients can access care more easily. Compounding these challenges is a nationwide shortage of healthcare professionals, and Wyoming's vast rural geography makes access even more difficult. We need targeted strategies to strengthen the healthcare workforce, improve provider recruitment and retention, expand telehealth and mobile health services, and support local clinics and hospitals. By focusing on access, affordability, and quality, we can ensure all Wyoming residents receive timely, high-quality care close to home.
column7:
Wyoming's public lands are not for sale. They belong to the people of Wyoming and are a vital part of our heritage, economy, and way of life. These lands support outdoor recreation, tourism, agriculture, energy development, and local communities across the state.

Public lands provide access for hunting, fishing, hiking, camping, and other outdoor activities while preserving the natural beauty, wildlife habitat, and open spaces that make Wyoming unique. As stewards of these resources, we have a responsibility to keep them accessible, productive, and well managed for future generations.

I oppose efforts to sell or transfer public lands that would reduce public access or compromise Wyoming's long-term interests. Protecting these lands ensures that future generations can enjoy the same opportunities and freedoms we value today.
column8:
Over the last several years, the Wyoming Legislature has worked thoughtfully to address rapidly rising property taxes while recognizing the need for counties and local governments to maintain essential infrastructure and public safety services. We have enacted a 4% cap on annual property tax increases and provided a 25% property tax reduction to deliver meaningful relief to homeowners.

Moving forward, we must continue to balance taxpayer relief with the fiscal needs of our communities. Taxes should remain low, fair, and broad-based, while ensuring that counties have the resources necessary to maintain roads, bridges, emergency services, and other critical infrastructure that Wyoming residents depend on every day.
column9:
Economic development in Wyoming should focus on creating good-paying, stable jobs that allow families and communities to thrive. We must remain competitive with the other 49 states in attracting new industries and investment while continuing to support and strengthen our legacy industries, including energy, agriculture, tourism, and mining.

Wyoming's business-friendly environment, skilled workforce, and quality of life should provide a strong foundation for growth. As we recruit new companies and industries, they should complement our existing economy, respect Wyoming values, and contribute positively to our communities. The goal is not growth for growth's sake, but sustainable economic development that expands opportunities, diversifies our economy, and preserves the Wyoming way of life for future generations.
column10:
Transparency is essential to maintaining public trust in our elections and government. Wyoming law requires campaign contributions and expenditures to be reported to the Secretary of State's office, and that information is readily accessible to the public.

Voters have a right to know who is funding political campaigns and how campaign funds are being spent. Public disclosure promotes accountability, helps ensure compliance with campaign finance laws, and strengthens confidence in the electoral process. Campaign contribution and expenditure records should continue to remain publicly available and easily accessible to Wyoming citizens.

column2: Casper, HD57. I was born and raised in Casper, and lived in Natrona County my whole life.
column4:
I am a lifelong Casper resident, and a stepdad to three amazing little girls. I’ve grown up here, and I’ve watched where we have struggled to provide support to families who need it most. My family was forced to move during the market crash of ‘08, highlighting to me at a young age how fragile stability can be. 

I worked to save Rock the Block. I organized the Pokémon Go community to cleanup downtown Casper. I’ve helped on service projects at the Historic Trails Center. I’ve built playgrounds with my bare hands. 

I am running to give my neighbors a choice this year. I don’t believe an election should be decided by one party, at the primary election. 70% of voter-aged residents didn’t even show up at our last primary election, and it left me wondering what a “majority” decision looks like. I’m running because my representation feels designed to manage my concerns, not seek to sincerely understand my viewpoint, and I believe I can provide the representation I feel I’m lacking.
column5:
We have an opportunity to grow in several ways across the state. First, our kids are leaving, schools are being cut. Next, healthcare in Wyoming is struggling, from physical to mental wellbeing, and even addiction. Wyoming is often a leader across the nation in youthful suicides, with suicide being the second-highest leading cause of death. Finally, our minimum wage hasn’t increased in Wyoming for decades. 

I think that in order to solve these problems, we first need to understand the impact of tax cuts before recklessly implementing them. We should expand healthcare access across the state, while holding monopolistic organizations accountable for the harm they have caused our communities. And, let’s be frank, the economy isn’t survivable at $5.15/hour.
column6:
Wyoming already sees a shortage of doctors in nearly every county across the state. While we’ve seen that shortage, many of these communities have seen one or two specific resources take over where local doctors used to serve. It is imperative when we have access to federal grants, that we consider accepting and allocating those funds. 

We also see a shortage of mental health care. As a legislator, I would push for mental health emergency services, taking some of the most vulnerable in our community and providing solutions that allow officers to focus their efforts in service and protection of our community where it is better placed.
column7: I want to be very clear here; I grew up here in Wyoming. I’ve hunted and fished these lands religiously since I was a child. These are also the same lands I take my children to learn, play, and grow. I appreciate the resources, labor, and love that are dedicated to caring for our land daily. I do not support any measure attempting to sell public lands, and would vigorously defend the land on behalf of the people.
column8:
I believe that the public deserves tax relief, but I’m not sure we’re approaching it the right way. Property tax relief specifically helps people who own property. Roughly a quarter of all Wyoming families are renters. Some are born and raised here, others have lived here for years but sold the family farm to be closer to relatives, and nearly every one I’ve talked to would love to find their own home to live in. 

I would like to see tax relief for all residents, which means ensuring that relief reaches the seniors and families in rentals, without doing so at the expense of my children’s future. I would advocate for legislation that aligns with these values.
column9:
I believe the Legislature's role in economic development should be to create the conditions for private industry to thrive, not to run or ruin it. The film industry is a perfect example.
 
Far too many productions have chosen to represent Wyoming with an older, out-of-touch view, and I believe allowing these productions to film here would encourage tourism, and create a more modern view of Wyoming across the world. When a $20,000,000-and-growing industry comes knocking at your door to spend money in your city, why are we saying no?
column10: I believe in transparency for campaign donations. I’m not sure if I think the system needs reform at this time, but if I were to support any reform, it would be with ease of access for the voter, while not creating a barrier for potential candidates, at the front of my mind.

column2: I was born and raised in Sheridan and have spent nearly 36 of my 43 years here.  I moved away a couple times, but Sheridan always calls me home.
column4:
I have spent most of my life serving others and helping strengthen the communities where I live. For 15 years, I served as a pastor in the Wesleyan Church, walking alongside families through life's challenges and learning the value of servant leadership. 

As Director of Marketing for the Sheridan County Chamber of Commerce, I gained a firsthand understanding of the importance of a strong business community and the role collaboration plays in solving local challenges. Later, as Executive Director of Sheridan KidsLife, I worked closely with children, giving me an appreciation for the challenges they face.

Today, I serve as Director of Development at The Hub on Smith, where I help secure funding for essential services that support older adults as they age in place.

These experiences have given me a broad perspective on the needs of Wyoming families and businesses, and reinforced my belief that strong communities are built through service, responsibility, and neighbors working together.
column5:
One of the greatest challenges facing Wyoming today is ensuring that our economy provides enough opportunity for the people to stay, work, and raise a family here. Too many people leave Wyoming in search of better job opportunities, while many employers struggle to find the workforce they need. At the same time, residents are facing a housing market that has become increasingly difficult to afford. As a family of six, we are faced with these realities every day.

I believe these challenges present some of our greatest opportunities. I would make economic growth and job creation a top priority by attracting new businesses and creating an environment where employers can invest and grow. 

I would also be committed to addressing the rising cost of housing. We should be able to afford to live in the communities we serve. Finding practical solutions that increase housing availability and affordability will be critical to Wyoming's future.
column6:
My family has experienced the rising cost of healthcare firsthand. With four kids, our second largest expense each year, after housing, is healthcare. Wyoming consistently ranks among the most expensive states in the nation for individual health insurance, and rural access challenges contribute to higher overall healthcare costs.

The Legislature should focus on solutions that work for our rural state. We need to strengthen our healthcare workforce by expanding programs that attract and retain doctors, nurses, and other providers in rural communities. We need to continue to supporting telehealth services and expanding broadband access to help connect patients with specialists without requiring hours of travel. In addition, supporting rural hospitals and clinics is critical to ensuring that Wyoming residents can receive care close to home.

No single policy will solve this challenge, but every piece can help make healthcare more affordable and accessible for Wyoming families.
column7:
Wyoming's public lands are one of our state's greatest assets. I’ve spent a lifetime enjoying them and would love to see my children and future generations do the same. They support agriculture, energy production, outdoor recreation, wildlife habitat, tourism, and our way of life. I do not support efforts to sell off public lands because once those lands are gone, they are gone for future generations.

As a legislator, I would support policies that protect public access, respect private property rights, and ensure that land management decisions are informed by local stakeholders. I also support efforts to improve coordination between federal agencies and state and local governments, so Wyoming's concerns are heard and addressed.

The goal should be responsible stewardship that preserves our public lands, supports Wyoming's economy, and ensures these resources remain available for future generations to enjoy and benefit from. Our kids will thank us.
column8:
I understand why many homeowners are asking for relief. I own my home and the cost of living affects us daily. At the same time, property taxes help fund essential local services such as schools, roads, law enforcement, and fire protection. These services affect us daily as well. 

I believe we should pursue responsible property tax relief that helps homeowners while preserving the ability of local communities to provide the services their residents rely on. Any proposal should be carefully evaluated for its impact on counties, municipalities, and taxpayers before it is implemented.

Rather than shifting costs from one place to another or creating unintended consequences for local governments, we should look for sustainable solutions that provide relief while maintaining local control. Local communities should have a voice in decisions that directly affect their revenue and their ability to meet local needs.
column9:
The Legislature should focus on the fundamentals that make Wyoming an attractive place to invest and create jobs. That includes maintaining a business-friendly regulatory environment, investing in critical infrastructure, supporting workforce development, and ensuring that housing is available and affordable for working families. 

The Legislature should work with local communities, businesses, educational institutions, and economic development organizations to remove barriers to growth and encourage private-sector investment. Strong communities, a skilled workforce, affordable housing, and a healthy business climate are all essential to Wyoming's long-term prosperity.
column10: My values when it comes to campaign donations is simple: transparency and honesty. Voters should be able to easily see where campaign money comes from and how it is spent. Wyoming's campaign finance system has traditionally emphasized transparency. However, I am open to reviewing the system to ensure disclosure requirements remain clear, timely, and accessible to the public. Any reforms should focus on increasing transparency and public trust.

column2: Afton Wyoming.  I have lived here all my life except for my during my college years.
column4:
I have a deep love for the State of Wyoming that was instilled by my family who established the Valley and Western Wyoming.  
I taught/coached and advised at Star Valley High School for 29 years after teaching in Utah for one year.  I was also the high school teacher representative to the administration and school board for most of those years.  I also was active in many community activities and committees during that time including recreation, multiple use, forest collaborations and fish and game.   I then served as a precinct committeeman for the Lincoln County Republican Party.  I later served as the vice chair of that committee.  
I was then elected to House District 21 Representative.
column5:
Wyoming faces some major issues going forward but has many things in its favor.  
I like to focus on the many good people that have Wyoming's best interest in mind.  I believe that good ideas and leadership come from many different groups and factions.  However, the deep division in the Republican Party has caused more harm than good.  We need to focus on "being on the same" team and working to make Wyoming better and not to "burn it down" to make one point or another.  
I am focused on improving education, creating jobs and promoting our many energy possibilities.  We also must work to solve the problem of why our best and brightest are leaving the state.  Quality and quantity of good paying jobs have to be our focus as well as affordable standards of living.
column6: We must make this a priority in word and deed.  We have to work together to recognize and fund our unique health care problems.  We have to get over comparing our state to that others.  We are unique but can make it better if we move this up on list of urgent issue to solve.  We have the resources to help and the hardworking people to do as much as they can  to proactively participate.
column7: I do not support efforts to sell public land.  They are very important to our way of life.  We must make a strong stand on this and not let national interests overwhelm us that live here.
column8: I have been very active in approving major steps to provide tax reform.  I will continue to do this.  I also recognize that we must fund our schools, towns, counties and special districts because they are what makes Wyoming great.
column9: I believe that more oversight and  accountability can be provided.  I think the State can and should be a partner in selected projects to allow growth and development under tight and fair guidelines.
column10: Campaign donations should be closely monitored.  Out of State donors must be identified and transparent.

column2:
Since 2018 my wife and I have live on Jim Bridger Ave. in Casper. We previously lived on the west side of Casper in a home we purchased in 1988. 
I first arrived in Casper in 1979 for a summer job at the end of my freshman year at the University of South Dakota. I returned in the spring of 1980 and was married here in 198.
I was born in Kadoka, SD.
column4:
Although I arrived in Casper from South Dakota for a pipeline construction job in 1979, I actually spent each summer from 1975 to 1978 working for the Rimrock Ranch west of Cody.
Casper is where I entered the natural gas industry, met and married my wife, raised our children, and am now retired.
I spent more than 40 years in the natural gas industry, gaining extensive experience in safety, environmental compliance, engineering standards, and federal and state regulations. I've also have decades of experience addressing public land access policy and served over 15 years in multiple leadership roles within the Natrona County Republican Party, helping craft bylaws, resolutions, and policies that remain in effect today.
The experience I've gained during my first term as representation is immeasurable and will be of enormous benefit to my constituents and myself.
column5:
While the current administration is focused on addressing our energy needs, we also want to ensure America regains its energy independence. Wyoming is uniquely positioned to play an important role in achieving both. Our challenge is to accomplish this while preserving Wyoming's heritage.
I believe we have the resources and technology to do both if we can assure Wyoming citizens that they will be equal partners in making these decisions.
If we want to reduce the impact of shrinking revenue in one sector, we need to increase our production in another. As an advocate for abundant, clean-burning natural gas, I would be doing our state a disservice if I didn't encourage advancements in natural-gas-fired electricity generation to be part of the solution. On-demand electrical generation that doesn't conflict with Wyoming's open spaces is a good fit.
column6:
A couple of suggestions I've had depend on changes to federal tax law, which are nearly impossible to achieve; I think a broad expansion of healthcare savings accounts could provide an avenue to change the relationship between consumers and providers by eliminating the intermediary for ordinary medical goods and services. For this to happen, several major changes are required. Everyone is eligible regardless of insurance status, with high contribution limits, plus rollovers from other tax-deferred accounts, and, lastly, the account can be used on behalf of any person the account holder chooses. Please forward this to the House Ways and Means Committee, please.
If "affordable" healthcare means inviting half a million more people to live in Wyoming so that premium prices can fall, I think it will be a tough sell.
column7:
I do not support broad efforts to dispose of federal public lands solely to transfer ownership.

Wyoming has successfully sold or exchanged state lands when doing so benefited trust beneficiaries and Wyoming citizens. The Kelly Parcel sale demonstrated that exceptional value can be realized. Exchanges can also improve management, public access, and ownership patterns. These transactions are lengthy and should be evaluated on their merits.

The Wyoming Constitution guides what is in the best interests of the people by requiring that state trust lands support designated beneficiaries while balancing other public interests. For federal lands, decisions should be made as close as possible to affected communities, with input from citizens, local governments, and state officials. My goal is to maintain access, support multiple uses, protect important resources, and provide a fair return to Wyoming taxpayers.
column8:
Any property tax reform package has to account for the differing property tax environments between counties. These differences are well documented and I expect a see a formula that will account for it.
Our original intent in 2025 was to backfill most of the anticipated reductions. It didn't happen, but I expect backfill to be re-considered.I cannot tell you what form that will take. We still have two more meetings in the Revenue Committee and I urge anyone interested to stay tuned.
I am concerned about the 4% cap and credit a cap with the one instrument that prevents property tax revolts from recurring. I believe a reasonable cap is essential.
column9:
Article 16 of the Wyoming Constitution repeatedly addresses the legislature's scope in economic development. I think we've taken a pray-and-spray approach over the last few years, hoping that some ironclad result will prove the rightness of past legislative decisions.
I expect some notable reform to this model in the 2027 session and certainly re-appropriation and updated guidance for Community Consensus Grants.
For me, it was important that, for projects already underway, the operations staff managing them were available until their completion. Having experience in project management on an industrial scale, I know that the project isn't finished until the paperwork is done, and it is likely that completion would still be underway in 2028.
column10: My experience is WYCIS is pretty straightforward albeit the UI isn't very intuitive, but maybe donations and expenditures over my campaign aren't very complicated. Perhaps when this campaign season is over, my perspective may change. I'm not that familiar with controversy about our reporting system.

column2: Green River, 50 years, 50 years, Steubenville, Ohio
column4:
I graduated from Green River High School in 1987 and proudly served our nation in the United States Marine Corps. After my military service, I spent more than 22 years with the Sweetwater County Sheriff's Office, serving as a law enforcement officer and Bomb Squad Commander.

I previously had the honor of serving as State Representative for House District 60, where I served on the Joint Judiciary Committee and the Joint Transportation, Highways and Military Affairs Committee.

As a Marine, law enforcement officer, business owner, and legislator, I understand the importance of public safety, responsible government, fiscal accountability, and economic opportunity. I have remained active in veterans' organizations and community service because I believe leadership is about serving others.

These experiences have prepared me to stand up for Wyoming values, protect our freedoms, and ensure government remains accountable to the people it serves.
column5:
Wyoming's biggest challenge is strengthening and diversifying our economy while protecting the industries that built our state, including energy, mining, agriculture, and tourism. We must create opportunities that keep young people in Wyoming and attract new businesses without sacrificing our values.

Another challenge is affordability. Rising property taxes, housing costs, healthcare expenses, and inflation continue to impact families, seniors, and small businesses.

Our greatest opportunity is Wyoming's people, abundant resources, and strong communities. By supporting workforce development, vocational education, infrastructure, and responsible economic growth, we can build a stronger future.

As a legislator, I will focus on protecting taxpayers, supporting public safety, defending local control, encouraging job creation, and ensuring government remains accountable, efficient, and focused on serving the people of Wyoming.
column6:
Healthcare affordability and access are major concerns, especially in rural Wyoming where many residents must travel long distances for care. The Legislature should focus on practical solutions that increase access while controlling costs.

I support efforts to strengthen Wyoming's healthcare workforce by attracting and retaining doctors, nurses, mental health professionals, and other providers, particularly in rural communities. We should also reduce unnecessary regulations that drive up costs and encourage innovation such as telehealth services where appropriate.

At the same time, we must work to protect rural hospitals and healthcare facilities that serve as lifelines for many communities. My goal is to improve access to quality healthcare, increase provider availability, and promote policies that help make healthcare more affordable for Wyoming families, seniors, and small businesses while remaining fiscally responsible.
column7:
I support keeping Wyoming lands in public hands. Our public lands are one of Wyoming's greatest assets, providing opportunities for hunting, fishing, recreation, grazing, energy development, tourism, and public access. These lands belong to the people and should remain available for future generations.

I will oppose efforts to transfer or sell public lands that could limit public access or place an undue burden on Wyoming taxpayers. I support responsible, multiple-use management that balances recreation, agriculture, energy production, wildlife conservation, and economic development.

I will work to ensure local communities have a strong voice in land management decisions and advocate for better coordination between state, local, and federal agencies. My goal is to keep Wyoming lands productive, accessible, and managed in the best interests of the people of Wyoming, not outside special interests.
column8:
Property tax relief is important, but it must be done responsibly. No two Wyoming counties are the same, and many local governments rely heavily on property tax revenue to fund essential services such as public safety, roads, emergency response, and other community needs.

I will work to provide meaningful tax relief while protecting counties, municipalities, and special districts from unfunded mandates or sudden revenue losses that could force cuts to services. I believe local communities are best positioned to understand their own financial needs, and any tax relief plan should include safeguards that prevent shifting the burden onto local governments.

My goal is to find a balanced solution that provides relief to taxpayers while ensuring our counties, cities, towns, and special districts can continue delivering the services Wyoming families depend on.
column9:
The Wyoming Legislature has an important role in creating an environment where businesses can succeed and jobs can grow. While government should not pick winners and losers, it can help attract investment by maintaining sound infrastructure, supporting workforce development, and ensuring regulations are reasonable and predictable.

I believe taxpayer dollars must be spent responsibly. Any economic development incentives or programs should be transparent, accountable, and focused on delivering measurable benefits for Wyoming communities. Our goal should be to attract businesses that create good-paying jobs, expand our tax base, and provide long-term economic opportunities.

I will support responsible investments that encourage businesses to locate and grow in Wyoming while protecting taxpayers and ensuring government remains a good steward of public funds.
column10:
I believe campaign finance should be transparent, ethical, and open to public scrutiny. I support campaign contributions that are fully disclosed and available for voters to review, but I have serious concerns about dark money organizations that hide the true source of political spending.

I also believe there should be a clear separation between fundraising and legislative duties. Contributions should not be solicited, given, or received during legislative proceedings. The incident commonly referred to as "Checkgate" undermined public confidence in government and demonstrated why strong ethical standards are necessary.

I support the additional rules and safeguards adopted by the Governor, Senate, and House following that incident. Wyoming's campaign finance system should continue to emphasize transparency, accountability, and public trust so voters can have confidence that elected officials are serving the people, not special interests.

column2: Casper, born and raised,
column4:
•	Attended Casper schools graduated KWHS
•	Graduated Casper College and Chadron State College  
•	Casper Police Department 1980 – 2000
•	Masters degree in public administration from UW 
•	Retired as Interim Chief of Police 
•	Faculty Casper College - since 2000 Wyoming Faculty Member of the Year 2018
•	Married to Megan since 1981 with six children five of whom live in Wyoming 

2018 elected to Wyoming legislature 
2019/2020 –  Member of the House Judiciary Committee 
2021/2022 – Vice chairman of the House Judiciary Committee 
2023/2024 – Chairman of the House Judiciary Committee
		        Member of the House Rules Committee
		        Co-chairman Wyoming State Shooting Complex Oversight Task Force
2025/2026 – Chairman of the House Judiciary Committee 
                        Co-chairman Wyoming State Shooting Complex Oversight Task Force
                        Chairman of the House Special Investigative Committee
9 sponsored bills and great many co-sponsored bills are now law!
column5:
Challenges involve - workforce housing, good paying jobs and a skilled workforce.  Another huge issue is aging.  Also the lack of water and the condition of the highways .  

Uranium, rare earth minerals, tourism, outdoor recreation, renewed demand for coal and continued demand for oil, natural gas, helium and even gold are opportunities.  Our investment portfolio and pension funds are the envy of most states.  

We don't control all our challenges.  Our workforce can be enhanced by expanded public private partnerships.  The legislature must support the community colleges.  The Hathaway Scholarships need to be expanded. The Wyoming Community Development Authority and community banks will play a role in easing the housing crunch.   

Regardless of these, the greatest challenge we face is the growing national debt.
column6: This is a complex problem that defies a simple answer.  Transfering the cost to government, is a possibility but it is expensive.  Relying on funding from a national government that will soon be $40 Trillion in debt is not sound sustainable.  We have taken preliminary steps to create a Physician Assistant degree program at the University.   The legislature has been adjusting the scope of practice for professionals like optometrists, pharmacists and athletic trainers.  There is more work to be done.  Given the situation, it is my opinion that every healthcare professional ought to be able to perform all of the practices that they are trained and capable of doing.   We lose practitioners when we limit their scope of practice despite their skills. Travelling health care might make sense.  Alaska has a model that might work here.  They rely on paraprofessional health aides, telehealth and air ambulances from hub hospitals.  Midwives should also be a bigger part of the health care system.
column7: Selling off the public lands in a wholesale fashion is a horrible idea.  There may be, however, a few parcels of public land that, after public discussion and study, might make sense to sell or transfer.  We already do something similar from time to time.  For example, sometimes we can trade parcels of state land or school trust land so that the affected land owners, the public and the state all benefit.  If the trade gives public access to an otherwise inaccessible tract of land, that might be a good trade.  If the trade puts several sections of public land side by side and accessible instead of individual parcels scattered and largely inaccessible to sportsmen and recreationists, that could be a smart trade.  If we can identify a few parcels of public land to sell and the interested parties agree, having a blanket prohibition on sales or transfers would not make sense.   Once again, the answer is somewhere in between the extremes.
column8: We had a proposal in 2025 that had great merit which we voted for and then reversed course.  The idea was to base the correction in property taxes to the percentage of tax increases that had actually occurred in each county.  Hypothetically, let's say a county had experienced only a 10% increase in property values during the years when property values and consequently property tax payments had exploded, why provide a 25% reduction?  We also amended the Constitution to allow residential property to be a separate class.  There is more we can do with that
column9:
We have over the years created legislative mandates for the Wyoming Business Council.  Review all of that and retain what makes sense today and repeal the rest.  The free market approach that many long for is simply no longer a reality. If Wyoming becomes the only state without such an operation, we will not compete with other states that are more than happy to provide incentives.  

One thing we should not do is set a precedent that whenever major industrial projects want to operate in our state the legislature must give a thumbs up.  That would make attracting businesses to our state, very difficult.  

The legislature should set up processes within the executive branch and local governments and then get out of the way.  Only if such a process is found to need correction, should the legislature get involved and only to the extent to correct the process, not rule on individual projects.
column10:
The system is fine.  The individuals giving and receiving donations need to look in the mirror and do the right thing.  Voters need to ask candidates during a campaign how much PAC money they are accepting and who are the folks making the big donations?  The idea that folks are spending $40,000 and up for a legislative race is NUTS!  

Voters can reward candidates who spend great sums or they can reject them.  If voters don't want dozens of oversized full color glossy postcards in their mailbox throughout a campaign season, let the candidates know!   If high dollar attack ads work to win campaigns, we will see more high dollar attack ads.  If voters see such ads for the manipulation attempts that they are, we can rein them in.  The best candidate may not be the one who can afford a great deal of advertising.  

I received a campaign check before the 2026 session and before I had declared my candidacy.  I sent the check back.  That captures my values.

column2: We currently live at 1974 Harrison Street, Laramie.  We moved into the new house Nov. 28 1998 and have lived there since then. We lived in Rawlins prior to moving to Laramie April 14, 1997/July 1, 1997 (I worked for ACSO, but didn't get the family moved until July 1, 1997.  I was still preaching full time in Rawlins and travelled between the two until the family move and training academy was completed in mid June 1997.  I was born in Scott City, Kansas
column3: I will be 76 years old on election day.
column4:
Gary Wilken is a retired United Methodist pastor, law enforcement professional, and lifelong public servant in communities across Colorado and Wyoming.
Gary earned a B.A. in History from Colorado State University and a Master of Divinity from the Pacific School of Religion, where he also trained as a police chaplain and served with the Oakland Police Department Reserve Program.
Throughout his career, Gary has united ministry and public service, pastoring rural congregations, advocating for environmental protections in eastern Colorado, and serving the Albany County Sheriff's Office as a detention deputy, corporal, and dual-certified patrol officer. He later worked as Albany County's Code Enforcement Officer, addressing public safety, zoning, and hazardous waste concerns alongside residents and state agencies.
Gary and his wife, Bernice, have been married 46 years, building a life rooted in faith, family, and service- values that continue to guide his leadership today.
column5: The biggest challenges facing Wyoming today are the issues of training and providing housing, healthcare, education, a living wage and care for the environment that houses us.  As a legislator I will engage in continuing education and investigation of these issues with my colleagues in the legislature, and offer constuctive changes to meet our challenges through cooperation and compassionate care, listening, and discussion.
column6: The legislature and citizens of wyoming need to come together to study, share information and diversify many of the approaches to medical, educational, and systemic challenges.
column7: I do not support the sell or transfer of public land.  I call for stewardship and responsible care for the public lands that are under our responsibility.  We as citizens of Wyoming need to accept the responsibility to care for as well as enjoy the local, state and federal lands we have within our state boundaries.
column8: As a legislator I will respect the property rights of our citizens while recognizing the need to provide taxes and programs to care for public land.  I have experienced the cutbacks of limited income that we have gone through in Albany County, with significant Public lands that do not pay taxes in Wyoming.  A PILT (Payment in Lieu of Taxes) program helps level the use of state and federal lands to meet the growing costs of living in Albany County
column9: The legislature needs to continue being in dialogue with the business community, as well as the extractive recource users, the manufacturing and research and healthy growth of our state
column10: I appreciate financial support from our constituents, but will try to limit contributions to a equitable level so that all persons have input but not domination of free choice.

column2: Cheyenne, Wyoming. I have lived here for three years. I am a Wyoming native and grew up in Farson, Wyoming. I was born in Rock Springs, WY.
column4: My Wyoming roots run deep. Raised in Farson, I have an appreciation for hard work, independence, and service. My experience in business, community engagement, and policy shaped my belief that our future should be built by the people who live, work, and raise families here. I am proud of the Wyoming way of life and I believe our families want safe communities, strong economies, protected freedoms, and conservative leaders who will fight for their values. I am running for Wyoming State Senate to bring principled, conservative leadership. As a wife, mother, and community leader, I understand the values that make our state strong: faith, family, freedom, and a deep respect for the people who call Wyoming home. I believe we deserves leaders who listen, stand firm in their values, and work collaboratively to get things done. I am committed to protecting our freedoms, defending constitutional rights, supporting local communities, and ensuring state government works for the people it serves.
column5:
Wyoming shaped me. Growing up in here, I learned hard work, self-reliance, and what it means to be part of a community. That was my Wyoming — and I want it to still be Wyoming when my daughter grows up.
Keeping Wyoming strong for our youth means protecting their constitutional freedoms. It means keeping parents in charge of their children's education, not bureaucrats. It means building an economy where they can actually stay — protecting our energy industry, growing opportunity, and making housing affordable so our kids don't have to leave the state they love.
When I look at my daughter, I see every Wyoming kid who deserves to grow up free, safe, and full of possibility. That is what I am fighting for.
column6:
Wyoming is a big state with small towns, and healthcare access is a real challenge. Here is how I would address it.
Expand telehealth so rural families can access quality care without driving hours to see a provider. Grow our healthcare workforce by continuing to build out programs and capacity at our colleges and creating support to recruit and retain providers across the state. Protect our hospitals — they are lifelines for small Wyoming communities and state policy should support them, not burden them. And increase competition and transparency so patients have options and can afford the care they need.
Healthcare solutions that work elsewhere don’t work for Wyoming. Our communities should have the flexibility to address their unique needs without one-size-fits-all mandates from the federal government.
Accessible healthcare isn't about expanding government — it's about removing barriers so Wyoming families can get the care they need, close to home, at a price they can afford.
column7:
I do not. Wyoming's public lands are part of who we are — where we hunt, fish, ranch, and make our living. Keeping them public means every Wyoming family has access to the land that defines this state.
But keeping land public is not enough. Federal lands require proper management and investment, and that means Wyoming needs a real seat at the table. Too often, decisions about our land are made by people in Washington who have never set foot here.
I believe in collaboration — where Wyoming's ranchers, energy producers, sportsmen, and local communities have a direct voice in how our federal lands are managed. We know this land. We depend on it. We are its best stewards.
Managed well, public lands support grazing, energy, recreation, and tourism — all of which matter deeply to Wyoming families. The goal is not less management. It is smarter, locally informed management that reflects Wyoming's values.
column8:
Wyoming's tax structure needs more than quick fixes — it needs a long-term, comprehensive review that looks at the full picture.
I support a measured approach that does two things at once: delivers real relief to Wyoming families and businesses, and ensures our state can still fund the services people depend on. Those goals are not in conflict — but achieving both requires honest, thorough work rather than reactive legislation.
Wyoming has always been a low-tax state and I intend to keep it that way. Smart tax policy is not just about cutting — it is about making sure every dollar collected is spent wisely and the burden is distributed fairly.
Wyoming families and businesses deserve a tax system they can understand, predict, and trust. That starts with a serious, data-driven review and a commitment to reform that puts people first.
column9:
The legislature's role in economic development is to create conditions where Wyoming businesses can grow and thrive. That means smart infrastructure investment, a predictable regulatory environment, and support that empowers communities.
The Wyoming Business Council needs to evolve into a clear, outcome-driven model with defined goals, measurable results, and real accountability. This work must be built from the ground up. Local communities know their assets and gaps better than anyone in Cheyenne.
Workforce development is a key driver this conversation, which is what I work toward daily in my career. Businesses cannot grow without people, and people cannot stay without opportunity. Infrastructure — roads, broadband, water, and energy — is the foundation that makes all of it possible.
Wyoming deserves leadership that sets clear priorities, tracks outcomes, and works hand in hand with local communities to build an economy built to last.
column10:
Campaign donations are an investment in the democratic process — nothing more, nothing less. When someone donates to my campaign, they are helping fund the practical work of running for office: yard signs, mailers, events, and the tools needed to reach voters across Senate District 5. That is what donations do. They do not buy influence, they do not guarantee alignment, and they absolutely do not purchase a vote.
I answer to the people of Senate District 5 — all of them — not to any donor, organization, or outside interest. Every decision I make in the State Senate will be guided by what is right for my constituents and for Wyoming.
Wyoming's campaign laws are not stringent like other states, and I hope we can continue on a process that keeps government out of the way. CheckGate challenges Wyoming's values, but I do not believe we need more regulation, we need Wyoming voters to come to the polls on August 18th. That you for your consideration and support!

column2: Rock Springs, Wyoming all my life. 40 years.
column4: Bio from my website is fine.
column5:
Wyoming’s biggest challenge is creating opportunities for future generations while protecting the industries and values that define us. Energy, mining, agriculture, and construction remain vital, and we must support them while responsibly diversifying our economy.

We can grow without losing what makes Wyoming special. That means investing in education, creating good paying jobs, and strengthening communities so young people can build their futures here.

As a legislator, I support policies that encourage growth and innovation while avoiding unnecessary burdens on our core industries. Continued investment in infrastructure, workforce development, and education is essential to keeping Wyoming competitive.

At the same time, we must preserve our open spaces, wildlife, and strong sense of community. Wyoming doesn’t need to change its identity to succeed we can grow while staying true to who we are.
column6:
Healthcare affordability and access are challenges for families across Wyoming, especially in rural communities. We must ensure our rural hospitals, clinics and providers remain viable so Wyomingites can access care close to home. We also need to recruit and retain healthcare professionals by supporting training opportunities and reducing barriers for providers who want to practice in Wyoming.

Increasing competition can help lower costs and expand choices. Wyoming's small population often means limited insurance options. I believe we should strongly encourage our federal delegation to pursue reforms that allow health insurance to be sold across state lines, giving Wyomingites access to more plans and greater competition in the marketplace.

Our focus should be on practical, fiscally responsible solutions that improve access, strengthen local healthcare systems and help make healthcare more affordable for Wyoming families.
column7:
For the most part, I believe in keeping public lands in public hands. Growing up in southwest Wyoming has taught me there can be exceptions, but they should be limited, practical, and locally driven.
When communities need room to grow, there should be a reasonable process for acquiring small parcels of public land. Land exchanges should remain an option when they benefit the public, and private landowners should not be unfairly landlocked.
The key is balance. Wyoming has a long history of cooperation among landowners, local governments, industry, recreationists, and public land managers. We should continue that approach and ensure decisions reflect Wyoming’s needs and values.
I do not support opening large swaths of public land for sale or transfer. Public lands are an important part of Wyoming’s economy, heritage, and way of life.
column8:
Wyoming's counties are not all the same, and a one size fits all approach to property tax relief can create unintended consequences. I support providing meaningful property tax relief to homeowners, but we must also recognize that property taxes fund essential local services such as schools, roads, law enforcement, fire protection, and other community needs.

My approach is to pursue targeted, sustainable relief while giving local governments the flexibility to address their unique circumstances. Any reduction in property tax revenue should be carefully evaluated to avoid simply shifting the burden elsewhere or forcing cuts to critical services.

It would be foolhardy to chase property tax relief only to create the conditions for an income tax or ever-increasing fees and assessments to make up the difference. Wyoming's competitive advantage has long been its low tax environment, and we should be careful not to trade one tax burden for another.
column9:
The Wyoming Legislature has an important role in economic development, but government should be a partner, not the driving force. Our job is to maintain a tax and regulatory environment that encourages investment, job creation, and long-term growth.
Wyoming should have someone there to answer the phone when opportunities come calling. We need to be responsive, competitive, and ready to showcase what our state has to offer. That means supporting workforce development, investing in critical infrastructure, and ensuring permitting processes are predictable and efficient.
Economic development is not about picking winners and losers. It is about creating an environment where businesses of all sizes can grow and succeed. Wyoming's low-tax, pro-business climate is one of our greatest strengths, and we should continue building on it while protecting our quality of life.
column10:
Transparency in campaign donations is essential. Voters should know where candidates’ funding comes from to make informed decisions.

Wyoming’s system offers some transparency, but it can be improved. The Legislature should continue reviewing it thoughtfully.

I support clear disclosure rules that inform voters while respecting constitutional rights and avoiding unnecessary burdens. Open campaigns and accurate information help build public trust.

column2: I live in Jackson and have been here since 2008. Previously, I lived in Saratoga and Cheyenne, and have lived in Wyoming most of my adult life and spent summers in Saratoga as a child. I was born in Cleveland, OH.
column4:
I am the Democratic state representative for Wyoming House District 23 and the President and CEO of the George B. Storer Foundation. I was first elected in 2022 to represent Teton County, where I focus on practical, bipartisan solutions that reflect Teton County values and strengthen communities across our state.

I lead my family’s foundation, which supports early childhood education, climate and conservation work, and Wyoming communities, and I’ve helped modernize its governance, investment policy, and strategic direction since 2008. A lifelong angler and conservationist, I was the first woman to serve on the U.S. Fly Fishing Team and was honored with the Theodore Roosevelt Conservation Partnership’s Conservation Leadership Award in 2019.

I hold BA and MFA degrees from the University of Southern California’s School of Cinema, and I bring that storytelling and strategic perspective to my legislative and philanthropic work.
column5:
Wyoming’s biggest challenges are economic dependence on boom‑and‑bust fossil fuel revenues, workforce and housing pressures, and protecting land, water, and communities amid rapid change. Our opportunities lie in diversifying the economy, investing in people and infrastructure, and leveraging world‑class natural assets and communities like Teton County as innovation hubs.

As a legislator, I would:

Pass a long‑term fiscal plan that diversifies revenue and supports local governments.

Invest in education, job training, childcare, broadband and cell service so Wyomingites can thrive in new industries.

Tackle housing and land‑use policy so workers and families can afford to live where they work.

Protect public schools, clean water, and public lands as pillars of our economy and quality of life.

Model civil, solutions‑focused leadership rooted in Wyoming values.


.
column6:
As a legislator, I would do the following: 

Expand Medicaid with a Wyoming‑specific plan to cover thousands of low‑income adults, cut uncompensated care and stabilize premiums and rural hospitals.

Use the new federal Rural Health Transformation dollars to keep local hospitals, EMS and clinics open and invest in telehealth, behavioral health and primary care.

Raise targeted Medicaid reimbursement rates (maternity care, EMS, rural providers) so more doctors and hospitals can afford to serve Wyoming patients.

Spur competition and innovation in the insurance market, including exploring a high‑risk/catastrophic pool and stronger rate review to push down premiums.

Grow a homegrown healthcare workforce through loan‑repayment, training partnerships and incentives for providers to practice in rural and frontier communities.
column7:
I do not support large‑scale sale or transfer of Wyoming’s public lands to private interests or away from public ownership. Our lands are the backbone of our economy, outdoor heritage and communities, and once they’re sold, we don’t get them back. 

As a legislator, I would:

Oppose public‑land sell‑offs and support keeping lands public, open and accessible.

Ensure state trust lands are managed to grow long‑term value for school and other beneficiaries while balancing conservation and community needs on the ground.

Back “multiple use, local voice” management that balances grazing, hunting, recreation, conservation and responsible energy with real input from Wyoming communities.

Require agencies to plan for climate‑driven drought and wildfire and invest in forests, rangelands and watersheds to build long‑term resilience for people, wildlife and local economies.
column8:
I support targeted relief that keeps people from being taxed out of their homes without blowing a hole in county and school budgets.

As a legislator, I helped champion Constitutional Amendment A, which gives us a constitutional way to lower taxes on owner‑occupied primary homes, not second homes or commercial property. By contrast, the 4% cap is delivering the biggest breaks to wealthy second‑homeowners and creating huge disparities in how similar properties are taxed, which raises serious questions about whether our system is still fair, uniform and equal under the Wyoming Constitution. 

Going forward, I’d lean on Amendment A, targeted state‑funded relief tools, and fairer state‑local revenue sharing so every county can tailor solutions that protect primary‑residence homeowners while keeping schools, EMS and local services strong.
column9: The Legislature absolutely has a role in smart economic development that serves Wyoming people, not just the next boom. I believe we should be more aggressive and strategic in using a portion of our permanent funds for rotating, low‑interest loans through SLIB and the Business Council to build community infrastructure, workforce housing and other critical projects the private market is struggling to or can't finance on its own. Done prudently, these investments keep the funds intact, recycle dollars through Wyoming communities, help grow our workforce, keep more of our kids and grandkids here and diversify the economy. By investing in our communities and people, new industries will be more inclined to consider Wyoming as a place to grow a business.
column10:
I believe transparency and accountability in campaign finance are essential to earning and keeping the public’s trust. I support taking donations that reflect Wyoming values, disclosing them clearly and on time, and refusing money that comes with strings attached or undermines public confidence.

Wyoming’s current disclosure system is better than it used to be but still has big gaps, especially around late, hard‑to‑track contributions and outside "dark money" spending. I think the Legislature should strengthen reporting rules so voters can easily see who is funding campaigns before they cast their ballots, improve enforcement and auditing, and bring more sunlight to dark‑money “issue” campaigns that are really about influencing elections.

column2: I currently live in eastern Laramie County and have lived here for 10 years. I have lived in Wyoming since 2005 and was born in Victoria, TX.
column4: I grew up on a cattle ranch, an experience that led me to Texas Tech University, where I earned a degrees in Range Science, focusing my graduate research on prescribed fire and grazing. Today, my husband, Jason, and I own and operate our ranch in Laramie County and a consulting company. Through consulting, I provide expertise in agriculture, conservation, and policy. Before focusing on our businesses, I served as Executive Director of the Wyoming Stock Growers Land Trust and as a policy advisor to former Wyoming Governor Matt Mead. I’ve also worked with the Western Landowners Alliance, the WY Department of Agriculture, the Agricultural Research Service, and Northwest College. Across these roles, I’ve worked to keep working lands productive and economically viable. The values that guide this work—practical problem solving, respect for local knowledge, and a commitment to community—have broad application beyond land management and would serve me well in the legislature.
column5:
Wyoming is built on hard work, strong communities, and the responsible use of our natural resources. We face real challenges — from pressures on our core industries to the need for a more resilient, diverse economy and the increasing outmigration of our young people. But these challenges also create opportunities to strengthen what we already do well and build new pathways that keep our kids and grandkids here at home.
As a legislator, I would bring my experience working with landowners, businesses, and local governments to support the industries that define Wyoming while encouraging thoughtful growth and diversification. Our future depends on good jobs, strong schools, and communities where families can put down roots and thrive.
I believe we can honor the values that make Wyoming what it is while building something that lasts — an economy that works for working people, an education system that prepares the next generation, and communities that remain resilient and connected.
column6:
The rising cost of healthcare and the difficulty of accessing services are real challenges for Wyoming families, especially in rural communities. We need reforms that reduce costs, expand access, and meet people where they are. The Legislature can help by exploring opportunities such as expanded telemedicine, increasing the number of providers, and strengthening home health and in home support options.
As a legislator, I would work closely with the members of the 2026 Health Insurance Affordability Task Force and other partners to fully understand the barriers Wyoming citizens face and to develop practical solutions to deliver the quality healthcare our communities deserve.
column7:
I do not support broad efforts to sell or transfer Wyoming’s public lands. Our federal lands provide real value to the people of this state — from recreation and hunting to livestock grazing and mineral development. I support the multiple use concept and would continue working to ensure these lands remain productive, accessible working lands. Wyoming’s state trust lands are also an important asset, offering open space, recreation, grazing opportunities, and critical revenue for our schools. 
While I oppose sweeping efforts to dispose of public or state lands, I recognize there may be individual situations where selling or transferring a parcel is clearly in the best interest of Wyoming’s people and those opportunities should be carefully considered.
I believe in keeping working lands working and understand the balance required across our state to manage them responsibly for future generations.
column8:
Property taxes must be continually refined so people can afford to buy homes, stay in their homes, and build equity and pride of place. At the same time, I recognize that every Wyoming county depends on a different mix of revenue, and our core industries—as well as efforts to diversify our economy—rely on stable, predictable local funding.
Taxes play a critical role in maintaining the infrastructure we depend on: roads, schools, emergency services, and more. Wyoming is a revenue generating state, and we must continue supporting programs that strengthen our economy and serve our communities well.
I am not interested in raising taxes, but I do believe we need honest, informed conversations about how we provide relief while keeping counties functional. That means exploring responsible adjustments that help homeowners who need relief—without jeopardizing essential services.
column9:
The Legislature plays an important role in strengthening economic development. At the forefront of that role is creating clear, consistent processes and regulations so businesses have confidence when they look to Wyoming. We should streamline and reduce cumbersome regulations and permitting timelines that slow down investment.
Economic development also depends on maintaining and improving critical infrastructure and supporting workforce development so Wyoming has the skilled workers our industries need. Careful investment of state revenues through a balanced budget is essential to sustaining these efforts over the long term.
When these pieces work together—predictable regulations, strong infrastructure, a skilled workforce, and responsible budgeting—they have the potential to positively stimulate Wyoming’s economy. I can be part of the solution by helping create a stable, reliable environment that encourages growth while protecting taxpayers.
column10:
I appreciate every campaign donation because it represents trust in the work I’m doing and the future we’re building for Wyoming. When someone chooses to give, they’re partnering in a vision—not trying to influence it. I take that seriously.
Currently, I do not see a need to revise Wyoming’s system for monitoring campaign donations and expenditures.

column2: I live in Worland. I grew in Worland but moved away to go to law school. I returned to Worland in 2001
column4:
I grew up in Worland and graduated from WHS. I also graduated from UW with a degree in Political Science and graduated Baylor Law School. I was a business litigator in Houston for many years until we began our journey back west.

I loved growing up in Worland, where I first learned about the importance of being a good neighbor and of getting involved in the life of my community. As an adult, I began to realize that communities like Worland don't happen by accident. They happen because good people devote their time and talent to make the community a better place. Their selfless commitment makes communities great! 

When I moved back to Worland, I wanted to give back to the community. I was honored to serve for many years on the Washakie Museum Board and was President of the board when we build the new museum. I also served on the Washakie Development Ass Board,  Northwest Community College Foundation board and Washakie Leadership – an initiative to develop local leaders.
column5:
1. Using our minerals resources to create new opportunities that provide more good paying jobs for Wyoming Citizens
2. Workforce development 
3. Mental Health services particularly for our youth
4. Rising cost of healthcare
5. Strengthening local communities
6. Aging infrastructure 
7. Affordable Housing

I would approach solving these problems in this way - Gather relevant, unbiased information,   listen to the publics' concerns or ideas, consider which level of government should be leading the way, work with other elected officials to craft the best policy for Wyoming.
column6: This is an important challenge. I believe the new Rural Health Transformation Program is giving Wyoming an opportunity to address issues related to access. This  is an important first step in addressing rising costs. The state legislature must take a deep dive on other causes for the steep rise in healthcare costs. When state regulations are identified  as a reason for added cost, the legislature will need to address that. When state reimbursement rates are an issue, we need to consider adjusting those rates. Some of the solutions to rising healthcare costs must come from the federal level as Medicare and Medicaid are federal programs.
column7: I believe we should keep public lands in public hands. Wyoming needs to have more input in the management of federal lands. State lands are to be used by the state to generate revenue. It is the fiduciary duty of those who manage state lands to produce income.
column8: As a legislator representing two hardship counties that have been adversely affected by the property tax cuts, I am very concerned about further cuts. I believe it is important for the citizens of each county to understand how any additional cuts will affect their local government services including special districts. I am disappointed with efforts by some to disconnect local taxes from local services.
column9: The legislature's role begins with appropriating adequate funding for economic development. The legislature also has a role in guiding the focus and priorities of economic development through statutory changes. The executive branch also has a role to play as well.
column10: I would support more transparency in campaign donations. I believe the priority should be protecting the people of Wyoming from out of state dark money. Greater transparency is the key. However, some of that transparency must come from the federal government changing the rules under 503(c) of the Federal Tax Code. Additionally, when groups fail to comply with Wyoming campaign finance laws, there should be stiffer penalties.

column2: I am a 5th generation Campbell County Native .
column4: I have worked in the Coal, Oil, and Gas industries for over 45 years and have ran a small cattle and sheep operation for 20 plus years on my grandparents' homestead. I have formed the Wyoming Constitutionalist, and was chairman of the Campbell County Constitution Party. I have served in the Wyoming House of Representatives in 2021 and 2022 and feel a need to return as Data Centers and Nuclear energy are taking center stage using Presidential Executive Orders that are unconstitutional. I feel we need to slow walk Wyoming through these industries so future generations can enjoy Wyoming as much as I have.
column5: Executive Orders forcing Data Centers and Nuclear on Wyoming. Executive Orders would have to be addressed as Unconstitutional and needs to be repealed.
column6: All cost for goods and services is a by product of devaluation due to inflation created since 1913 to June 2026, there is about 97% decline in the dollar. Allow more competition in the healthcare sector.
column7: Article 1 Section 8 of the United States Constitution says, what the federal government can own, and it is very little. I believe we should turn to the homestead act using a lottery system to pick parcels for citizens, not corporations, to get public land and minerals back into the hands of private ownership.
column8: I believe in property tax elimination with a consumption tax to make up the difference only after all hidden taxes savings and investment accounts are revealed.
column9: If a business comes to Wyoming it should have a business plan that does not require State and Federal hand outs, therefore the State should not be picking winners and losers with economic development using the citizens tax dollars.
column10: I pay for my own campaigns out of my pocket and do not have to answer to others about what I say or do. I believe there could be more transparency for PACS and Super PACS.

column2: I lived at 6225 Mountainview Drive, Cheyenne, WY since 2005.  I am a 4th generation Wyomingite, born and raised in Lander. I have lived in Cheyenne for 28 years and in Fremont County for over 35 years.
column4: I am a graduate of Lander Valley High School and the University of Wyoming.  While growing up I worked on the family ranch feeding cows before class.  My first job out of law school was clerking for the district court in Lander.  In 1985 we moved to Cheyenne where I worked in the Attorney General's office.  Two of our three kids were born in Cheyenne.  We moved to Riverton in 1990 where I opened my own law practice.  In 2005 the family moved back to Cheyenne. I continue practicing law today. I have served in the Wyoming legislature since 2011 representing Cheyenne and Laramie County.  My service to the State has been one of the great honors of my life.
column5: There are challenges and opportunities wherever you look.  To name a few:  Health care desserts coupled with ever increasing medical costs; community impacts from multiple property tax relief proposals with no backfill; Aging community infrastructure coupled with declining and aging populations and shrinking local revenues; fewer children and the less school resources due to legislative actions.  Despite such challenges, Wyoming remains one of the best kept secrets in America.  We have the people and natural resources to secure our way of life now and for years to come.  We have the lowest individual tax burden in the U.S.  Wyoming is consistently ranked as one of the best managed state governments in the country.  We do more with less.
