We have a balanced and stable state budget, one of the best school programs in the world and developable natural resources to protect our standard of living and Wyoming way of life for generations to come.
column6: We work on aspects of this problem every session. Health care related matters constitute the largest component of the State budget.  This is a all hands on deck issue. This includes ensuring adequate funding our healthcare workforce, reducing red tape to direct more time and dollars for direct care, expanding telehealth services and supporting rural emergency service districts.  We have funded new construction for the State Hospital in Evanston and Life Resource Center in Lander.  We have provide grants and matching funds of rural hospitals an clinics ARPA dollars for rural hospitals like the new hospital in Riverton.  Much more must be done, directly and indirectly.  Vibrant, attractive communities draw medical professional to Wyoming.  Maintaining existing services keeps providers here. All of us must continue to work together to protect our citizens and communities across the state.
column7: No, with minor exceptions, such as adding state land to national parks (like the Kelly parcel) and potential small building sites to facilitate the construction of low-income employee housing.
column8: We are the lowest taxing state in U.S. Our schools, local governments and special district rely on property tax revenue to provide essential services and maintain our standard of living for all of Wyoming. Further cuts will impact our ability to retain good health, quality education and essential services. Everyone loses.
column9: To compete in regional and national markets, bring in new business, and help expand existing industries fostering economic development is a fundament building block for Wyoming's future.
column10: Yes.  The political terrain in Wyoming has morphed in the past several years.  Out of state dark money involving negative, misleading, and often patently false statements are common place. Almost overnight it has become a multi-million dollar industry.  Transparency is essential to ensure fair and informed decision making by the public.

column2: I live in Cheyenne.  My life in Wyoming started when I attended UW in 2011.  My wife and I moved to Colorado in 2018, but always knew we would come back to Wyoming.  In 2023, we moved our family to Cheyenne.  We proudly call ourselves Wyoming converts.  I was raised in Parker, CO.
column4: I am a husband, father of three wonderful kids, a teacher, and a strong conservative.  I have been a teacher for eight years and know firsthand many of the issues in our public education system.  My initial degree was in political science and I have experience working as a legislative aide in the Colorado capitol.  I will bring my experience as a teacher, father, and dedicated resident to the position of representative.
column5:
I am optimistic for Wyoming.

With education, the legislature got a lot right in its recent recalibration. There are still many issues facing education and I will bring the unique perspective of an educator to the discussion. To provide a robust public option, curriculum needs to be efficacious and knowledge based. School choice and parental rights will be focuses of my policies.

As a legislator, I will fight for our staple industries. That will involve empowering natural gas, oil, and coal extraction as well as enabling the resurgence of uranium.

In conversations with constituents, data centers have been main concerns I have heard and discussed. While the business of Wyoming is business, Mainstreet shouldn't be diminished for the sake of Wall Street or Silicon Valley. I'll work to maintain Wyoming as the best state to start a business in while working with local governments to ensure that the quality of living and water/energy resources aren’t disregarded in the process.
column6:
Transparency for medical bills should be continued and further emboldened. The Hospital Price Transparency Act was a step in the correct direction with that regard, but SF 57 took its teeth out.
Healthcare access and affordability is a nuanced issue with no singular or direct answer, but I will further explore expanding telehealth services as well as policy to encourage and educate people regarding HSAs. I will consider policies that would integrate HSAs with telehealth, direct primary care, or wellness incentives—building on recent federal changes.
column7: Nearly half of Wyoming is under federal control.  I support Wyoming lands being in Wyoming hands.  That doesn't mean the full privatization of those lands, but I believe that interests and stakes are greater for those with a closer connection to the land than a bureaucrat in Washington.  With red tape and confusing or redundant regulation, when it comes to management of forests and open spaces, the federal government is not the best steward of lands; local or state governments would have leaner, more direct means of managing the lands in the best interests of Wyomingites.
column8: In my conversations with hopeful constituents, nearly all have expressed appreciation for the reduction of property taxes.  Several also have brought up that property taxes feel arbitrary with home values and thus property taxes increasing often outside of their control.  Who can say to own their land or property if they are continually paying taxes that feel like rent to the government?  I am in favor of maintaining or expanding property tax relief, especially for those on fixed incomes.  And, to address any resulting shortfalls, I will first pursue fiscal management approaches such as requesting an expansive audit for glut or redundant programs and pursue spending restraints like capping the growth of state appropriations or overall spending to population growth plus inflation (PGI). I would not rule out the possibility of tax swapping.
column9: Saying that the government ought to play an active role in facilitating economic sounds nice and, in some circumstances, it might be done altruistically in practice.  However, it can seem as if publicly funded institutions that seek to play an active role in economic development end up choosing winners and losers.  I believe the best role a government can play when it comes to economic development is ensuring that there are no laws or rules that make the playing field uneven for anyone and otherwise getting out of the way and allowing for personal innovation and business growth.
column10: I consider campaign donations as an extension or form of the First Amendment.  A private individual or association of individuals in the form of PACs can choose to use their money to indicate whose voice/what issue they support in the public sphere.  Regarding monitoring campaign donations, I believe that the current system is sufficient but should continue to be quick and accurate in its reporting.  The secretary of state's website allows for the viewing and scrutinizing of donations and expenditures that each campaign receives and makes.

column2: I live on the Sommers Ranch along the Green River in Sublette County.  I have lived there my entire 67 years of life. I was born in the hospital in Jackson Wyoming.
column4: I am a life long Sublette County rancher that has served both the rancher community, my local communities, and the State of Wyoming for decades on various boards and commissions.  I served for 12 years in the Wyoming House of Representatives, and I was Speaker of the House for the 67th Legislature (2023-2024).
column5:
At a statewide level, how does Wyoming do a better job of youth retention. This is coupled with the greater challenge of how does Wyoming grow through economic development (providing jobs for Wyoming's youth), while preserving the custom, culture, values and landscapes of Wyoming and its people. Wyoming cannot say no to everything. We have to enable and welcome industries  to Wyoming, but under our terms.  I am a big supporter of local control.  Let local communities decide what is best for them through their local siting regulations.  The State needs to make sure industries don't negatively impact our water, our environment and our electric grid. 

Locally, the issues and politics around the Colorado River and its tributaries are of paramount importance.  Wyoming needs to invest in our Attorney General's office to fight for our water, investment in the State Engineer's office, and invest in water projects in the basin and around the state(including water conservation projects).
column6: Having served 12 years in the Wyoming Legislature, I know that I cannot be an expert in every arena. I think we need to turn to our very competent Department of Health, Wyoming Medical and Insurance professionals, and health advocacy groups to seek solutions that work for Wyoming.  Wyoming's small population creates incredibly difficult challenges for providing low cost healthcare.
column7:
I support SJ0009, Keeping public lands protected and decisions local  - This resolution passed last session. The core of it is this:
  That the Wyoming Legislature opposes any state or federal legislation or policy that promotes the broad or indiscriminate sale or exchange of public lands.
  That the state of Wyoming demands Congress and federal agencies respect existing land management frameworks, county land-use plans and the voices of local communities.
  That the Wyoming Legislature supports responsible, locally supported land exchanges, adjustments or disposals under existing laws and procedures when they demonstrably enhance public access, natural resource use or community benefit.
  That the Wyoming Legislature supports adequately funding state and federal agencies and the corresponding programs that exist so that the agencies can efficiently and effectively review land purchases, exchanges or limited disposals under the existing legal framework.
column8: I prioritized property tax relief during my term as Speaker of the House, we passed at least five pieces of legislation designed for tax relief. Subsequently, the Legislature has passed even more relief.  We are now at a point where we are impacting local government services and funding for education. Any property tax relief going forward must be carefully weighed against its impacts to local government and education funding. Property taxes was how Wyoming's founders, literally its embedded in our Constitution, funded local government and education. Property taxes is embedded in so many areas of the law that eliminating them would require years of work.  How would we replace those revenues and with an increase in what kind of tax? I believe I have the experience to understand these big picture issues, and how to navigate the best solutions.
column9: I support the Wyoming Business Council and the Wyoming Energy Authority. I support both of their efforts to attract business to Wyoming. We need jobs to retain our youth. Every state in the nation does this. I do agree that  we need to take a hard look at the Wyoming Business Council to determine what is working, what is not working, and how we should move the Council to best serve Wyoming.
column10: I accept personal donations to my campaign from individuals.  I am NOT currently taking any PAC money for my campaign.  I have done it both ways in past elections. I think Wyoming's system is within the constraints of what the courts will allow.  Any real reform would take a US Constitutional Amendment. I am open to the idea of campaign reform. The Dark Money PACs that have invaded Wyoming politics are making campaigns more expensive and are often misleading voters.

column2: My husband and I have lived just outside Dayton, Wyoming for the past 20 years. I was born in Sheridan and have spent most of my life in Wyoming. Aside from six years in Oregon and Washington immediately after college, I have lived, worked, and built businesses in Wyoming for more than three decades.
column4: I attended Casper College, the University of Oregon and Trend Business College. My professional background includes service in both the public and private sectors. After college I worked as a legal assistant in Washington State. Since returning to Wyoming in 1995 I have worked as the administrative services manager for the Central Wyoming Regional Water System, a public involvement specialist for the Wyoming Department of Transportation, executive director of the Sheridan County Chamber of Commerce, and for more than 30 years as a public process facilitator helping communities navigate complex issues and long-range planning. My husband and I own and operate businesses in Sheridan County, including Innominate Coffeehouse & Bakery and the Lazy R Campground in Ranchester, and The Outpost in Sheridan. We previously owned and operated a sporting goods store in Ranchester that sold hunting and fishing supplies. Over the years I have served on several nonprofit boards in Sheridan County.
column5:
Wyoming's biggest challenge is keeping the promise of Wyoming within reach for the next generation. Families are facing rising property taxes, healthcare costs, housing pressures, workforce shortages, and concerns about long-term economic stability. At the same time, Wyoming has tremendous opportunities in energy, agriculture, tourism, entrepreneurship, and emerging industries.

As a legislator, I would focus on practical conservative solutions: disciplined budgeting, responsible tax relief, strong infrastructure, workforce development, and policies that support local communities rather than imposing one-size-fits-all mandates from Cheyenne. My career has been spent helping people work through difficult decisions and find common ground. I believe government works best when it listens carefully, solves real problems, and measures success by results rather than rhetoric.
column6:
Healthcare affordability and access are major concerns across Wyoming, especially in rural communities where provider shortages and long travel distances can make care difficult to obtain. The Legislature should focus on strengthening rural hospitals and clinics, expanding telehealth, reducing barriers to recruiting healthcare professionals, and exploring ways to make insurance more affordable for working families and small businesses.

I also believe Wyoming should have an honest, fact-based discussion Wyoming-specific alternatives to Medicaid expansion. The question should not be ideological. It should be whether a policy improves access, supports rural healthcare systems, encourages work and personal responsibility, and delivers value for Wyoming taxpayers.
column7:
I do not support broad efforts to sell or transfer Wyoming's public lands. Public lands are an important part of our economy, outdoor heritage, and way of life. They support grazing, hunting, fishing, recreation, wildlife habitat, and responsible energy development.

That does not mean federal management is always effective. Wyoming should continue pushing for greater local input, responsible multiple-use management, improved access, and decisions that reflect the needs of the people who live and work closest to the land. My goal is to keep public lands productive, accessible, and available for future generations while ensuring Wyoming communities have a meaningful voice in how they are managed.
column8:
Property tax relief is necessary. Many Wyoming homeowners, especially seniors and families on fixed incomes, have seen tax bills rise faster than their ability to pay. People should not be taxed out of homes they worked hard to own.

At the same time, property taxes help fund many of the things that make Wyoming communities strong: local schools, roads, fire protection, law enforcement, hospitals, senior services, libraries, and other essential services. Strong communities don't happen by accident, and neither do the services that support them.

I support property tax relief that is targeted, sustainable, and honest about its impacts. That means focusing relief on primary residences, protecting those most at risk of being priced out of their homes, and recognizing the varying needs of different counties. The goal should be to provide meaningful relief without simply shifting costs elsewhere or undermining the local services that families and businesses depend on every day.
column9:
Economic development is not about government creating prosperity. It is about creating the conditions that allow people, businesses, and communities to succeed.

The Legislature's role is to provide stability: responsible budgeting, predictable tax policy, reliable infrastructure, quality education, workforce development, and a regulatory environment that is fair and consistent. When those fundamentals are in place, Wyoming people and Wyoming businesses do what they have always done—innovate, invest, and build.

For me, economic development is ultimately about opportunity. Can a young person graduating from high school or college find a future here? Can a family afford to stay and put down roots? Can a small business owner invest with confidence? Can a community remain vibrant as it grows?

The goal is a Wyoming where hard work pays off, where people can build a life, and where our communities remain strong for generations to come.
column10:
Campaign donations should never buy influence. My responsibility is to the people of Senate District 21, not to any donor or political organization.

I believe voters deserve transparency about who is funding campaigns and political advertising, especially when outside groups are involved. Wyoming's reporting system should be easy to access, easy to understand, and timely enough to help voters make informed decisions before they cast their ballots. I would support practical reforms that improve transparency and public confidence while protecting citizens' right to participate in the political process.

column2: I've called Laradise home since 2004, moving from Georgia where my dad was stationed for his military career. I just celebrated my 20th year of marriage to a 6th generation Wyomingite from Wheatland.
column4:
I have 20 years of community building experience in Laramie, as an economic developer, a non profit director and public historian. I am a graduate of Leadership Wyoming, co-founder of the Albany County Housing Coalition, Laramie Mural Project, and am the senior member of the House Appropriations Committee. 

I utilize civility, honesty, optimism and empathy as essential leadership skills to serve my constituents, small businesses and hard working families. I enjoy learning new things and building relationships based on shared values. I am passionate about attainable housing, opportunities for young people, public education, access to mental health services and recreating on public lands.
column5:
We need to address the outmigration of our youth if we are to build strong communities. 

Young people want good paying jobs, attainable housing, affordable healthcare, access to mental health and maternity services, vibrant downtowns and outdoor amenities. The state should invest in these things and the systems that support them. 

We should establish an "economic infrastructure" trust as a long term funding source and work with the executive branch to get everyone “rowing” in the same direction. More agencies than just the Wyoming Business Council should be addressing this challenge. The legislature should work with the Dept. of Workforce Services, Tourism, State Parks, Dept. of Family Services, Dept. of Health, etc. to strategically leverage the state's resources to retaining our young people.
column6:
Wyoming is one of the most expensive states in the nation for health care. To address this, I will continue to support Medicaid expansion, increases to reimbursement rates for maternal and rural EMS services, educational incentive training and recruitment programs. 

Last session, I voted to use our Rural Health Transformation funds to support rural hospitals, for emergency and maternal care, primary prevention services, behavioral health, workforce education, and telehealth. 

I also support expanding Family and Medical Leave and opening the state Employee Group Insurance plan to school districts, small businesses and the self-employed.
column7: Public lands belong to the public. They are NOT to be traded for corporate gain or sold to the highest bidder for private use. We need to ensure our state agencies that provide recreation (State Parks), hunting opportunities (Game & Fish), and conservation (Forest Service, WWNRT) are staffed appropriately with budgets that allow them to manage the land, collaboratively, with federal partners for the people of Wyoming.
column8:
Property tax relief should be targeted to those who need it most. I do not support blanket reductions for those able to pay, i.e. second homeowners and the ultra rich. Collected locally, property taxes provide essential community services - roads, water, emergency response, and public education that we all benefit from. 

I am opposed to further reductions in property taxes as this ensures services will decline, sales taxes will go up, and increases the likelihood of a statewide income tax.
column9:
The state should facilitate a landscape where hard working Wyomingites can innovate. I support policies that increase economic opportunities, good paying jobs and investments in community infrastructure. The legislature should facilitate, promote and invest in an entrepreneurial ecosystem that supports homegrown innovation while maximizing economies of scale. 

If we want to retain our young people, we need growth on our own terms. A commitment to making Wyoming the best version of itself. The state should support a robust economy built on our shared values, independent entrepreneurial spirit, and natural assets.
column10:
Constituents should have full access to the donations accepted by or spent on behalf of a candidate's campaign. From individual gifts to out-of-state corporations, voters have the right to know who is investing in and influencing our political process. 

More frequent reporting, random audits, and a requirement for organizations buying ads for or against candidates to reveal their funding sources, i.e. "dark money", would improve the transparency of our current system.

column2: I currently live in Rock Springs - for the past 34 years. Prior to that I lived in Green River. I am a lifelong resident of Sweetwater County, Wyoming
column4:
I worked 20 years for Church & Dwight Company (Arm & Hammer Baking Soda) in various positions. For 17 years I worked shiftwork and served as the Maintenance Supervisor when I left to join the Fire Service full time. I worked rotating shifts for 17 years and drove 80 miles round-trip to work every day. 
I have been a full-time Fire Chief for 20 years - 9 years with Sweetwater County Fire District 1 and 11 years for the Rock Springs Fire Department. I currently serve as the Chair of the Rock Springs-Sweetwater County Airport Board (13 years, 11 as Chair), Vice-Chair of the Sweetwater Combined Communications Board (16 years, 5 as Chair), and Chairman of the Wyoming State Emergency Response Committee.
I am the 1st Vice President of the Wyoming Fire Chief's Association, and the Missouri Valley Division Director to the International Association of Fire Chiefs representing the states of Colorado, Nebraska, North Dakota, South Dakota, Iowa, Kansas, Missouri, and Wyoming.
column5: Ensuring that we have our fair share of water tops the list - it is literally our life force. Access to mental health resources and suicide prevention - we have lost enough of our own. Promote economic development - let the world know that Wyoming is open for business. Ensure an effective education for every child in Wyoming - meet the student where they are regarding CTE, post-secondary education, etc. Wyoming needs affordable housing to accommodate the workforce needed to promote growth.
column6: Explore and expand options for Mobile-Integrated healthcare and Community Paramedicine. Bring healthcare to where the people are.
column7: I do not support the sale or transfer of public lands in Wyoming except in very narrow circumstances - only parcels that abut municipal corporate limits and then only for affordable housing. I would lean heavily on our US Congressional Delegation to keep our lands and access intact with multi-purpose use in the forefront.
column8: Property tax is the tax closest to the people and is the tax which most supports local government. These funds should be managed with objective restraint and discretion.
column9: We can't expect people to invest in Wyoming if we do not invest in ourselves. Responsible investment in infrastructure such as water, waste water, power transmission, and roads will attract investment from outside. At the local level, communities also need to invest in themselves to create and improve quality of life.
column10: I am not well informed enough to answer this question.

column2: I live in Evanston, where I have resided for 31 years. In total, I have lived in Wyoming for 32 years. I was born in American Fork, Utah, to parents whose deep Wyoming roots trace back to my grandparents and great-grandparents, who homesteaded in Otto, Wyoming. This multi-generational connection to our state shapes my lifelong commitment to Uinta County.
column3: I will be 64 years old on Election Day. At this stage in my life, I bring over three decades of deep roots in Uinta County alongside the wisdom, professional maturity, and real-world experience needed to lead. My age reflects a lifetime of service as a special education teacher, a former journalist, and a community advocate. I am running for the State Senate not to build a political career or chase higher office, but to protect our families, defend our property rights, and ensure Wyoming remains a free, prosperous sanctuary for the next generation of our children and grandchildren.
column4: Chris Katzl is a certified special education teacher and Republican candidate for Wyoming State Senate District 15, bringing over two decades of experience working with Uinta County families. A former journalist and editor, Katzl emphasizes community-first leadership, aiming to apply his skills in navigating complex systems to advocate for local property rights and fiscal accountability. For more details, visit the [Ballotpedia Profile for Chris Katzl](https://ballotpedia.org/Chris_Katzl)
column5: Wyoming faces fiscal volatility due to reliance on mineral revenues alongside rising cost-of-living challenges for residents. My proposed platform aims to implement disciplined budgeting, enhance property tax protections, defend constitutional rights, and support pro-life initiatives with compassion.
column6: The Legislature must expand access by cutting regulatory red tape and proactively educating the public on existing coverage avenues. Having navigated the pre-Medicare gap as a retired teacher, I know affordable care exists through options like the federal marketplace. However, the system is far too complex. Finding coverage shouldn't take years of trial and error, or depend on an insider showing you "which buttons to push."As State Senator, I will push for state-backed educational initiatives and localized resource hubs so Uinta County families can easily find and secure affordable care. Furthermore, my special education background drives my commitment to protecting our critical rural hospitals from closing, lowering prescription drug costs through market transparency, and cutting regulations to recruit more doctors to Wyoming without expanding government bureaucracy
column7: I strictly oppose transferring Wyoming’s public lands to out-of-state corporate monopolies or private equity giants like BlackRock. Our lands must be managed exclusively for verified, long-term Wyoming residents who possess deep roots in our communities and lack predatory motives.We see this dangerous trend in Uinta County with the massive Prometheus data center planned near Evanston. For years, global financial interests have plotted resource-heavy developments on our landscape. These projects threaten to drain local water supplies, strain our electrical grid, drive up utility rates, and erode our rich agricultural and ranching heritage.When globalist entities treat our open spaces as an extraction machine, local voices get ignored. As State Senator, I will stand as an iron-clad wall against these maneuvers. I will demand strict residency verification, enforce total transparency, and block out-of-state exploitation. Wyoming must stay Wyoming for Wyoming citizens.
column8: Wyoming sits on massive budget surpluses tied up in complex reserve accounts, yet local governments face funding uncertainty. We must reform how we manage this excess capital. I propose establishing a permanent sovereign fund modeled after the Alaska Permanent Fund. By capturing our mineral revenue surpluses and letting investments compound, we can achieve true fiscal stability.The interest generated from this permanent fund can directly offset local municipal shortfalls and fund essential infrastructure. This allows the Legislature to grant permanent, comprehensive property tax relief to homeowners without stripping funding from local police, rural roads, or public schools. We must stop burying surpluses in bureaucratic backrooms and start utilizing our state's wealth to protect Wyoming taxpayers and their home equity.
column9: The Wyoming Legislature should approach economic development through a free-market lens, adhering to a Milton Friedman philosophy: the government’s role is to get out of the way, eliminate red tape, and maintain minimal regulations necessary to protect citizens' rights. True growth comes from small businesses and local workers, not top-down state planning or picking winners and losers with taxpayer dollars.However, a hands-off approach does not mean corporate lawlessness. The legislature must act as a barrier when predatory out-of-state entities, global private equity giants, or resource-heavy developments threaten our local resources, utility grid, or resident home equity.Cheyenne must focus on creating a stable, predictable, and low-tax climate. Government should never run the economy, but it must vigorously defend property rights and personal liberties so Wyoming citizens can thrive.
column10: My campaign values are rooted in a lean, community-first approach. I refuse to be bought by special interests or out-of-state political action committees. My campaign relies on direct phone calls, personal conversations, and a strong digital footprint.We proved the power of a grassroots, low-cost approach when my digital push mobilized Uinta County citizens to defeat the massive, sneaky $40.6 million sales tax hike last November. We didn't need deep-pocketed donors to protect taxpayers; we just needed the truth, local organization, and a clear voice.Wyoming's monitoring system should focus on ensuring transparency, particularly regarding dark money and out-of-state special interest groups trying to buy local elections. We must make campaign finance disclosures simple and accessible. Elections should be decided by the voters of District 15, not by the size of a candidate's war chest or wealthy outside influence.
